Burroughs,  John.  SHARP,  DALLAS  LORE.  The 
seer  of  Slabsides.  1921.  C  21070 

One  of  Burroughs'  long-time  friends  is  Dallas 
Lore  Sharp  ...  he  has  told  of  Burroughs  as 
"the  seer"  and  the  writer  in  this  slender  volume 
of  appreciation.  A  bulky  volume  could  not  have 
told  more  about  the  man  and  his  character — the 
two  things  that  lovers  of  Burroughs  are  seeking. 
— Chicago  Evening  Post. 

Chambers,  Julius.  News  hunting  on  three 
continents.  1921.  C  21193 

A  record  of  great  events,  great  personalities, 
great  mysteries,  crimes  and  detective  work.  As  in- 
teresting as  any  story  of  adventure. 


Guiney,     Louise     Imogen. 
Louise    Imogen   Guiney. 


BROWN,    ALICE. 
1921.        C 22238 


A  well-written  biography  of  an  interesting  liter- 
ary personality.  A  further  attraction  to  the  buyer 
is  the  fact  that  each  copy  contains  a  signed  wood- 
cut by  Timothy  Cole. 

Hamilton,  Alexander.  VANDENBERG,  ARTHUR 
HENDRICK.  The  greatest  American,  Alex- 
ander Hamilton;  an  historical  analysis  of 
his  life  and  works  together  with  a  sym- 
posium of  opinions  by  distinguished 
Americans.  1922.  C  22283 

This  book  is  dedicated  to  the  Hamilton  Club  of 
Chicago,  which  the  author  believes  "has  done  more 
than  any  other  American  group  to  perpetuate  the 
memory  and  doctrines  of  the  greatest  American." 

Hutten,  Ulrich  von.  JORDAN,  DAVID  STARR. 
Ulrich  von  Hutten,  "knight  of  the  order 
of  poets."  1910.  C  22540 

Melville,  Herman.  WEAVER,  RAYMOND  M. 
Herman  Melville,  mariner  and  mystic. 
1921.  C  23359 

With  great  enthusiasm  for  his  subject,  Mr. 
Weaver  nas  gleaned  much  interesting  material 
about  the  man  who  discovered  valuable  literary 
material  in  the  South  Seas,  long  before  Maugham, 
O'Brien  or  even  Conrad. 

Metchnikoff,  Elie.  METCHNIKOFF,  OLGA.  Life 
of  Elie  Metchnikoff,  1845-1916.  1921. 

C  23344 

A  narrative  of  the  life  and  marvelous  accom- 
plishments of  a  great  scientist.  His  discoveries 
and  their  development  in  the  face  of  great  opposi- 
tion mark  an  epoch  in  modern  science. 

Nichols,  George.  A  Salem  shipmaster  and 
merchant;  the  autobiography  of  George 
Nichols,  with  introduction  and  notes  and 
concluding  chapters  by  his  granddaughter. 
1921.  C 23594 

An  autobiography  rich  in  incidents  of  the  au- 
thor's seafaring  experiences.  We  see  Salem  as  it 
was,  and  catch  a  glimpse  now  and  then  of  the  far 
east. 

Nietzsche,  Friedrich.  Selected  letters  of 
Friedrich  Nietzsche;  ed.,  with  a  preface, 
by  Dr.  Oscar  Levy.  1921.  C  23601 

Robertson,  Sir  William.  From  private  to 
field-marshal.  1921.  C  23968 

A  candid  autobiography  purporting  to  be  the 
first  to  describe  the  "climbing  of  a  soldier  from 
the  bottom  to  the  top  of  the  military  ladder "_  in 
the  British  army  and  including  an  intimate  view 
of  England's  part  in  the  war. 

Stinnes,  Hugo.  BRINCKMEYER,  HERMANN. 
Hugo  Stinnes.  1921.  C  24391 

The  life  and  work  of  the  "German  Rockefeller." 
The  book  gives  a  good  description  of  the  develop- 
ment of  the  "vertical"  trust  in  Germany,  as  con- 
trasted with  the  "horizontal"  development  of  the 
trust  in  America. 

Tree,  Herbert  Beerbohm.  BEERBOHM,  MAX, 
comp.  Herbert  Beerbohm  Tree;  some 
memories  of  him  and  of  his  art.  1920. 

C  24590 

A  collection  of  affectionate  tributes  by  such  lit- 
erary men  as  Shaw,  Gosse  and  Parker — friends 
and  admirers  of  the  great  actor.  That  part  of  the 
biography  which  will  endear  him  to  the  reader  is 
the  "fond,  trivial  record"  of  "Herbert  and  I"  by 
his  wife.

FICTION 

Anthony,    Joseph.      The    gang. 

r  IUDUO 

It  is  a  story  about  rivalries  of  boys  in  the  New 
York  Ghetto,  but  it  is  also  a  careful  depiction  of 
Jewish  family  life  by  a  witty  and  sympathetic 
hand. — Burton  Rascoe  in  The  Bookman. 

Arlen,  Michael,  pseud  of  Dikran  Kouyoum- 
djian.  The  romantic  lady.  1921.  F  24304 

Contents:  The  romantic  lady. — Fay  Richmond. — 
Consuelo. — The  romance  of  Iris  Poole. 

A  collection  of  very  individual  short  stories  with 
fascinating  character  sketches  of  the  modern 
woman.  The  episodes  are  humorous,  human  and 
ironical — brilliantly  written,  and  entertaining. 

Ashmun,  Margaret.  Topless  towers;  a  ro- 
mance of  Morningside  Heights.  1921. 

F 10686 

The  author  has  depicted  in  a  very  clever  way 
the  cool  aloofness  and  unfamiliarity  which  exists 
among  most  apartment-house  dwellers.  Thruout 
the  story  there  is  a  great  deal  of  talk  on  the  modern 
woman,  her  freedom  and  her  rights. — Chicago 
Evening  Post* 

Atherton,  Gertrude.  Sleeping  fires;  a  novel. 
1922.  F 10710 

A  Boston  girl  is  transferred  to  the  San  Fran- 
cisco of  the  "60s  by  a  husband  who  does  not  sym- 
pathize with  her  fondness  for  books.  She  meets 
and  loves  a  congenial  young  journalist,  and  the 
tale  becomes  sensational  when  the  husband  com- 
pels him  to  leave  town  and  both  lovers  try  to 
drink  themselves  to  death — though  the  process  is 
stopped  in  time. 


Atkey,  Bertram, 
wolves.     1922. 


Winnie  O'Wynn  and  the 
F  10701 


Mr.  O'Wynn's  dying  advice  to  his  daughter  was 
to  treat  all  men  as  wolves  and  trust  no  women. 
She  proves  to  be  fully  capable  of  protecting  herself, 
and  her  experiences  in  so  doing  are  improbable 
but  amusing. 

Berend,  Alice.  Babette  Bomberling's  bride- 
grooms. 1921.  F 11114 

A  little  farce  translated  from  the  German,  in 
which  Mamma  Bomberling  is  doing  her  best  to 
marry  off  her  flapper  daughter  "wisely." 

Bindloss,  Harold.  The  man  from  the  wilds. 
1922.  F 11131 

A  determined  young  man  is  made  guardian  of 
the  estate  of  a  beautiful  but  wilful  English  girl. 
One,  you  see,  is  determined  and  the  other  wilful. 
A  lot  has  to  happen  before  the  final  fadeout  and 
a  lot  does  happen. — Chicago  Evening  Post. 

Bunner,  H.  C.  Short  sixes;  stories  to  be 
read  while  the  candle  burns. — The  sub- 
urban sage;  stray  notes  and  comments 
on  his  simple  life.  1919.  F  20784 

Burnett,    Frances    Hodgson.      The    head   of 

the  house  of  Coombe.     1922.  F  20786 

An    entertaining   story    of    a    lonely    and    pathetic 

character.      The    child    life    of    Robin    has    features 

resembling  both   Sara  Crewe  and   Lord  Fauntleroy. 

Carpenter,  Grant.  Long  sweetening;  a  ro- 
mance of  the  red  woods.  1921.  F 11667 
A  poor  boy  is  robbed  of  his  property  in  the 
California  redwoods  by  a  banker  who  falls  in  love 
with  the  spot  and  wants  it  for  a  summer  home. 
The  boy  swears  vengeance  and  the  story  tells  of  his 
long  struggle,  his  final  victory,  and  his  love  for  the 
daughter  of  his  enemy. 


Casserly,  Gordon.    The  elephant  god.     1921. 

F 11692 

An  exciting  story  of  the  jungle  by  a  man  who 
has  spent  many  years  of  his  life  as  an  English 
officer  in  India.  It  is  unusual  in  the  fact  that, 
aside  from  the  conventional  and  wonderful  hero, 
there  is  an  elephant  who  performs  some  startling 
and  superhuman  feats. 

Cervantes,  Miguel  de.  The  adventures  of 
Don  Quixote;  adapted  by  Edwin  Gile 
Rich.  1921.  F  11737 

Chester,   Mr.   and   Mrs.    George    Randolph. 

The  son  of  Wallingford.     1921.        F  11904 

Sequel    to    Get-rich-quick    Wallingford,     F  11871; 

Young    Wallingford,    F  11858;    Wallingford    in    his 

prime,  F  11880. 

A  cheerful  account  of  the  breaking  into  busi- 
ness of  Jimmie,  son  of  Get-rich-quick  Wallingford. 
It  is  quickly  moving,  sprightly  and  up  to  date  in 
the  slang  of  the  day. 


Comfort,     Will      Levington 
world.     1921. 


This      man's 
F 21321 

The  hero,  Tom  Steepe,  has  been  a  waif  on  the 
streets  of  Chicago  but  falls  in  with  a  soldier 
deserter  who  takes  him  to  the  Philippines.  His 
adventures  are  numerous  and  blood-curdling,  but 
he  finally  wins  the  girl  and  happiness  for  life. 

Coppard,    A.    E.      Adam    &    Eve    &    pinch 
me;  tales.     1921.  F  21364 

Contents:  Marching  to  Zion. — Dusky  Ruth. — 
Weep  not  my  wanton. — Piffingcap. — The  king  of  the 
world. — Adam  &  Eve  &  pinch  me. — The  princess 
of  kingdom  gone. — Communion. — The  quiet  wo- 
man.— The  trumpeters. — The  angel  and  the  sweep. 
— Arabesque :  the  mouse. 

Of  Mr.  Coppard  the  Nation  says  "He  is  that 
rare  thing — an  almost  quite  pure,  quite  disinterested 
artist  to  whom  the  deep  forms  of  beauty  have  a 
mystical  finality  of  value." 

Duffus,     Robert     L.      Roads     going     south. 
1921.  F 21805 

A  first  novel  possessing  atmosphere  and  humor, 
and  dealing  with  the  conflict  between  the  hero's 
New  England  heritage  and  his  Southern  temper- 
ament. 

Dunn,  J.  Allan.     The  girl  of  Ghost   Moun- 
tain.    1921.  F  12616 

Clashes  between  the  cowboys  and  the  Mexicans 
make  this  a  true  western  story  of  thrills  and  ad- 
venture. The  love  element  is  not  neglected,  for 
an  eastern  girl  led  by  a  pioneer  spirit  settles  with 
her  maid  on_  Ghost  Mountain  in  the  very  midst 
of  the  turmoil. 

Evarts,  Hal   G.     The  bald  face,   and  other 
animal  stories.     1921.  F  12793 

The  author  is  a  trapper,  ranchman,  and  lover 
of  out-door  life.  The  Chicago  Daily  News  eays: 
"Evarts  has  the  gift  of  snappy  descriptions;  his 
tales  are  propelled  forward  with  speed  and  carry 
the  reader  with  them." 

The   passing    of    the    old   West.      1921. 

F 12790 

In  a  story  that  pleads  for  the  conservation  of 
natural  resources  .  .  .  Hal  Evarts,  trapper,  guide, 
lover  of  nature,  unfolds  the  great  drama  in  which 
the  beaver,  the  buffalo,  the  wild  pigeon  and  the 
elk  have  one  by  one  been  exterminated  by  sense- 
less slaughter. — Chicago  Evening  Post. 

—The  settling  of  the  sage.  1922.  F  12792 
A  good  western  story.  Old  Mr.  Warren  has 
willed  Three  Bar  Ranch  jointly  to  his  daughter 
and  Cal  Harris,  son  of  an  old  friend.  After  many 
experiences,  the  two  young  people  decide  to  make 
it  a  life  partnership. 


56 


THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Fletcher,  J.   S.     Scarhaven   Keep. 


1922. 
F 22129 

The  most  perplexing  and  involved  of  mystery 
stories,  very  well  written,  and  one  which  holds 
the  reader  at  feverish  attention  throughout. 

Gartland,    Hannah.      The    house    of    cards. 
1922.  F  13137 

A  mystery  story  suggested  by  the  Elwell  murder 
case  leaving  the  main  incidents  unchanged.  In  the 
original  case  the  killing  of  the  great  bridge-whist 
expert  was  never  explained  but  the  book  offers  a 
surprising  solution. 

Gerould,  Katharine  Fullerton.     Lost  Valley. 
1922.  F 13198 

A  beautiful  little  half-witted  girl  wanders  away 
from  Lost  Valley,  following  an  organ  grinder's 
monkey,  and  the  main  thread  of  the  story  is  the 
search  for  her  by  her  sister  Madge,  the  most  suc- 
cessfully drawn  character  in  the  book. 

Gorky,  Maxim,  pseud,  of  Pyeshkov.     Three 
of  them;   tr.  from  the  Russian.     1922. 

F  16709 

The  story  is  of  two  boys  and  a  girl  and  their 
lives,  or  rather  existences,  in  horrible  surroundings. 
A  sombre  gripping  representation  of  Russian  slum 
life. 


Graga  Aranha,  J.  P. 
Portuguese.     1920. 


Canaan;  tr.  from  the 
F  22607 


A  young  German  flees  to  Brazil  hoping  to  find 
among  the  simple  natives  in  the  new  country  real 
human  happiness  and  absence  9f  all  hypocrisy.  Dis- 
illusion comes  when  he  discovers  malice  and 
cruelty  in  the  land  of  his  dreams. 

Grimshaw,    Beatrice.      Conn    of    the    coral 
seas.     1922.  F  22625 

In  this  movie  thriller  with  a  South  Sea  Island 
setting  the  heroine  is  taken  to  a  voracious  cannibal 
village.  She  enters  the  sacred  temple  where 
"skulls,  some  new,  some  sienna  brown  with  age  were 
set  in  rows,"  but  being  accustomed  to  adven- 
tures she  survives  them  all. 

Hemon,   Louis.     Maria   Chapdelaine;  a  tale 
of  the   Lake   St.  John  country.      1921. 

F 13672 

The  idyllic  love  of  Maria  and  Francois  for  one 
another,  the  tragedy  of  his  death  and  her  life 
without  him.  It  is  an  extremely  simple  yet  power- 
ful tale  of  French  Canadian  life. 

Holland,    Rupert     Sargent.      The    panelled 
room.     1921.  F 14049 

A  good  mystery  story.  When  Maurice  Vibert 
and  the  blackmailer  quarrel  in  the  panelled  room, 
the  blackmailer  is  killed  by  shots  from  an  un- 
known source. 

Hulit,    Leonard.     Fishing   with   a   boy;    the 
tale  of  a  rejuvenation.     1921.  F  14240 

A  delightful  story  of  young  Matt  Buckley  whose 
great  knowledge  of  fish  and  fishing  fills  the  book 
with  real  fishing  lore  and  the  life  of  the  great 
out-of-doors.  Matt  and  his  Aunt  Mary  are  real 
country  characters. 

Jenkins,  Burris.  Princess  Salome;  a  tale  of 
the  days  of  camel-bells.  1921.  F  14339 
A  novel  of  the  time  of  Christ.  Stephen  and 
Paul  journey  to  Jerusalem  to  await  the  coming  of 
the  Messiah.  On  the  way  Stephen  meets  the  notor- 
ious Salome  and  falls  madly  in  love  with  her 
but  they  are  separated.  Salome  thereafter  be- 
comes a  "soulless  dancer"  and  Stephen  dies  a 
martyr  to  the  faith. 

Jesse,  F.  Tennyson.     The  white  riband;  or, 
A  young  female's  folly.     1921.         F  24084 


A  tragic  story  of  a  little  country  girl  who  though 
poor  and  untaught  has  an  exquisitely  fine  nature 
and  through  her  great  passion  for  beauty  stakes 
her  happiness  on  gaining  the  possession  of  a  white 
ribbon.  Anita  Kepler  in  the  Daily  News  writes, 
"As  evanescent  as  a  bit  of  thistledown,  as  fleeting 
and  as  lovely,  is  this  brief  tale." 

Keable,  Robert.     Simon  called  Peter.     1921. 

F 14456 

A  war  story  whose  chief  character  is  a  ^elf- 
righteous  English  clergyman.  His  gospel  of  life 
was  turned  to  chaos  by  his  experiences  in  France. 
It  is  unpleasant  reading  but  strong  and  realistic. 

Kipling,  Rudyard.  Selected  stories  from 
Kipling,  ed.  by  William  Lyon  Phelps. 
1921.  F  24262 

Contents:  The  man  who  would  be  king. — The 
drums  of  the  fore  and  aft. — The  phantom  rick- 
shaw.—Wee  Willie  Winkie. — The  courting  of  Di- 
nah Shadd. — The  man  who  was. — Without  benefit 
of  clergy. — The  incarnation  of  Krishna  Mulvaney. 
— "Rikki-tikki-tavi." — The  brushwood  boy. — Wil- 
liam the  'conqueror. — "They." — An  habitation  en- 
forced. 

A  new  collection  of  some  of  Kipling's  best  short 
stories,  with  an  introduction  by  William  Lyon 
Fhelps. 

Knibbs,  Henry  Herbert.  Partners  of  chance. 
1921.  F  24291 

A  western  tale  which  gives  a  picturesque  idea 
of  life  in  the  Arizona  country. 

London,  Jack.  The  call  of  the  wild. — The 
scarlet  plague.  1920.  F  24631 

Marroquin,  Lorenzo.  Pax  (Peace);  tr.  by 
Isaac  Goldberg,  and  W.  V.  Schierbrand. 
1920.  F  24964 

(Brentano's     Hispano-American    series.) 
Full    of    action,    love    interest,    revolutions    and 
"big  business." 

Maxwell,  W.    B.     A   little  more.     1922. 

F  25025 

A  charming,  cleverly-handled  tale  based  on  an 
old  moral.  The  discontented  Welby  family  would 
be  happy  with  just  a  little  more.  When  it  suddenly 
.  comes,  bringing  financial  indigestion,  the  result  is 
not  joyous,  but  the  war  solves  their  problems  leav- 
ing them  happy  at  last. 

Norris,  Charles  G.  Salt;  or,  The  education 
of  Griffith  Adams.  1918.  F  25607 

The  younger  brother  of  Frank  Norris  here  tells 
the  story  of  an  ordinary  American  who  is  pre- 
vented from  a  normal  character  development  by 
the  inadequacies  of  our  educational  system. 

Norton,  Brayton.  El  Diablo.  1921.  F 15704 
El  Diablo  is  an  island  off  southern  California 
where  a  gang  of  smugglers  are  working  to  ruin 
Gregory's  fish  canning  business.  Things  start  mov- 
ing when  they  meet  the  combined  resistance  of 
Gregory's  son,  a  veritable  live  wire,  and  the  in- 
trepid Dickey  who  takes  charge  of  her  father's  fish- 
ing fleet. 


Oppenheim,  E.  Phillips, 
quis.     1919. 


The  wicked  mar- 
F 25761 


The  story  of  a  character  more  entertaining  than 
convincing  told  in  a  more  leisurely  tempo  than 
many  Oppenheim  novels. 

Punshon,  E.   R.     Old   fighting  days.     1921. 

F  16690 

In  Napoleonic  days  a  little  English  waif,  the 
protege  of  a  former  prize  fighter,  becomes  him- 
self a  famous  pugilist.  His  fighting  victory  over 
the  villain  leads  to  the  discovery  of  his  noble 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


57 


parentage,   he  saves  Britain   from  a   conspiracy  and 
wins  a  patrician  bride. 

Quick,  Herbert.     Vandemark's  folly.     1922. 

F 16759 

A  truly  American  tale  of  mid-western  pioneer 
days,  refreshing,  wholesome  and  absorbing. 

Rohmer,  Sax,  pseud,  of  Arthur  Sarsfield 
Ward.  Fire-Tongue.  1922.  F 18993 

Heroic  hero  rescues  beautiful  heroine  from  vil- 
lainous villain.  Hero  slays  villain,  after  which 
hero  and  heroine  live  happily  ever  after. 

Sackville-West,  V.,  (Mrs.  Victoria  Nicol- 
son).  The  dragon  in  shallow  waters. 
1922.  F 15681 

An  unpleasant  story  but  a  strong  one  of  two 
brothers,  one  of  whom  is  blind,  the  other  _deaf 
and  dumb,  and  their  reaction  to  their  environ- 
ment. The  theme  is  an  aged  Chinese  proverb: 
"The  dragon  in  shallow  waters  became  the  butt 
of  shrimps." 

Savi,    E.   W.     The   devil    drives.      1922. 

F  17670 

The      love      affair      of      a      young  soldier      in 

France  is  the  main  theme  of  this   tale.  There  are 

a    few   minor    romances,    one   of   which  takes    place 
in   an    Indian    mission. 

Smith,   Constance  I.     Ten  hours.     1921. 

F 18214 

Often  thrilling  and  again  and  again  convincing  is 
this  story  of  one  extraordinary  day  in  the  life  of 
a  middle  class  English  woman.  There  is  one  stun- 
ning dramatic  situation. 

Tobenkin,  Elias.  The  road.  1922.  F  27801 
The  "road"  is  the  life  journey  of  Hilda,  the 
daughter  of  Danish  immigrants.  It  takes  her  from 
a  small  Wisconsin  town  to  Russia  through  stren- 
uous experiences  as  a  waitress,  a  factory  worker, 
a  convert  to  socialism,  a  participant  in  safety 
campaigns  in  New  York  and  strikers'  relief  in 
Arizona. 

Tremaine,  Herbert.     The  tribal  god.     1922. 

F  27871 

This  is  a  somewhat  unpleasant  but  well-written 
story  of  a  family  whose  tribal  god  is  money, 
worshipped  in  the  guise  of  filial  affection. 

Turgenev,  Ivan.  The  two  friends,  and  other 
stories.  1921.  F  27913 

Contents:  The  two  friends. — Father  Alexey's 
story. — Three  meetings.— A  quiet  backwater.

1921. 


Hugh.      Jade,     and    other

F  19486 


C  ontents:  Jade. — Joss. — Hop. — Junk. — Tong. —

Short  stories  of  San  Francisco's  Chinatown. 
"Junk"  is  perhaps  the  most  artistic  but  all  are 
colorful  and  exotic. 

—Lady  Luck.     1921.  F 19485 

Sequel   to    The    Wildcat,    F  19484. 

A  series  of  humorous  edisodes  in  which  Wild- 
cat and  his  mascot  goat  "Lily"  continue  their  ad- 
ventures. As  a  Pullman  porter  adept  in  the  art 
of  "rolling  bones"  Wildcat  is  truly  mirth-pro- 
voking.

Herford    has    given    the    old    fables    a    new    tang 

without     detracting    from     their     simplicity. — Book- 


Bealby,  J.  T.,  and  Fairford,  Ford.  Canada, 
by  J.  T.  Bealby;  and  Newfoundland,  by 
Ford  Fairford.  1921.  H  5464 

(Peeps    at    many    lands.) 

Benton,  Rita.  The  star-child  and  other 
plays.  1921.  E 13502 

Contents:  The  star-child. — Where  love  is,  there 
God  is  also. — The  happy  man  (an  old  Greek  le- 
gend).— A  spark  neglected  burns  the  house. — King 
Robert  of  Sicily. — Yussouf. — The  life  beyond. — 
The  ring  of  magic  power. 

Plays  adapted  from  well  known  stories  and 
poems  and  intended  for  boys  and  girls  from  eight 
to  fourteen.  Simple  and  dramatic  with  accom- 
panying photographs. 

Finnemore,  John,  and  Browne,  Edith  A. 
Italy,  by  John  Finnemore;  and  Greece,  by 
Edith  A.  Browne.  1921.  H  5477 

(Peeps   at   many   lands.) 

Hope,  Winifred  Ayres.  Friends  in  book- 
land.  1921.  E  13827 

A  play  that  will  interest  both  boys  and  girls  and 

five  a  chance  for  a  number  of  actors.    Ruth  has  to 
e  quarantined  but  is  consoled  because  in  a  dream 
many    friends    from    Bookland    visit    her    and    tell 
enough   about    themselves    so   that  she   will   want   to 
know    them    better. — Booklist. 

Johnson,  Gaylord.  The  star  people;  with 
drawings  on  sand  and  blackboard,  by 
"Uncle  Henry  and  the  society  of  star- 
gazers."  1921.  H4516 

The  story  of  the  constellations  told  in  a  fascinat- 
ing, fictional  form.  For  children  in  the  grades. 

Johnston,  Lena  E.,  and  Finnemore,  John. 
China,  by  Lena  E.  Johnston;  and  Japan, 
by  John  Finnemore.  1921.  H  5465 

(Peeps   at    many   lands.) 

Mockler-Ferryman,  A.  F.,  and  Thomson,  M. 

Pearson.     Norway,   by    Lieut.-Col.    A.    F. 

Mockler-Ferryman;   and  Denmark,  by  M. 

Pearson  Thomson.     1921.  H  5483 

(Peeps    at    many    lands.) 

Moses,  Montrose  J.,  ed.  A  treasury  of  plays 
for  children.  1921.  E  14067 

Contents:  The  little  princess,  by  Frances  H.  Bur- 
nett.— The  silver  thread,  by  Constance  D.  Mackay. 
— The  testing  of  Sir  Gawayne,  by  Marguerite  Mer- 
ington. — Pinkie  and  the  fairies,  by  W.  G.  Rob- 
ertson.— Punch  and  Judy. — The  three  wishes,  by 
H.  Williamson  and  T.  Sarg. — The  toymaker  of 
Nuremberg,  by  A.  Strong. — Six  who  pass  while  the 
lentils  boil,  by  S.  Walker. — Master  Skylark  (J. 
Bennett)  by  Anna  M.  Lutkenhaus. — Alice  in  Won- 
derland (L.  Carroll)  by  Alice  Gerstenberg. — The 
travelling  man,  by  Lady  Gregory. — The  months:  a 
pageant,  by  Christina  G.  Rossetti. — The  forest  ring, 
by  W.  C.  De  Mille  and  C.  Barnard.— The  seven  old 
ladies  of  Lavender  Town,  by  H.  C.  Bunner. 

Here  in  one  comfortably  portly  volume  are  four- 
teen plays  that  not  only  have  incident  and  action 
sufficient  to  render  them  delightful  to  young  peo- 
ple but  literary  merit  of  an  order  high  enough  to 
entitle  them  to  a  permanent  place  in  their  affections. 
— Literary  Review. 

Patch,  Edith  M.    Bird  stories.    1921.   H  5659 

(Little    gateways    to    science.) 

Twelve  stories  that  show  a  happy  combination  of 
accurate  information  with  the  ability  to  state  things 
so  interestingly  that  any  child  will  read.  Not  only 
the  birds  but  their  setting  in  the  country  life  are 
delightfully  revealed  in  the  stories  and  the  pic- 
tures.— Booklist.

THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Stories 

Bassett,  Sara  Ware.  Steve  and  the  steam 
engine.  1921.  H  7322 

(The  invention   series.) 

Steve  enjoys  some  adventures  and  learns  all 
about  the  inventing  and  perfecting  of  the  steam 
engine  from  his  father  who  can  tell  the  most  in- 
teresting stories  of  railroading  in  the  early  days. 

Colum,  Padraic.  The  golden  fleece  and  the 
heroes  who  lived  before  Achilles;  illustra- 
tions by  Willy  Pogany.  1921.  H  7713 

Mr.  Colum  has  woven  together  all  the  old  Greek 
tales  into  one  beautiful  story  without  in  the  least 
destroying  the  clarity  and  simplicity  of  the  original 
myths.  Llewellyn  Jones  of  the  Chicago  Evening 
Post  considers  the  book  "a  work  that  deserves 
to  become  a  classic." 

Douglas,  Barbara,  tr.  Favourite  French 
fairy  tales,  retold  from  the  French  of 
Perrault,  Madame  d'Aulnoy  and  Madame 
Leprince  de  Beaumont.  1921.  H  7907 

Contents:  Cinderella,  Little  Red  Riding-Hood, 
The  sleeping  beauty,  Diamonds  and  toads,  Puss  in 
boots. — Little  Thum'bling,  Riauet  with  the  tuft.  The 
white  cat  by  C.  Perrault. — Beauty  and  the  beast, 
Prince  Darling,  by  Mme.  Leprince  de  Beaumont. — 
Goldenlocks,  by  Mme.  d'Aulnoy. 

A  retelling  of  the  pleasing  old  tales  from  the 
French.  Charming  illustrations  in  color  by  R. 
Cramer. 

Duganne,  Phyllis.     Ruthie.     1921.         H  7940 

Ruthie  is  a  girl  of  thirteen  who  returns  to  Amer- 
ica after  a  long  residence  in  China  and  other 
far-away  countries.  Her  life  at  home  and  at  school 
and  the  funny  mistakes  she  makes  are  told  with  a 
charming  grace  that  will  delight  young  girls. 

Gerry,  Margarita  Spalding.  Philippa's  for- 
tune. 1921.  H  8218 

Philippa  is  a  girl  _  of  _  thirteen  who  has  her  for- 
tune told  at  the  beginning  of  the  story.  The  rest 
of  the  book  tells  of  the  fulfillment.  For  girls  from 
ten  to  fourteen. 

Gregor,  Elmer  Russell.    The  war  trail.     1921. 

H8301 

There  is  epic  spirit  to  such  a  narrative,  and 
many  a  boy  reader  will  have  singing  in  his  ears 
the  war-cry_  of  the  brave.  Indian  guile  pitted 
against  Indian  guile  is  bound  to  produce  excite- 
ment.— Literary  Digest.

tures and  decorations  by  N.  C.  Wyeth. 
1921.  H  4432

fairy  book.  1921.  H  5003 

These  seventy-thiee  stories  probably  represent 
the  most  comprehensive  and  varied  collection  of 
oriental  fairy  tales  ever  made  available  for  Am- 
erican readers.

Noteworthy  Annual  Reports 

Illinois.  Department  of  Mines  and  Minerals. 
Annual  coal  report,  1921. 

Completes  the  fortieth  volume  of  the  history 
of  coal  production  in  Illinois,  showing  its  growth 
and  development,  the  increase  in  output  and 
in  number  of  men  employed,  together  with  the 
usual  yearly  statistics. 

United  States.  Department  of  Commerce. 
9th  annual  report,  1921.  Cl.l/a  1921 

This  first  report  of  Herbert  Hoover  as  Secre- 
tary of  Commerce  outlines  briefly  the  steps  to- 
ward efficient  reorganization  in  the  interests  of 
economy  and  increased  trade  and  tells  a  story 
worth  the  reading. 

Department  of  Labor.  9th  annual  re- 
port, 1921.  Ll.l/a  1921 

A  brief  and  interesting  story  of  the  work  of  the 
Department  and  its  bureaus,  by  the  Secretary  of 
Labor.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate.    Those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  Room;  other 

collections  are  shelved  in  the  Civics  Department,    Music    Room   and   Thomas 

Hughes  Room  and  are  marked  accordingly.    *Ser.    denotes   serials 

which  do  not  circulate.

Psychology 

Eastman,  Max.     The  sense  of  humor.    1921. 

L 13967 

A  psychological  discourse  on  humor  w'hich  at- 
tempts to  explain  this  mysterious  "sense"  by 
analysis  of  various  experiences  which  cause  laugh- 
ter. Although  the  primary  purpose  of  the  book 
is  scientific  it  is  likewise  interesting  for  its  literary 
quality. 

Porter,   Mary   F.     Applied   psychology   for 
nurses.     1921.  L 13976

Jones,   Ernest.     Papers   on   psycho-analysis.

Contents:      General    papers. — Papers    on    dreams.

Kolnai,  Aurel.  Psychoanalysis  and  soci- 
ology. 1922.  L 15315 

Contents^:  Sociological  results  of  psychoanalysis. 
— Sociological  tasks  of  psychoanalysis. 

Discusses  the  "intimate  mutual  determinism  be- 
tween the  individual  and  society,"  the  expansion 
which  the  original  idea  of  psychoanalysis  has  under- 
gone, the  service  it  may  render  to  social  aims,  and 
its  bearing  upon  the  history  of  civilization. 

O'Shea,  M.  V.  Mental  development  and  edu- 
cation. 1921.  L 13707 

Attempts  to  show  from  the  standpoint  of  present- 
day  biological  psychology  how  the  normal  individual 
reacts  at  different  periods  in  his  development  to 
typical  situations,  physical,  intellectual,  esthetic,  and 
social. — Booklist. 

Mental  Hygiene,  New  Thought 


Hoyt,  Ethel  Phelps  Stokes. 
S.  H.     1921. 


Spirit,  by  E.  P. 
L  13246 


A^ittle  book  with  a  religious  turn,  on  the  value 
of  right  thinking,  faith  and  prayer  for  an  efficient 
life.  Its  diagrams  show  "vicious  circles"  engen- 
dered by  negative  thoughts,  and  its  quotations  from 
the  Bible  offer  antidotes. — Booklist.

Babson,   Roger  W. 
1921. 


Enduring  investments. 
L 14371 


Beginning  with  an  economic  discussion  of  the 
sources,  uses,  abuses  and  dangers  of  wealth  and 
of  the  different  forms  of  stock  and  bond  invest- 
ments, Mr.  Babson  makes  his  appeal  for  more 
enduring  investments  in  the  shape  of  human  souls 
and  happiness. — Book  Review  Digest. 

Sinclair,    Upton.      The    book    of   life;    mind 
and   body.      1921.  L 14142 

A  new  Upton  Sinclair  pleads  with  some  of  his 
old  time  intolerance  for  tolerance  in  others,  realiz- 
ing that  "life  is  a  series  of  compromises  among 
many  different  needs,  many  different  desires,  many 
different  duties."

France;  a  study  in  the  history  of  social

A  work  which  unites  the  most  pains-taking 
scholarship  with  an  easy  and  lucid  style  and  an 
imaginative  grasp  of  the  spirit  of  French  Catholi- 
cism. Within  its  chosen  field  it  is  so  complete, 
accurate  and  sympathetic  as  to  leave  nothing  to  be 
desired. — Independent  and  Weekly  Review.

Statistics 

Anglo-South  American  handbook  for  1921, 
incorporating  Mexico  and  Central  Amer- 
ica; ed.  by  W.  H.  Koebel.  1921.  L  15601

Concise,-  conveniently  arranged  information  on

Economics,    Industry 

Keynes,  John  Maynard.  A  revision  of  the 

Treaty;  being  a  sequel  to  The  economic 

.  consequences  of  the  peace  (L 16626). 

1922.  L 16718 

It  is  a  pleasure  to  read,  lucid  and  concise  in  its 
economic  arguments,  dashing  in  its  political  satires 
and  its  judgment  of  persons.  .  .Contains  matter 
that  is  of  real  importance. — New  Statesman. 

Parker,  Cornelia  Stratton.  Working  with 
the  working  woman.  1922.  L  16913 

Clad  in  old  clothes  of  brilliant  hues,  and  well 
supplied  with  chewing  gum,  Mrs.  Parker  finds 
work  in  a  candy  factory,  brass  works,  laundry, 
dressmaking  shop  and  a  large  hotel.  She  has  no 
ax  to  grind,  but  hopes  her  experiences  (which 
are  graphically  told)  will  help  labor,  capital,  and 
the  consumer  to  a  better  understanding. 

Roe,  Frederick  William.  The  social  philos- 
ophy of  Carlyle  and  Ruskin.  1921. 

L 16717 

Mr.  Roe,  associate  professor  of  English  in  the 
University  of  _  Wisconsin,  here  reveals  himself  as 
a  student  of  literature  with  a  social  consciousness. 
To  him  the  pages  of  Carlyle  and  Ruskin  have 
meant  more  than  a  literary  style  for  study  and 
analysis. — Daily  News. 

Socialism 

Eucken,  Rudolf.  Socialism;  an  analysis. 
1922.  L  17574 

It  is  with  socialism  as  a  philosophy  of  life  that 
the  author  takes  issue.  Too  much  emphasis,  he 
thinks,  is  placed  on  the  material  prosperity  of 
man  and  not  enough  hope  is  held  out  of  a  spiritual 
content  without  which  there  can  be  no  inner  life 
and  therefore  no  real  happiness.

L  17505 

(Americanization    studies.)

L  16108

to  the  lot  of  the  author  personally  while  he  was 
in  the  service. — Boston  Transcript.

Burlingame,  Anne  Elizabeth.  The  battle 
of  the  books  in  its  historical  setting. 
1920.  J  7642 

Contents.  Introduction,  by  J.  H.  Robinson. — 
Introductory  chapter:  The  attitude  toward  the 
classical  authors  during  the  mediaeval  period;  Ital- 
ian humanism;  Humanism  beyond  the  Alps;  Mon- 
taigne.— The  scientific  phase:  Francis  Bacon;  Gali- 
leo; Descartes;  Thomas  Hobbes. — The  literary 
phase:  Contrast  between  the  thought  environment 
of  the  seventeenth  century  and  our  own;  Sir  Wil- 
liam Temple's  Essay  upon  ancient  and  modern 
learning;  William  Wotton's  Reflections ;  Richard 
Bentley's  Dissertation;  Jonathan  Swift's  Tale  of  a 
tub  and  The  battle  of  the  books;  The  French  point 
of  view  as  compared  with  the  English;  Charles 
Perrault ;  Fontenelle. — Conclusion. 

An  inquiry  into  the  battle  between  the  upholders 
of  the  classics  as  authorities  and  the  modernists  in 
the  seventeenth  and  eighteenth  centuries. 

Hergesheimer,  Joseph.  CABELL,  JAMES 
BRANCH.  Joseph  Hergesheimer,  an  essay 
in  interpretation.  1921.  J  7237 

This  diagnosis  of  Mr.  Cabell's  dearest  rival  is 
as  generous  as  it  is  favorable.  The  critic  is  jealous 
only  at  the  thought  that  Mr.  Hergesheimer  may 
be  philandering  with  that  dangerous  baggage  Pop- 
ularity thus  endangering  his  true  vocation,  "to 
write  perfectly  of  beautiful  happenings." — Nation.

Marquis,  Don.     Poems  and  portraits.    1922. 

E  14853 

No  such  vital  volume  of  verse  has  been  published 
for  a  long  time.  There  is  not  a  dead  spot  in  it. 
Every  poem  is  animated  by  striking  thought  and 
deep  feeling,  expressed  in  terms  of  beauty,  strength 
and  wit. — Richard  Le  Gallienne  in  New  York  Times.

Three  volumes  by  a  woman  whose  house  on  the

Benchley,  Robert   C.     Of  all   things.      1921. 

E1912 

Mr.  Benchley  finds  himself  staggered  by  such 
grave  problems  of  conduct  as  when  to  tell  the 
elevator  man  that  you  want  to  get  off  at  the  six- 
teenth floor  or  how  to  attract  attention  at  a  crowded 
soda  fountain. — Daily  News. 

Chekhov,     Anton.       Note-book 
Chekhov.     1921. 

These  fragmentary  jottings  reveal  the  working 
methods  of  a  great  artist.  The  qualities  which  dis- 
tinguish his  short  stories  are  evident,  the  keen 
scrutiny  of  life,  the  sardonic  humor,  the  graphic 
touch. 

Day,  Clarence,  jr.     The  crow's   nest.     1921. 

E2390 

Sparkling,  original,  at  times  gay,  at  times  seri- 
ous, these  observations  about  men  and  human  na- 
tiire;  cows,  legs,  Fabre,  Noah,  marriage,  authors, 
Shaw,  Prometheus,  Humpty-Dumpty,  and  a  host  of 
others,  are  made  in  an  unusually  natural,  informal 
style.

Sir  Sidney's  point  of  view  is  delightfully  affected 
by  his  interest  in  literature  and  art.  His  chapter 
on  Gladstone  tells  of  the  statesman's  fondness  f9r 
Greek  literature,  not  of  political  problems.  Ruskin 
was  an  intimate  friend  of  his  family  and  Stevenson, 
whose  letters  he  edited,  was  a  close  personal  friend. 

Lansing,  Robert.  The  big  four  and  others 
of  the  Peace  conference.  1921.  C  19707 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


73 


Contents:  Portraits  of  the  big  four. — Impres- 
sions of  others:  Venizelos;  Emir  Feisul  ;  General 
Botha;  Paderewski. 

These  sketches  of  the  four  leading  figures  of  the 
Peace  Conference  undertake  to  analyze  their  per- 
sonalities and  compare  their  characteristics  as  they 
appeared  to  the  writer,  former  Secretary  of  State 
and  himself  a  member  of  the  so-called  Council  of 
Ten. — Book  Review  Digest. 

Sidebotham,  Herbert.  Political  profiles  from 
British  public  life.  1921.  C 19967 

Contents:  The  press  gallery. — Lord  Grey. — The 
Cecilians. — Sir  Robert  Home. — Lord  Reading. — 
Mr.  Lloyd  George. — Lord  Curzon. — Mr.  Asquith. — 
Mr.  Bonar  Law. — Mr.  Winston  Churchill. — Mr. 
Chamberlain. — Sir  Gordon  Hewart. — Lady  Astor. — 
Mr.  J.  H.  Thomas. — Lord  Birkenhead. — Lord 
Derby. — Mr.  Brace  and  others. — Lord  Carson. — 
Captain  Elliot. — Postscript:  The  future  of  parlia- 
mentary government. 

The  meat  of  the  book  lies  in  the  impression  one 
gets  of  the  sort  of  Englishman  who  goes  in  for 
politics — or  statesmanship,  if  you  please.  Mr.  Side- 
botham  gives  rather  vivid  pictures  of  the  individ- 
uals, analyzes  their  characters  with  refreshing  and 
seemingly  courageous  frankness. — Chicago  Evening 
Post.

in  history.  1918.  C  20050

clos. — Marie  de  Brinvilliers. — Anna  Brudenell. —

Elssler. — Lola  Montez. — Rachel.

_  A  book  that  instructs  the  reader  in  the  most  fas-

tertainingly is  it  written.  Not  a  study  but  rather

Lincoln,  Abraham.  CONWELL,  RUSSELL  H. 
Why  Lincoln  laughed.  1922.  C  23015 


This  book  contrives  to  make  the  great  figure  of 
our  national  epic  even  a  little  more  human  and 
homely  than  it  was  before,  closer,  that  is,  to  the 
understanding  of  men,  and  is  therefore  a  welcome 
addition  to  Lincoln  literature. — Literary  Review.

Soskice,  Juliet  M.  Chapters  from  child- 
hood; reminiscences  of  an  artist's  grand- 
daughter. 1922.  C  24310 
Not  a  diary,  but  a  record  of  impressions  charm- 
ingly presented  from  the  child  point  of  view  by  the 
granddaughter  of  Ford  Madox  Brown.  It  includes 
an  account  of  one  winter  when  Juliet  (aged  8)  and 
her  Rossetti  cousins  published  and  sold  a  socialist 
paper;  of  her  life  in  her  grandfather's  home,  and 
in  English  and  German  convents. 

Swift,  Jonathan.  Vanessa  and  her  corres- 
pondence with  Jonathan  Swift;  ed.  with 
an  introduction  by  A.  Martin  Freeman. 
1921.  C 24454 

They  have  finally  come  to  light  after  a  long 
disappearance  and  are  now  published  for  the  first 
time.  The  letters  are  dated  from  November  18, 
1711  to  August  7,  1722.

(American    historical    series.) 

Packed  with  concrete  facts  drawn  from  a  great

brilliant,  the  work  should  be  as  popular  as  a  thor- 
oughly scholarly  and  judicious  book  on  the  Re-

Mr.  Vopicka  was  United  States  minister  in

entire  wnr,  and  for  part  of  that  time  protected  the 
interests  of  as  many  as  eight  nations  at  once.

Beer,  Thomas.     The  fair  rewards.     1922. 

F  20338 

A  theater  manager  and  owner  is  divorced  from  his 
wife,  a  variety  actress,  nvich  older  than  himself. 
He  adopts  an  orphaned  niece  and  nephew.  The 
boy  turns  out  well,  the  girl  badly,  and  to  protect 
her  name  against  a  threat  of  slander,  he  ooens  his 
own  theater  with  an  inferior  play  and  inferior  ac- 
tors, which  is  the  "fair  reward  of  his  great  love."

F  20383 

Mr.  Prohack  unexpectedly  inherits  100.000 
pounds  and  with  the  help  of  his  affectionate  family 
learns  how  to  make  an  occupation  of  idleness. 
Written  with  the  deliciously  subtle  humor  of  Denry, 
the  audacious  (F  11037).

F 11198 

Every  outside  and  inside  force  that  contributes

Blackwood,  Algernon,  and  Wilson,  Wilfred. 
The  wolves  of  God,  and  other  fey  stories. 
1921.  F 20525 

Contents:  The  wolves  of  God. — Chinese  magic. 
—  -Running  Wolf. — First  hate. — The  tarn  of  sacri- 
fice.— The  valley  of  the  beasts. — The  call. — Egyp-

Stories  in  which  the  natural  frequently  merges

tre  naranjos).  1921.  F  11239 

I  Rafael,  a  political  leader  of  Alcira  who  is  at

with  a  beautiful,  brilliant,  sophisticated  prima  donna. 
Sunny  Spain  is  the  significant^  and  colorful  back- 
ground for  many  tense  torrential  moments. 

Bruun,  Laurids.  Van  Zanten's  happy  days; 
a  love  story  from  Pelli  Island.  1922. 

F  20754 

The  paper  jacket  of  the  London  edition  calls  it, 
"a  charming  love  story  of  the  South  Sea  Islands," 
but  it  is  more  than  that,  for  it  is  highly  successful 
in  picturing  the  fascination  of  native  life  before 
missionaries,  traders,  and  writers  made  all  the 
native  races  of  faraway  lands  self-conscious. — Lit- 
erary Review. 

Byng,    Lucy,    tr.    Roumanian    stories.      1921. 

F  20822 

Contents:  The  fairy  of  the  lake,  by  Sadoveanu. 
— The  Easter  torch;  At  Manjoala's  inn,  by  Cara- 
gUIe. — Alexandru  Lapushneanu,  1564-1569,  by  Neg- 
ruzzi.— Zidra;  Gardana;  The  dead  pool,  by  Beza. — 
Old  Nichifor,  the  impostor,  by  Creanga. — Cozma 
Racoare;  The  wanderers,  by  Sadoveanu. — The  fledge- 
ling, by  Bratescu-Voineshti. — Popa  Tanda,  by  Slav- 
ici. — Out  in  the  world,  by  Popovici-Banatzeanu.— 
The  bird  of  ill  omen,  by  Bratescu-Voineshti. — Iri- 
nel,  by  Delavrancea. 

These  short  stories  by  some  of  the  best  writers 
of  Rumania  deal  mainly  with  peasant  life.  Some  are 
sketches  of  moods,  touched  lightly  and  with  deli- 
cate tenderness.  The  Boston  Transcript  thinks  that 
"the  work  of  Beza  possesses  a  mystical  touch,  a 
thrill  of  fatalism  and  horror  combined,  which  few 
writers  can  achieve." 

Cabell,  James  Branch.  The  cords  of  vanity; 
a  comedy  of  shirking.  1920.  F 11533 


_  The  story  of  the  gradual  degeneration  of  a  Vir- 
ginian whose  rule  of  life  is  never  to  do  anything 
disagreeable.  The  book  is  consistent  throughout 
and  the  _ style  exquisite.  The  theme  is  not  pleas- 
ant but  it  is  adequately  treated. 

Cohen,   Octavus   Roy.      Midnight.      1922. 

F21306 

In  spite  of  the  complete  absence  of  style  and 
character  portrayal,  Mr.  Cohen  has  satisfied  his 
public — he  has  written  a  book  with  a  mystery  that 
is  unsolvable  until  the  last  chapter. — New  York 
Times. 


Cooper,  Courtney  Ryley. 
1922. 


The  white  desert. 
F 21350 


A  picturesque  tale  of  the  continental  divide,  and 
the  desert  of  snow.  Mr.  Cooper  knows  this  sin- 
ister country  very  well  and  writes  of  it  with  con- 
siderable power. — Chicago  Evening  Post. 

Cooper,    Henry    St.    John.      The    garden    of 
memories.     1921.  F  21353 

A  modern  love  story  with  background  and  setting 
of  an  old  English  garden  which  is  haunted  by  mem- 
ories and  loves  of  long  ago. 


De  la  Mare,  Walter. 
1922. 


Memoirs  of  a  midget. 
F  21531 


The  memoirs  of  a  tiny  little  woman,  the  _  record, 
especially,  of  one  year  of  her  life  when,  with  'pro- 
tecting father  and  mother  both  dead,  she  faces 
regular  sized  life  alone,  and  finds  it — what  every 
spiritual  changeling  knows  it  is.  There  is  a  mys- 
tery about  the  Midget,  a  mental  mysticism  which 
leaves  one  with  a  sense  of  having  looked  into  the 
heart  of  a  fairy. — Chicago  Tribune. 

De  la  Roche,  Mazo.  Explorers  of  the  dawn; 
with  a  foreword  by  Christopher  Morley. 
1922.  F  21 541 

A  delightful  story  about  three  healthy,  active 
boys  left  in  the  cure  of  a  governess  who  was  "the 
spirit  incarnate  of  law  and  order."  The  humor- 
ous incidents  are  told  by  8-year-old  John — Angel 
is  9  and  Seraph  5. — In  the  spirit  of  the  book  lies 
its  charm  for  both  young  and  old. 

Ewart,  Wilfrid.    Way  of  revelation;  a  novel 

of  five  years.     1921.  F  12796 

An    English    novel    in   which    the   interest   centers 

around    four    young   people    and    the    effect    of    the 

war    on   their   lives. 


Fitzgerald,    F.    Scott, 
damned.      1922. 


The    beautiful    and 
F  22105 


This  original  and  curious  book  depicts  the 
married  life  of  two  charming  idlers,  Anthony  Patch 
and  beautiful  Gloria  Gilbert.  They  indulge  in  their 
passion  for  "living  the  moment,"  and  alcohol  be- 
comes the  dominant  note.  Through  the  breaking 
of  a  will  they  secure  thirty  millions  and  leave  for 
Europe. 

Fleuron,  Svend.  Kittens:  a  family  chron- 
icle; foreword  by  Carl  Van  Vechten. 
1922.  F  22135 

An  enterprising  and  resourceful  cat  mother  with- 
draws her  kittens  to  a  remote  field  and  brings  them 
up  without  human  aid  or  molestation.  Each  of 
the  six  kittens  has  a  distinct  personality,  and  when 
Grey  Puss  sees  each  one  filling  a  place  in  society 
she  returns  to  the  old  home. 

Gibbs,  Philip.  Beauty  and  Nick;  a  novel 
of  the  stage  and  the  home.  1921. 

F  22412 

A  story  with  good  character  delineation,  showing 
the  strong  influence  of  an  idolized  and  idealized 
mother  upon  a  fine  sensitive  young  man. 


Goldberg,  Isaac,  ed.  and  tr.

Gowing,  Sidney. 


The  joy  of  living.     1922. 
F  22539 


Aimee,  the  daughter  of  an  arch-deacon  is  about 
to  be  sent  away  to  a  maiden  aunt,  at  which  she 
rebels  and  substitutes  her  cousin.  She  has  a  num- 
ber of  thrilling  escapades  with  an  American  motor- 
cyclist. It  is  clean,  amusing,  and  improbable. 

Graham,     R.     B.     Cunninghame.       Scottish 

stories.     1914.  F  22611 

Contents:        The      colonel.  —  Aunt      Eleanor.  —  My 

uncle.  —  The    grey    kirk.  —  At    Dalmary.  —  A    retainer. 

—  Beattock    for    Moffat.  —  Fate.—  Ha    til    mi    tuliadh. 

—  Miss   Christian   Jean.  —  A   princess.  —  A   braw    day. 

—  Caisteal-na-Sithan.  —  M'Kechnie    v.    Scaramanga.  — 
Pollybaglan.  —  A  convert. 

Gregory,  Jackson.  The  everlasting  whisper; 
a  tale  of  the  California  wilderness.     1922. 

F  13222 

The  everlasting  whisper  of  the  wind  in  the  pine 
trees,  casts  its  magic  spell  over  a  beautiful  eastern 
girl  and  a  big,  fine  man  of  the  west,  drawing  them 
together,  leading  them  on  to  seek  for  gold  in  the 
very  heart  of  the  wilderness  where  they  find  the 
most  precious  of  all  treasures,  a  great  and  enduring 
love. 

Gunnarsson,  Gunnar.     Guest  the  One-eyed. 
1922.  F  22657 

Contents:  0rmarr  0rlygsson.  —  The  Danish  lady 
at  Hof.  —  -Guest  the  One-eyed.  —  The  young  eagle. 

A  beautiful  tale  of  peasant  life  in  Iceland,  giving 
also  a  very  vivid  picture  of  the  landscape.  The 
story  centers  about  three  generations  of  the  Borg 
family,  of  which  two  brothers  (one,  Guest,  the 
one-eyed)  are  the  outstanding  characters. 


Hall,  Jarvis. 
1921. 


Through   Mocking  Bird   Gap. 
F 13449 


This  is  a  new  and  pleasing  handling  of  Mexican 
border  life,  full  of  swift  action  and  unexpected- 
ness, told  with  an  interesting  straightforwardness 
of  style. — Literary  Review. 

Hendryx,  James   B.     Snowdrift;  a  story  of 
the    land    of   the    strong    cold.      1922. 

F 13661 

A  story  of  a  search  for  gold  in  the  great  North- 
west, in  which  a  fortune  is  made  and  lost  but  a 
great  love  won. 

O.Henry  memorial  award;   prize  stories   of 
1921.  F 18283 

Contents:  The  heart  of  Little  Shikara,  by  E. 
Marshall.— The  man  who  cursed  the  lilies,  by  C.  T. 
Jackson. — The  urge,  by  M.  Allen. — Mummery,  by 
T.  Beer.. — The  victim  of  his  vision,  by  G.  Chitten- 
den. — Martin  Gerrity  gets  even,  by  C.  R.  Cooper 
and  L.  F.  Creagan. — Stranger  things,  by  M.  Cram. 
— Comet,  by  S.  A.  Derieux. — Fifty-two  weeks  for 
Florette.  by  E.  A.  Heermann. — Wild  earth,  by  S. 
Kerr. — The  tribute,  by  H.  A.  Kniffin. — The  get- 
awav,  by  O.  F.  Lewis.— "Aurore,"  by  E.  W.  Mum- 
ford. — Mr.  Downey  sits  down,  by  L.  H.  Robbins. 
— The  marriage  in  Kairwan,  by  W.  D.  Steele. — 
Grit,  by  T.  Tupper. 

Hervey,     Harry.       Caravans     by     night;     a 
romance  of  India.     1922  F  13723 

A  colorful  tale,  full  of  mystery,  intrigue  and 
romance,  which  centers  around  the  theft  of  jewels. 
The  hero,  a  young  American  detective,  has  many 
an  exciting  chase  over  India  in  pursuit  of  the  thief. 
The  recovery  of  the  jewels  and  the  culmination  of 
a  very  pretty  love  story  make  a  delightful  ending. 


Hine,   Muriel,   (Mrs.   Sidney    Coxon).     Tor- 
quil's    success.      1922.  F  21390 

An  excellent  character  study  of  a  struggling 
English  novelist  who  is  brilliant,  but  a  thorough 
egotist.  He  ultimately  finds  the  road  to  real  suc- 
cess by  "putting  behind  him  the  greed  for  fame 
and  writing  only  for  the  love  of  his  work." 

Hurst,   Fannie.     The   vertical  city.      1922. 

F  23284 

Contents:  She  walks  in  beauty. — Back  pay. — 
The  vertical  city. — The  smudge. — Guilty. — Roulette. 

The  setting  of  these  short  stories  is  New  York, 
the  vertical  city  of  slim  sky  scrapers,  "that  shoot 
upward  with  the  rush  of  a  Roman  candle."  The 
stories  are  well  done,  interesting  but  somewhat  de- 
pressing as  each  one  has  its  tragedy. 


Huxley,  Aldous.      Crome   yellow. 


1922. 
F  23308 

A  novel  that  is  not  a  novel,  by  a  youthful  scion 
of  the  Huxley  and  Arnold  families.  Literature, 
astrology,  cubism,  Victorian  artificiality,  physical 
vs.  intellectual  vigor,  birth  control,  relativity,  and 
psychoanalysis  are  touched  upon  with  subtle  irony 
and  delicacy. 

Jewell,  Edward  Alden.  The  charmed  circle; 
a  comedy.     1921.  F  24089 

The  charmed  circle  surrounds  Kenneth,  an 
American  boy  who  has  been  expelled  from  numer- 
ous schools  in  the  U.  S.  and  now  lives  in  Paris 
with  his  guardian.  The  buoyancy  and  whimsical 
humor  of  the  characters  is  refreshing.  "They  are 
alive  to  the  point  of  giddiness"  says  the  Literary 
Review. 


Johnston,   Mary.     Silver   cross. 


1922. 

F  14412 

A  historical  romance  of  sixteenth-century  Eng- 
land. The  New  York  Times  compares  it  to  a 
"rich  and  glowing  tapestry.  .  .on  which  is 
pictured  in  colors,  sometimes  subdued,  sometimes 
brilliant,  the  life  of  the  time." 

Kaye-Smith,  Sheila.     Toanna  Godden.     1922. 

F  24141 

The  story  of  a  remarkably  dominating  person- 
ality impelled  by  primitive  instincts,  but  influenced 
also  by  the  traditions  of  her  family  and  class—- 
the prosperous  farmer  class  of  provincial  England. 
Excellent  characterization  throughout  with  appro- 
priate subordination  of  minor  characters. 

Levison,  Eric.     Hidden  eyes.     1920.     F  24517 

An  exciting  detective  story  in  which  the  mystery 
is  solved  by  a  doctor  who  is  fond  of  experiment- 
ing in  auto-suggestion  and  psychoanalysis. 

McCutcheon,   George    Barr.     Yollop.      1922. 

F  24825 

A  satirical  little  book  on  the  modern  hold-up 
man  and  the  kind  philanthropist.  The  New  York 
Times  says,  "His  novelette  as  a  whole  is  enter- 
taining, it  has  frequent  flashes  of  wit,  and  its 
hits  at  the  faults  and  foibles  of  our  system  of 
penology  are  well  aimed." 

Rich  relatives.   1921. 
F  15229 

"Rich  relatives"  is  even  more  amusing  than 
"Poor  relations"  (F  14940)  was.  It  is  the  story 
of  a  young  girl  left  alone  by  the  death  of  her 
father,  who  is  handed  about  among  her  rich  rela- 
tives— a  simple  enough  plot,  but  one  from  which 
Mr.  Mackenzie  extracts  an  essence  of  fun. — Chi- 
cago Tribune. 

McNeile,  Cyril,   (Sapper).     The  man  in  rat- 
catcher, and  other  stories.     1921.     F  24901 
Contents:      The    man    in    ratcatcher. — "An    arrow 
at   a   venture." — The    house   by    the   headland. — The 
man    who    would    not    play    cards. — A    question    of

personality. — The  unbroken  line. — The  real  test. —

Marshall,    Archibald.      Big    Peter.      1922. 

F 24974 

The  hero,  a  young  man  who  has  grown  up_  in 
Australia  in  the  bush,  suddenly  finds  to  his  astonish- 
ment that  he  is  the  rightful  heir  to  a  large  estate 
in  England.  There  follow  complications  and  sur- 
prises, in  the  course  of  which  Peter  discovers  the 
girl  of  his  dreams  and  finally  comes  into  his  own. 


Marshall,    Edison. 
1922. 


Shepherds    of   the    wild. 
F  24979 


This  is  a  story  of  life  in  the  open,  of  wild  ani- 
mals, and  of  adventure.  A  young  eastern  socjety 
man  goes  west  to  try  some  big  game  hunting. 
The  development  of  his  character  and  his  love 
for  a  western  girl  form  the  human  interest  of  the 
story. 

Milne,  A.  A.     Mr.  Pirn.     1922.  F 15385 

A  novelized  form  of  the  author's  play,  Mr.  Pim 
passes  by. 

Mitchell,  Ruth  Comfort.    Jane  journeys  on. 
1922.  F 15407 

Jane  journeys  o_ut  of  a  quaint  New  England  vil- 
lage into  the  whirlpool  of  New  York  and  on  to 
the  career  of  a  successful  playwright.  In  Chicago, 
Maine  and  Mexico  she  has  some  lively  adventures 
but  at  length  returns  to  New  York  and  the  man 
she  loves. 

Nicolson,    Harold.      Sweet    waters.       1922. 

F 15683 

A  story  of  the  love-life  of  a  young  girl  in  the 
Orient  during  the  Balkan  wars.  It  combines  the 
impressionistic  method  of  characterization  with  ex- 
cellent descriptions.  To  quote  the  New  York  Times, 
"It  is  a  very  curious  book  but  it  is  well  done." 

Norris,  Kathleen.     Lucretia  Lombard.   1922. 

F  25612 

_A  triangle  theme  involving  Stephen  Winship,  dis- 
trict attorney,  and  two  lovely  women.  Mimi,  the 
younger  of  the  two,  is  the  sweet  light-hearted  girl 
to  whom  he  is  engaged.  Dignified  Lucretia  Lom- 
bard is  the  other  woman. 

Oppenheim,  E.  Phillips.     The  great  Prince 
Shan.     1922.  F  25762 

The  scene  is  laid  in  England  in  1934,  and  con- 
cerns the  breaking  up  of  a  plot  against  England, 
whereby  a  secret  treaty  was  to  be  signed  by  Ger- 
many, China  and  Russia. 

Owen,  John.     The    cotton   broker.     1921. 

F 25828 

"Prosperity  Crossford,"  wins  his  success  by  a 
ruthless  disregard  of  the  rights  of  others.  The 
time  comes  when  he  must  pay  the  price  of  his 
intolerance.  The  New  York  Times  says,  "There 
is  some  excellent  psychological  analysis,  often  a 
clear  understanding  and  delineation  of  character 
and  motive." 

Pryde,  Anthony,  and  Weekes,  R.   K.     The 
purple  pearl.     1922.  F  16657 

The  purple  pearl,  a  gem  of  great  value,  has  been 
lost  for  Agree  generations,  and  has  been  the  oc- 
casion of  a  bitter  feud  between  three  branches  of 
a  noble  family.  When  the  three  branches  dwindle 
to  two  remarkably  attractive  young  men  and  two 
adorable  girls,  there  are  reasons  for  a  romantic  end 
to  the  family  quarrel. 

Robinson,     Edwin     Meade.       Enter    Jerry. 
1921.  F 17382 

An  intimate  detailed  story  of  the  life  of  an 
Indiana  schoolboy,  told  by  the  boy  himself,  from 
his  earliest  recollections  until  at  seventeen  he  is 


ready    to    enter    college.      Delightfully    written    and 
often  amusing,  a  very   successful   first  novel. 

Sangster,  Margaret  E.  The  island  of  faith. 
1921.  F 17618 

A  poignant  tale  of  New  York's  tenement  district, 
an  island  of  hundreds  of  poor,  written  by  the  grand- 
daughter of  Margaret  Sangster  with  the  same  sweet 
sincerity  and  faith  which  characterized  the  work 
of  that  celebrated  writer. 

Scrymsour,  Ella.  The  perfect  world;  a 
romance  of  strange  people  and  strange 
places.  1922.  F  17875 

Highly  imaginative  and  somewhat  imitative  of 
Rider  Haggard. 

Sears,  Clara  Endicott.  The  romance  of  Fid- 
dler's green.  1922.  F  17883 
The  story  is  told  simply,  but  with  power  and 
distinction  of  style,  and  truthfully  depicts  one  of 
those  strangely  quaint  romantic  episodes,  touched 
with  superstition  and  mysticism,  such  as  sometimes 
happened  on  New  England  farms  two  or  three  gen- 
erations ago. 

Shanks,  Edward.  The  people  of  the  ruins; 
a  story  of  the  English  revolution  and 
after.  1920.  F 17954 

The  revolution  breaks  out  in  1924  and  Jeremy 
Tuft  is  shocked  by  a  bomb,  from  which,  one  hun- 
dred and  fifty  years  after,  he  awakens  into  a 
ruined  and  uncivilized  world.  The  ruler  desires 
to  undertake  reconstruction  and  Jeremy  Tuft  is 
pressed  into  service.  During  the  ensuing  war  dis- 
asters all  the  main  characters  are  destroyed. 

Sinclair,  Bertrand  W.  Poor  Man's  Rock. 
1920.  F 18160 

The  interest  of  the  story  lies  in  the  description 
of  the  salmon  fisheries  of  British  Columbia  and 
a  struggle  against  big  interests.  The  strong  char- 
acter is  a  returned  soldier  who  carries  on  a  grudge 
of  his  father.  The  love  interest  is  slight. 

Sinclair,  May  Life  £nd  death  of  Harriett 
Frean.  1922.  F 18164 

In  content  and  significance  it  is  a  long  novel, 
although  in  the  number  of  words,  it  is  very  short. 
It  contains  more  feeling,  thought  and  utterance 
than  may  be  found  in  many  another  novel  of  four 
or  five  times  its  length,  and  the  richness  and  ful- 
ness of  humanity  pervades  every  page. — -E.  F. 
Edgett  in  Boston  Transcript. 


Terhune.     Albert      Payson. 
1922. 


Black     gold. 
F  27744 


In  a  very  frank  preface  Mr.  Terhune  declares 
that  his  new  mystery  romance  is  just  "a  yarn 
with  lots  of  action.  .  .Its  hero  is  a  hero.  Its 
villain  is  a  villain.  The  heroine  is  just  a  hero- 
ine; not  a  human."  But,  he  adds,  "it  moves." 

—His  dog.     1922.  F  27745 

Story  of  a  lonely  young  farmer  who  finds  a 
broken-legged  collie,  takes  it  home  and  cares  for 
it.  A  mutual  devotion  springs  up  between  the  two 
and  they  become  inseparable  companions.  A  slender 
love  story,  brought  to  a  happy  conclusion  by  a 
heroic  act  of  the  dog,  adds  interest. 


Tozzi,    Federigo.      Three    crosses. 


1921. 
F  27847 


The  story  of  three  brothers  who  have  a  book- 
shop but  seem  unable  to  make  a  success  of  it.  One 
becomes  a  forger  and  is  successful  for  a  time  but 
the  demoralizing  effect  upon  the  three  is  deadly. 
Powerfully  drawn  by  a  master  hand. 

Young,  Francis  Brett.     The  black  diamond. 

1921.  F  19940 

The    story    opens    with    the    leading    character    a 


78 


THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


boy  of  fourteen  and  takes  him  through  the  period 
of  adolescence  _  to  manhood.  The  novel  is  very 
strikingly  individual,  firm  of  outline,  rich  in  de- 
scription and  characterization — "one  of  the  very 
best  novels  of  the  past  year"  in  the  opinion  of 
the  New  York  Times.

A  statement  of  the  fundamental,  familiar  rules

of  childhood.     1921. 


The  melody 
E  15100 


Wright,  Harriet  Sabra.  New  plays  from 
old  tales,  arranged  for  boys  and  girls 
1921.  E  13027 

Contents:  Aucassin  and  Nicolette. — The  birth- 
day of  the  infanta,  by  Wilde. — The  princess  who  hid 
her  shoes,  by  Nyblom. — Tamlane,  an  old  ballad. — Pil- 
grim's progress,  by  Bunyan. — Three  Sundays  in  a 
week,  by  Foe. — On  the  old  plantation,  by  Pyrnelle. 
— Feathertop,  by  Hawthorne. 

Folktale,  fairy  tale  and  history  appropriately 
adapted  for  every  occasion.  All  the  speeches  fol- 
low the  text  closely.  The  arrangements  are  very 
simple,  and  the  plays  can  easily  be  given  by  schools, 
settlements,  clubs,  and  other  amateur  groups. — 
Chicago  Evening  Post. 


Branner,   John    C. 
1921. 


Stories 

How   and 


why   stories. 
H7488 


A  group  of  African  folklore  stories  written  as 
nearly  as  possible  in  the  spirit  and  language  of 
the  negro.  There  are  such  interesting  titles  as: 
"Why  the  palms  of  a  negro's  hands  are  white," 
and  "Why  the  owl  stays  up  nights." 

Curtis,  Alice  Turner.     A  little  maid  of  old 
New    York.      1921.  H  7758 

This  story  tells  the  adventures  of  a  loyal  Ameri- 
can girl  and  her  dearest  friend,  a  Tory  girl,  in 
the  days  when  British  soldiers  occupied  New  York. 

Deland,   Ellen   Douglas.     The   secret   stairs. 
1921.  H  7808 

Isabel  Rodney  is  a  delightful  little  heroine  who 
has  many  adventures.  A  thread  of  mystery  keeps 
the  reader  guessing  and  helps  to  make  a  thor- 
oughly fascinating  story  for  girls  from  twelve  to 
fourteen. 


Durell,  Charles  Pendexter. 
the   Cynthia   B.     1921. 


The  skipper  of 
H7951 


A  wholesome  story  of  the  Cape  Cod  country.  An 
old  sea  captain  helps  to  make  a  delightful  vacation 
for  a  young  city  boy.  For  the  boy  who  likes  sail- 
ing and  fishing. 

Fryer,  Jane  Eayre.  The  Mary  Frances 
story  book;  or,  Adventures  among  the 
story  people.  1921.  H  3937 


Mary  Frances  makes  an  imaginary  trip  to  the 
"enchanted  island"  of  good  story  tellers.  The 
stories  which  she  had  to  tell  are  contained  in  this 
book. 

Gray,  Violet  Gordon.  Margery  Morris  in 
the  pine  woods.  1921.  H  8297 

The  fourth  book  in  the  Margery  Morris  series. 
During  Margery's  recuperation  from  a  serious  ill- 
ness in  the  pine  woods,  she  proves  her  American- 
ism by  teaching  citizenships  to  the  children  of  that 
out-of-the-way  spot. 

Hendryx,  James  B.  Connie  Morgan  with 
the  Mounted.  1918.  H  8413 

Connie  Morgan  in  the  fur  country.  1921. 

H8417 

Further  adventures  of  Connie  Morgan.  He  is 
the  kind  of  a  hero  who  performs  one  remarkable 
feat  after  another. 

Herben,  Beatrice  Slayton.  Jack  O'Health 
and  Peg  O'Joy;  a  fairy-tale;  with  jingles 
by  school  children  of  Public  School  No. 
15  of  New  York  city.  1921.  H  8418 

Spirited  rhymes  and  jingles  with  a  slender 
thread  of  story  running  through,  compose  this 
modern  fairy  tale.  It  is  for  very  young  children 
and  its  purpose  is  to  encourage  cleanliness. 

Irwin,  Inez  Haynes.  Maida's  little  house. 
1921.  H  4433 

The  book  which  follows  Maida's  little  shop, 
(H3991).  It  tells  the  story  of  a  happy  summer 
in  the  little  house  with  many  exciting  circumstances.

Susan  and  Geneyieve  Gilyarry  are  two  sisters

Martin,    John,    pseud,    of   Morgan    Shepard.

H5257 

Ransome,     Arthur.       Old     Peter's     Russian 
tales.      1917.  H  5913 

The  stories  which  old  Peter  tells  to  his  grand- 
children are  well  told  and  amusing.  Many  of  them 
have  counterparts  in  other  folklore  but  they  differ 
enough  to  make  them  seem  new  to  the  children. 

Rolt-Wheeler,     Francis.       Hunting     hidden 
treasure    in    the    Andes.      1921.         H  6138 
(Round   the    world   with   the   boy   journalists,    2.) 
An   informational   story   of   South   America.      The 
hero  is  a  half-breed  Indian  boy  who  discovers  hid- 
den  treasure  in  an   old  Inca  temple.

The  Book  Bulletin 


of  the 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Volume  12  June  1922  No.  6 


Books  for  Vacation 

TO  undertake  the  compilation  of  lists  of  Vacation  Books  to  meet  the  needs 
of  a  great  variety  of  people  is  neither  an  easy  nor  a  very  useful  task. 
Any  book  may  be  a  vacation  book  if  it  gets  into  the  hands  of  the  right 
person,  and   the  librarian  is  far  better  employed  in   making  that  happy  con- 
junction  possible  than  in  trying  to  compile  book  lists  and  reading  courses  de- 
signed to  fit  all  sorts  and  conditions  of   men.      We    should    much   rather   pre- 
scribe for  individual   cases  than   deal  in  patent  medicines  and  we  believe  that 
we  are  reasonably  well  equipped  to  do  this. 

To  begin  with,  there  are  any  number  of  printed  catalogs,  lists  and  books 
about  books  which  are  useful  in  stimulating  appetites  or  establishing  reac- 
tions. Such  are,  for  example,  the  delightful  series  called:  Viewpoints  in 
Biography,  Viewpoints  in  Travel,  and  Viewpoints  in  Essays,  all  to  be  had  at 
the  Information  Desk  and  all  presenting  a  wide  variety  of  choice  in  these  fasci- 
nating fields.  Our  own  recent  catalogs,  the  Books  of  1916-20  and  its  continua- 
tions, are  especially  rich  in  modern  poetry  and  drama,  to  say  nothing  of  all  the 
other  subjects  covered.  And  there  are  plenty  of  other  bibliographical  aids  to 
facilitate  our  efforts  to  serve  patron's  with  the  right  books  for  all  occasions  and 
for  all  tastes.  Behind  all  these  is  the  vast  storehouse  of  the  books  themselves, 
each  waiting  its  turn  to  serve,  and  to  be  served  to,  exactly  the  reader  best 
fitted  to  use  it  as  a  Vacation  Book  and  to  read  it  "for  fun." 
Let  us  prescribe  for  you. 


82  THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

VACATION  loans  are  made  on  and  after  June  first  to  any  regis- 
tered card-holder.  As  many  as  ten  books  may  be  taken  out 
at  one  time,  to  be  returned  on  or  before  October  first.  Five  of 
the  ten  may  be  fiction,  but  not  more  than  two  seven-day  books;  the 
rest  may  be  drawn  at  will  from  any  or  all  classes  of  literature,  ex- 
cept the  most  recent  books,  or  all  ten  may  be  non-fiction  if  preferred. 

Vacation  loans  are  made  to  juvenile  borrowers  upon  the  writ- 
ten request  of  parent  or  guardian. 

The  loan  must  be  made  in  one  transaction  either  at  the  Cen- 
tral Library  or  at  a  branch.  The  books  will  be  issued  at  one  time 
for  the  whole  vacation  period  and  may  be  returned  in  installments. 
The  borrower's  regular  library  card  must  be  surrendered  when  the 
vacation  loan  is  made,  and  will  be  returned  to  him  when  all  obliga- 
tions have  been  canceled. 

Borrowers  may  arrange  to  have  their  vacation  books  shipped 
out  of  town  to  a  summer  address  by  leaving  the  amount  of  postage 
plus  a  fee  to  cover  wrapping  and  shipping,  at  the  rate  of  three 
cents  per  volume  or  twenty-five  cents  for  ten.  All  shipping,  both 
ways,  will  be  at  the  borrower's  risk. 

Borrowers  may  obtain  help  in  making  their  selections  by  in- 
quiring at  any  Branch  or  at  the  Information  Desk  of  the  Central 
Library. 

When  books  urgently  desired  for  vacation  use  are  not  available 
on  the  day  when  the  borrower  calls,  arrangements  may  be  made  to 
have  them  sent  later  by  leaving  a  deposit  to  cover  postage  and  ship- 
ping charges.  Any  balance  of  such  deposit  remaining  at  the  end  of 
the  vacation  period  will  be  refunded. 

Parcel-Post  Cards 

We  would  call  attention,  also,  to  the  parcel-post  card  issued 
by  the  Library.  This  enables  the  borrower  to  pay  for  a  dollar's 
worth  of  postage  at  a  time,  so  that  he  may  receive  and  send  back 
books  by  mail  when  out  of  the  city.  This  arrangement,  however, 
covers  only  the  regular  loan  period  of  two  weeks  (with  the  privilege 
of  renewal  for  two  more  weeks)  and  does  not  have  reference  to  va- 
cation loans.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate.    Those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  Room;  other 

collections  are  shelved  in  the  Civics  Department,    Music    Room   and   Thomas 

Hughes  Room  and  are  marked  accordingly.    *Ser.    denotes    serials 

which  do  not  circulate.

Brett,    George    Sidney, 
chology.     1912-1921.

3   v.  L  11106 


(Library   of  philosophy.) 

v.   1.     Ancient    and    patristic. 

2.  Mediaeval    and    early    modern   period. 

3.  Modern  psychology. 

Brill,    A.    A.      Fundamental    conceptions    of 
psychoanalysis.      1921.  L- 13965 

Addressed  to  the  intelligent  layman  who  is  seek- 
ing some  knowledge  of  the  ideas  which  have  revo- 
lutionized modern  psychology.  It  develops  the 
Freudian  theories  in  regard  to  dreams,  mythology, 
and  fairy  tales. 

Dewey,  John.     Human  nature  and  conduct; 
an  introduction  to  social  psychology.  1922. 

L  13964 

An  emphatic  protest  against  subjective  psychology. 
There  are  chapters  on  war,  custom,  education,  free- 
dom, the  psychology  of  thinking — all  carrying  out 
the  principal  theme,  the  interaction  of  human  na- 
ture and  environment. 


(Library  of  philosophy.) 

Russell  deals  with  his  problem  in  the  light  of 
some  works  of  William  James  and  his  American 
disciples,  but  he  handles  the  problem  afresh  in  a 
way  that  is  quite  his  own,  bringing  to  the  task 
a  native  and  acquired  equipment — logical,  mathe- 
matical, philosophical. — Literary  Review. 

Varendonck,  J.  The  psychology  of  day- 
dreams, with  an  introduction  by  Prof.

A  genuine  well-documented  first-hand  study  of  an

Westerfield,     Ray    B.       Banking    principles 
and    practice.      1921.      5v.  L 17379 

v.l.     Elements   of  money,   credit,   and  banking. 

2.  The  banking  system  of  the  United  States. 

3.  Domestic   banking;    cash   and  deposit   opera- 
tions. 

4.  Domestic  banking;   earning  assets. 

5.  The  foreign  division. 

A  work  for  bankers,  students  of  banking,  and 
business  men.  It  covers  completely  the  present 
United  States  banking  system,  the  theory  on  which 
it  is  based,  and  the  principles  which  underlie  bank- 
ing practice.  These  princples  are  traced  through 
every  department  of  a  representative  bank.

Dodd,  Walter  Fairleigh.  The  revision  and

L  15782,  1 

(Johns  Hopkins  University  studies  in  historical

MacDonald,  William.  A  new  constitution 
for  a  new  America.  1921.  L  18034 

Mr.  MacDonald  advocates  a  thorough  revision 
of  the  constitution  in  view  of  the  great  changes 
that  have  taken  place  since  its  adoption.  Its  pur- 
pose would  be  "to  break  the  rule  of  class  and  re- 
store government  to  the  people." 

Wade,  Martin  J.,  and  Russell,  William  F. 
The  short  constitution;  being  a  considera- 
tion of  the  constitution  of  the  United 
States,  with  particular  reference  to  the 
guarantees  of  life,  liberty,  and  property 
contained  therein,  sometimes  designated 
the  Bill  of  Rights.  1921.  L  18031 

(Elementary  Americanism  series.) 

Intended  for  use  in  the  home,  the  club  and  in 
general  Americanization  work.  It  contains  the  dec- 
laration of  independence  and  the  constitution  of 
the  United  States,  taking  up  each  clause  of  the 
latter  and  explaining  it  fully.  Following  each  chap- 
ter are  notes,  comments  and  questions.

Criminals 

Kingston,  Charles.  Remarkable  rogues; 
the  careers  of  some  notable  criminals  of 
Europe  and  America.  1921.  L  18893 

In  the  straightforward  snappy  style  of  the  news- 
paper reporter  Mr.  Kingston  relates  the  stories  of 
twenty  notable  criminals  of  recent  times.  Among 
those  discussed  are  Mme.  Rachel,  the  beauty  special- 
ist; Mme.  Guerin,  matrimonial  agent;  and  Belle 
Star,  the  girl  bushranger.

Fry,  Henry  P.  The  modern  Ku  Klux  Klan. 
1922.  L  19058 

Mr.  Fry  became  a  member  of  the  Klan  in  good 
faith,  but  after  a  thorough  study  of  the  system  was 
convinced  of  its  dangerous  character  and  assisted 
the  New  York  World  in  exposing  its  secrets.  He 
gives  a  clear  and  comprehensive  account  of  the  or- 
ganization from  the  inside.

from  North  America.  1907.  L  21 157 

With    music. 


Strachey,  Marjorie.  Savitri  and  other  wom- 
en. 1921.  L  21172 
Contents:  Savitri. — The  lay  of  the  ash  tree. — 
Yanka  and  her  brothers. — Saint  Iria. — rVassilissa  the 
wise. — -Janet  and  Tamlin. — Libussa  the  prophetess. 
— Jpukahainen's  sister. — The  bamboo-cutter's  story. 
— The  building  of  Skadar. — The  courtship  of  Etain. 
Folklore  stories  taken  from  the  legends  of  India, 
Brittany,  Serbia,  Portugal,  Russia,  Scotland,  Bo- 
hemia, Japan  and  Ireland.  The  Boston  Transcript 
considers  "Janet  and  Tamlin"  "the  gem  of  the  col- 
lection, in  so  much  that  the  style  of  the  adaptor 
here  runs  in  limpid  and  beautiful  prose." 

LANGUAGE 

McGregor,  A.  Laura.  Supervised  study  in 
English  for  junior  high  school  grades. 
1921.  J  7187 

Miss  McGregor  presents  a  technique,  for  the  .Eng- 
lish lesson  with  the  double  period  which  does  away 
with  the  necessity  of  home  work.  To  quote  the 
School  Review,  "A  more  immediately  practical  set 
of  definite  suggestions  of  the  sort  required  it  would 
be  difficult  to  find."

K  19794 


A  book  that  is  sure  to  be  "heartily  welcomed  by

Bolton,  L.  An  introduction  to  the  theory 
of  relativity.  1921.  K 19063 

Based  on  Mr.  Bolton's  essay  on  relativity  and 
gravitation,  which  won  the  Eugene  Miggins  five- 
thousand  dollar  prize.  The  London  Times  says,  "Mr. 
Bolton's  style  is  clear  and  precise,  and  ht-  is  ob- 
viously aware  of  many  of  the  difficulties  experienced 
by  the  ordinary  reader  who  attempts  to  follow  the 
theory  of  relativity." 

Burkitt,  M.  C.  Prehistory;  a  study  of  early 
cultures  in  Europe  and  the  Mediterranean 
basin.  1921.  K  20764 

It  is  indeed  a  feat  to  have  produced  so  early  in 
his  career  a  work  that  every  student  of  prehistorics 
will  henceforth  be  glad  to  have  at  his  elbow. — 
Times  (London.)

of  radioactive  substances  and  their  radia- 
tions, the  nature  of  radioactivity  and  the 
bearing  of  radioactive  transformations  on

mals. — The  birds  of  London. — The  plague  of  birds.

Sadler,  William  S.  Race  decadence;  an  ex- 
amination of  the  causes  of  racial  degen- 
eracy in  the  United  States.  1922.  N  2813 
This  book  deals  with  the  causes  of  racial  de- 
cadence, pointing  out  those  removable  by  the  indi- 
vidual himself  and  those  due  to  social  conditions. 
The  author  stresses  the  point  that  we  eat  too 
much  and  exercise  too  little.  A  valuable  work. 

Nutrition 

Emerson,  William  R.  P.  Nutrition  and 
growth  in  children.  1922.  N  2499 

Dr.  Emerson  explains  in  simple  terms  the  causes 
and  cure  of  malnutrition,  which  is  one  of  the 
great  problems  affecting  our  national  health.  He 
outlines  a  complete  nutrition  program  for  the 
home,  the  school  and  the  community. 

Macfadden,  Bernarr.  Eating  for  health  and 
strength.  1921.  N  2498

Files 

Disston  (Henry)  &  Sons,  Philadelphia.  The 
file;  its  history,  making,  and  uses;  a  de- 
scription of  the  development  of  the  file 
from  the  earliest  times  to  the  present  day; 
a  brief  statement  of  the  modern  method 
of  file  making;  a  description  of  the  great 
variety  of  files  and  the  numerous  uses  to 
which  the  tool  is  adapted.  1921.  K  22972 

Fremont,  Charles.  Files  and  filing,  tr.  from 
the  French.  1920.  K  22973 

A  translation  from  the  French  on  the  technology 
of  file  making.  It  begins  with  the  origin  of  the  file, 
describing  the  prehistoric  instrument  made  of  bone, 
skins,  shells,  and  stone,  and  follows  its  evolution  to 
the  present  form.

with to  demonstrate  its  possibilities  for  profit  and

ters on  the  seller,  the  buyer,  making  the  sale,  the 
sales  department,  the  sales  letter,  and  selling  at 
retail.

1921. 


Animal    proteins. 
K  25772 


(Industrial   chemistry.) 

Deals  chiefly  with  the  proteins  of  chemical  tech- 
nology, leather,  and  the  process  of  tannage,  gela- 
tine, and  glue,  and  only  briefly  with  the  proteins

atory glass-blowing.  1921.  K  25491 

Easy  exercises  are  followed  by  directions  for  mak- 
ing a  large  number  of  special  pieces  of  apparatus.

tubes,  vacuum  tubes,  thermometers,  etc.  —  New  York

proteins;  their  colloid  chemistry  in  theory 
and  practice.  1921.  K  25567 

The  treatment  of  the  whole  subject  is  such  that 
even  those  not  expert  in  the  concepts  of  organic 
and  colloid  chemistry  will  find  no  difficulty  in  get- 
ting at  the  thought  of  the  author.

FINE  ARTS 

Fry,   Roger.     Vision   and   design.      1921. 

*P3011 

Contents:  Art  and  life. — An  essay  in  aesthetics. 
— The  ottoman  and  the  whatnot. — The  artist's  vi- 
sion.— Art  and  socialism. — Art  and  science. — The 
art  of  the  Bushmen. — Negro  sculpture. — Ancient 
American  art. — The  Munich  exhibition  of  Moham- 
medan art. — Giotto. — The  art  of  Florence. — The 
Jacquemart-Andre  collection. — Durer  and  his  con- 
temporaries.— £E1  Greco. — Three  pictures  in  tem- 
pera by  William  Blake. — Claude. — Aubrey  Beards- 
ley's  drawings. — The  French  post-impressionists. — 
Drawings  at  the  Burlington  fine  arts  club. — Paul  Ce- 
zanne.— Renoir. — A  possible  domestic  architecture. — 
Jean  Marchand. — Retrospect. 

The  intensity  of  Mr.  Fry's  reaction,  the  strength 
of  his  intellect,  his  sincerity  and  his  great  knowledge 
of  the  painter's  craft,  combine  to  give  him  a  criti- 
cal instrument  of  inestimable^  value — the  power  of 
getting  close  to  the  fact. — Clive  Bell  in  The  New 
Statesman.

K  27287 

A  book  written  to  help  those  who  have  limited
