Music 

Holland,  Remain.  A  musical  tour  through 
the  land  of  the  past.  1922.  K  29613 

Contents:  A  humorous  novel  by  an  eighteenth- 
century  musician. — An  English  amateur  (Pepys's 
diary). — A  portrait  of  Handel. — The  origins  of  the 
"classic"  style  in  eighteenth-century  music. — The 
autobiograph'y  of  a  forgotten  master:  Telemann, 
the  successful  rival  of  J.  S.  Bach. — Metastasio;  the 
forerunner  of  Gluck. — A  musical  tour  across  Europe 
in  the  eighteenth  century:  Italy,  Germany. 

Sequel  to  Musicians  of  former  days,  K  11787. 
Discusses  men  of  the  transition  period  who,  though 
not  famous,  were  the  pioneers  of  our  present  music. 
The  book  will  appeal  especially  to  musicians,  but 
it  is  not  too  technical  for  the  layman.

A  useful  book  for  students  of  the  essay.  The  in-

and  how  to  study  them.  Examples  by  well  known 
authors  are  given  of  the  personal,  descriptive,  crit- 
ical, editorial  and  reflective  essay.

Journalism,   Authorship 

Lubbock,  Percy.    The  craft  of  fiction.     1921. 

J7239 

An  example  of  the  best  qualities  of  the  analyti- 
cal method  of  criticism  ...  It  is  a  book  which  no 
writer  of  fiction  can  afford  to  leave  unread,  and  it 
is  one  which  will  also  very  much  enhance  the  pleas- 
ure of  every  intelligent  novel-reader. — Spectator. 

Smith,  Russell  E.  Co-operation,  merchan- 
dising and  promotion;  a  hand-book  for 
the  merchandising  and  promotion  depart- 
ments of  newspapers.  1921.  J  7265 
Covers  in  detail  the  service  that  can  be  rendered 
to  an  advertiser  entering  a  new  territory  and  is 
based  on  actual  practice.  It  is  a  pioneer  work  in 
its  field,  is  clear  and  thorough  and  will  be  of  value 
not  only  to  the  advertising  staffs  of  newspapers 
but  also  to  advertisers. — Literary  Review.

The  best  and  highest  praise  that  a  critic  can  give

is    what   Miss   Benet's   has — magic — a   heap    of   it. — 

Oliver  Jenkins  in  The  Chicago  Daily  News.

Leonard,  Sterling  Andrus,  comp.  Poems  of 
the  war  and  the  peace.  1921.  E  14363 
An  anthology  for  high-school  use  grouped  into 
poems  on  love  of  country,  pictures  of  the  war,  the 
supreme  sacrifice,  and  the  ideal  of  peace,  with  bio- 
graphical notes  on  the  poets. 

Masefield,  John.     King  Cole.     1921.    E  14867 
He  tells  the  story  of  the  legendary  king  returned 
to  earth  to  be  a  friend  to  man  in  his  usual  straight- 
forward   graphic    style,    good,    workmanlike    realism, 
enlivened  by  genuine  poetry. — Literary  Review. 

Metcalf,  John  Calvin,  and  Wilson,  James 
Southall,  ed.  The  enchanted  years;  a  book 
of  contemporary  verse,  dedicated  by  poets 
of  Great  Britain  and  America  to  the 


University  of  Virginia  on  the  occasion  of 
its    one-hundredth    anniversary.      1921. 

E  14362 

The  great  majority  of  the  poems  are  new,  and 
more  than  occasional  verse.  D.  H.  Lawrence,  Amy 
LowelL  Vachel  Lindsay,  Edwin  Arlington  Robin- 
son, Thomas  Hardy,  Arthur  Symons  and  Walter 
de  la  Mare  are  among  the  contributors. — Bookman. 

Robinson,  Edwin  Arlington.  Collected 
poems.  1921.  E  14968 

Contents:  The  man  against  the  sky. — The  chil- 
dren of  the  night. — Captain  Craig,  etc. — Merlin. — 
The  town  down  the  river. — Lancelot. — The  three 
taverns. — Avon's  harvest. 

In  this  one  unadorned  novel-size  blue  volume  we 
have  a  whole  net-work  of  plots,  a  whole  township 
of  men  and  women,  evidence  of  an  observation  and 
imagination  that  .  .  .  would  have  furnished  forth 
any  _  number  of  volumes  of  short  stories. — W.  R. 
Benet  in  The  Literary  Review. 

Shelby,  Annie  Blanche,  comp.  The  lullaby 
book;  or,  Mothers'  love  songs.  1921. 

E 14361 

A  tale  of  a  walled  town,  and  other  verses, 
by  B.  8266,  penitentiary;  with  an  in- 
troduction by  William  Stanley  Braith- 
waite.  1921.  E  15041 

The  "walled  town"  is  the  state  penitentiary  where 
the  writer  of  these  verses  is  a  life  prisoner.  The 
title  poem  relating  the  story  of  his  life,  though  not 
a  second  "Ballad  of  Reading  Gaol,"  is  written  with 
some  technical  skill  and  is  obviously  sincere.

1921.  E 14103 

His  book  embodies  the  conclusions  of  all  the 
best  French  critics  and  students  of  Moliere,  as  well

other  work  in  English  that  illuminates  so  clearly 
the  career  of  the  great  French  dramatist. — Spec-

passion  in  which  all  beauty  is  exalted  and  the 
draibness  and  commoness  of  men  is  perpetually 
mocked."

Black,    Alexander.      The    latest    thing    and 
other  things.      1922.  E 1956 

These  papers  are  to  be  read  with  leisure  as  a

await  again  the  right  moment.  They  incite  a  re- 
flective mood,  inspire  a  gentle  argument,  hold  many 
a  smile.- — -Hildegarde  Hawthorne  in  New  York

Contents:  America  and  the  young  intellectual.

Vince,  Charles.    Wayfarers  in  Arcady.    1922. 

E6231 

A  collection  of  twenty-six  short  essays  on  the 
English  countryside.  They  are  written  in  a  straight- 
forward manner  which  is  quite  as  wholesome  as  the 
downs,  the  hills  or  the  quiet  spot  on  the  land- 
scape which  the  author  describes.

Clark,  Charles   Upson.     Greater   Roumania. 
1922.  I  11061 

A  work  full  of  information  on  Roumania,  her

Rosinante   to   the  road 
110484 


Dos  Passes,  John, 
again.     1922. 

Impressions  of  Spain  gained  by  Telemachus  and 
Lyaeus  on  a  walking  tour.  The  book  abounds  in 
clever  conversation,  subtle  wit  and  gentle  satire. 
It  contains  much  that  is  informative  and  is  delight- 
ful reading  in  a  very  real  sense. 

Goff,  A.,  and  Fawcett,  Hugh  A.  Macedonia; 
a  plea  for  the  primitive.  1921.  I  10982 
A  delightful  book  beautifully  illustrated.  A  pic- 
ture of  Macedonia  that  brings  out  every  detail  of 
this  rugged  country,  its  villages  and  people,  leav- 
ing an  indelible  impression. 

Graham,  P.  Anderson.  Highways  and  by- 
ways in  Northumbria.  1920.  I  10367 
A  good  travel  book  written  with  an  enthusiasm 
that  gives  new  life  to  the  historical  and  legendary 
incidents  related  oif  those  heroic  but  'shadowy 
characters  who  fought  in  the  Wars  of  the  Ro.°es. 

Graham,  Stephen.  Europe — whither  bound? 
(Quo  vadis  Europa?);  being  letters  of 
travel  from  the  capitals  of  Europe  in  the 
year  1921.  1922.  1 11255 

Mr.  Graham  enables  you  to  walk  the  streets  of 
the  European  capitals  with  him,  and  all  who  know 
his  power  of  visualization  and  admire  his  skill  in 
discovering  the  inner  meanings  of  the  ordinary 
sights  he  sees  will  find  this  the  most  attractive 
of  all  his  published  works. — New  York  Timef. 


McKenna,    Stephen. 
1921. 


While    I 


remember. 
I  10368 


His  generation,  he  says,  ended  with  the  peace  of 
Versailles,  and  he  is  setting  down  his  memories  of 
it  before  they  fade.  He  does  it  with  that  sad  reti- 
cence, punctured  by  irritation  over  our  non-com- 
prehension, which  is  the  badge  of  our  own  war 
youngsters. — Chicago  Evening  Post. 

Maurel,  Andre.    A  fortnight  in  Naples.    1921. 

I  10450 

This  is  neither  a  guide  book  nor  a  book  of 
travel  nor  a  collection  of  essays,  but  rather,  per- 
haps, it  is  a  perplexing  mixture  of  all  three,  and  is

Nutting,  William  Washburn.  The  track  of 
the  "Typhoon."  1922.  111385 

The  story  of  the  building  and  sailing  of  a  45- 
footer,  which  sailed  over  7,000  miles,  crossing  the 
Atlantic  in  15  days,  returning  through  bad  storms 
via  Spain  and  the  Azores. — Publishers'  Weekly.

Excerpts  from  the  classics,  with  English  trans-

This   great  volume,   in  quarto,   is  the   handsomest

Trend,  J.  B.  A  picture  of  modern  Spain; 
men  and  music.  1921.  I  10483 

Contents:  Spain  after  the  war. — Education  in 
Spain. — Literature. — The  Catalan  contribution. — 
Spanish  plays  and  incidental  music. — Three  mem- 
ories of  music. 

One  of  tihe  most  readable  of  recent  books  on 
its  subject,  by  an  author  who  is  equally  at  home 
in  discussing  the  politics,  the  novel,  the  drama,

Asia 

Maxwell,  Donald.  A  painter  in  Palestine; 
being  an  impromptu  pilgrimage  through 
the  Holy  Land  with  Bible  and  sketch- 
book. 1921.  I  10913 
It  is  as  an  artist  he  describes  the  holy  places 
and  sacred  cities  of  Palestine.  This  point  of  view 
helps  him  to  make  illuminating  explanations  of  some 
difficult  passages  in  the  Bible  narrative  and  to 
bring  some  helpful  and  unconventional  suggestions 
to  bear  on  problems  of  modern  criticism. — Book 
Review  Digest. 

Tyau,    Min-ch'ien    T.    Z.      China    awakened. 
1922.  I  10666 

Mr.  Tyau  is  a  Chinese  publicist  of  distinction 
who  is  now  representing  China  in  the  League  of 
Nations.  He  discusses  China  of  today  and  the 
many  changes  that  are  taking  place,  the  progress 
in  elementary  education,  the  spread  of  journalism, 
the  student  movement,  etc. 


Africa,  Oceanica 

Calderon,    George,    (Tihoti).     Tahiti.      1921. 

19183 

Has  a  veracious  charm  and  a  wealth  of  refined 
observation  which  put  it  close  to  the  top  in  the  : 
South  Sea  cycle.  Tihoti  feels  and  conveys  the 
spell  of  the  islands  as  well  as  any  one,  but  he 
delves  deeper  than  most  into  their  language  and 
customs. — Daily  News. 

Rayne,  Major  H.  Sun,  sand  and  Sqmals; 
leaves  from  the  note-book  of  a  district 
commissioner  in  British  Somaliland.  1921. 

18852 

The  scenery,  climate,  laws  and  customs,  loves  and 
hates  of  that  arid  country  which  is  vaguely  known 
to  us  as  British  Somaliland  are  the  subject  of 
Major  Rayne's  vivid,  unpretentious,  and  pleasantly 
written  book. — Times  (London).

Cole,  H.  E.  Baraboo,  Dells,  and  Devil's

Dillon,  E.  J.     Mexico  on   the  verge.     1921. 

110027 

The  author  is  a  partisan  of  Obregon  who  be- 
lieves that  foreign  capital  is  responsible  for  all  the 
evils  that  beset  Mexico.  His  book  is  a  plea  for 
the  official  recognition  of  the  Obregon  govern- 
ment. 

Freeman,    Lewis    R.      Down    the    Columbia. 

1921.  1 11783 
An    account   of    a   boat    trip    down    the    Columbia 

river,  from  its  glacial  sources  to  its  mouth.  .  .  . 
The  author's  account  of  his  adventures  and  of  the 
grandeur  of  the  scenery  through  which  he  passed 
is  fully  illustrated  with  photographs  taken  along 
the  way. — Book  Review  Digest. 

Illinois.  Chamber  of  Commerce.  Illinois 
facts.  1921.  .  14349 

A  brief  survey  of  the  business  opportunities,  re- 
sources and  advantages  of  Illinois. 

Sheridan,  Clare.     My  American  diary.     1922. 

14333 

In  a  refreshing  manner  Clare  Sheridan  describes 
her  life  in  New  York  w<here  she  was  dined  by  the 
great,  the  near  great  and  the  rich.  Her  experi- 
ences in  Mexico  and  California  and  the  people  she 
met  are  all  vividly  portrayed  and  make  enjoyable 
reading. 

Thompson,  Wallace.  The  Mexican  mind; 
a  study  of  national  psychology.  1922. 

110025 

The  author  gives  an  interesting  analysis  of  the 
workings  of  the  Mexican  mind  and  an  insight  into 
Mexican  character,  temperament,  and  culture.  He 
tells  the  origin  of  the  race  and  the  conditions  and 
environments  of  social  and  political  life  which  go 
to  make  up  the  Mexican  as  he  exists  today.

Clara  Barton  left  two  vaults  full  of  letters, 
diaries  and  fragments  of  autobiography.  From  this 
mass  of  material  her  nephew,  Dr.  Barton  of  Oak 
Park,  has  produced  a  most  complete  and  authentic 
account  of  her  life.  '••

Chaplin,  Charlie.     My  trip  abroad.     1922. 

C  21191 

Charlie  Chaplin  has  written  a  naive  account  of 
his  visit  to  the  capitals  of  the  Old  World  where 
he  received  the  attention  ordinarily  reserved  for 
kings  and  generals.  Fanny  Butcher  in  the  Chi- 
cago Tribune  says:  "Charlie  Chaplin  is  the  funniest 
writer  in  America  or  he  is  the  greatest  satirist."

yet  indifferent  and  yearning  for  quiet.  These 
strangely  varient  characteristics  have  been  caught 
up  by  M.  Madelin. — Publisher's  Announcement.

Higginson,  Thomas  Wentworth.  Letters 
and  journals  of  Thomas  Wentworth  Hig- 
ginson, 1846-1906;  ed.  by  Mary  Thacher 
Higginson.  1921.  C  22429 

An  autobiography  filled  with  glimpses  of  literary, 
political  and  social  celebrities  of  the  past  fifty 
years,  describing  army  life  of  the  Civil  War,  yester- 
day's foreign  tiavejs,  and  New  England  life  at 
Cambridge  in  a  recital  of  intimate  incidents  vivid 
with  humor  and  sympathy. — Booklist. 

Kitchener,  H.  H.,  Lord.  ESHER,  REGINALD, 
Viscount.  The  tragedy  of  Lord  Kitchener. 
1921.  C 22791 

A  biography  by  a  friend  and  co-worker  who  knew 
Kitchener's  greatness  and  yet  has  the  courage  to 
voice  his  criticisms.  It  depicts  his  brilliant  career 
in  India  and  then  shows  the  pathetic  picture  of  a 
man  whose  judgment  was  blurred  by  time  and  con- 
ditions when  called  into  the  war. 

Laurier,  Sir  Wilfrid.  SKELTON,  OSCAR  DOUG- 
LAS. Life  and  letters  of  Sir  Wilfrid 
Laurier.  1922.  2  v.  C  22879 

A  well  written  biography  combining  the  life  story 
of  an  eminent  Canadian  statesman  with  the  his- 
tory of  Canada  during  the  critical  period  of  her 
development.

Mr.  Morgan  has  not  written  a  critical  biography, 
but  rather  a  genial  story  that  will  engage  the  in- 
terest of  those  who  like  to  while  away  a  long  even- 
ing with  a  judicious  mixture  of  personalia,  gossip, 
and  history. — Nation.

regard    for    beautiful   phrasing,   express   the   diverse

rarely  independent  mind,  humorous,  rich  and  wise 
far  beyond  its  years."

HISTORY 

Europe 

Addams,  Jane.  Peace  and  bread  in  time  of 
war.  1922.  A  7191 

A  history  of  the  work  of  the  Woman's  Peace 
Party  during  the  war  and  of  its  part  in  forming  the 
Women's  International  League  for  Peace  and  Free- 
dom. It  gives  an  insight  into  what  pacifism  really 
means  to  Miss  Addams  and  her  supporters. 

Ault,  Norman.  Life  in  ancient  Britain;  a 
survey  of  the  social  and  economic  devel- 
opment of  the  people  of  England  from 
earliest  times  to  the  Roman  conquest. 
1920.  A  5422 

From  the  findings  of  arciheologists,  the  author 
pictures  living  conditions  from  the  paleolithic  to 
the  early  iron  age.  He  shows  people  in  their 
homes  .  .  .  taking  the  first  steps  in  solving  the  prob- 
lems which  have  passed  down  the  long  ages  to  mod- 
ern times. — Booklist. 

Frank,  Tenney,  An  economic  history  of 
Rome  to  the  end  of  the  republic.  1920. 

A  2812 

It  stands  alone  in  its  completeness,  in  the 
thorough  use  which  the  author  has  made  of  avail- 
able evidence,  in  the  sound  judgment  which  he  has 
shown,  and  in  the  clear  convincing  way  in  which 
he  has  set  forth  his  conclusions. — American  His- 
tory Review. 

Guilford,    Everard   L.    Nottingham.      1920. 

A  5435 

(The  story  of  the  English  towns.) 

One  of  a  series  which  gives  a  compressed,  but 
interesting,  survey  of  the  history  of  each  town  from 
its  legendary  beginnings,  of  its  government,  its 
industries,  and  its  religious  institutions. — Booklist. 

Hearnshaw,  F.  J.  C.,  ed.  Mediaeval  contribu- 
tions to  modern  civilization;  a  series  of 
lectures  delivered  at  King's  College,  Uni- 
versity of  London.  1922.  A  6469 
Contents:  Introductory:  The  middle  ages  and 
their  characteristic  features,  by  F.  J.  C.  Hearn- 
shaw.— Tlhe  religious  contribution  of  the  middle 
ages,  by  C.  Jenkins. — Philosophy,  by  H.  W.  Carr. 
— Science,  by  C.  Singer. — Art,  by  P.  Dearmer.— 
The  middle  ages  in  the  lineage  of  English  poetry, 
by  Sir  I.  Gollancz. — Education,  by  J.  W.  Adam- 
son. — Society,  by  Hilda  Johnstone. — Economics,  by 
E.  R.  Adair.— Politics,  by  J.  W.  Allen. 

Mr.  Hearnshaw  feels  that  the  "Middle  ages  are 
a  pit  from  which  we  were  digged  and  the  rock 
from  which  we  were  hewn,"  the  origin  and  begin- 
nings of  things  as  they  exist  today.  The  special 
contributions  are  excellent.

Command.  1921.  A  7194 

Marcovitch,  L.,  ed.  Serbia  and  Europe, 
1914-1920.  1920.  A  7190 

Contents:  Serbian  politics. — The  union  of  Ser- 
bians, Croatians  and  Slovenes. — The  struggle  with

and  Italy. — Serbia  and  Germany. — Serbia  and  Rus- 
sia.— The  politics  of  Roumania. — Serbo-Greek  rela- 
tions.— Austro-Germano-Bulgarian  atrocities.

A  4975 

One  of  the  most  distinguished  of  British  sailors 
has  traced  from  the  earliest  times  the  fortunes  of 
Japanese  sea  power,  including  the  creation  of  the 
modern  Japanese  navy. — Boston  Transcript.

A  4974 


"The  inner  history  of  the  critical  years  in  the

time,  with  an  account  of  his  personal  experiences 
during  that  period."

Harrison,  Francis  Burton.  The  corner- 
stone of  Philippine  independence;  a  narra- 
tive of  seven  years.  1922.  B  7617 
Mr.  Harrison,  who  was  Governor  General  of  the 
Philippines  believes  that  the  Filipinos  have  proved 
themselves  ready  for  self-government.  He  presents 
in  a  very  readable  way  his  experiences  and  opin- 
ions as  well  as  the  historical  background  of  these 
people.

Andrews,  Mary  Raymond  Shipman.  His 
soul  goes  marching  on.  1922.  F  10573 
A  patriotic  narrative  in  which  the  author  pays 
a  tribute  to  Theodore  Roosevelt  by  relating  the 
great  influence  he  had  on  the  life  of  a  boy  whom 
he  accidentally  met,  and  with  whom  he  spoke  only 
once. 

Bibesco,  Elizabeth  Asquith.  I  have  only 
myself  to  blame.  1921.  F  11127 

Contents:  I  have  only  myself  to  blame. — To- 
morrow.— "The  web." — An  ordinary  man. — The 
gesture. — Cyclamen. — The  dream. — The  farewell. — 
Tout  comprendre. — Three  love  letters. — The  suc- 
cessor.— As  it  was  in  the  beginning. — The  old  story. 
— The  pilgrimage. — The  ball. — Fragment  of  a  cor- 
respondence. 

Cleverness  and  sophistication  are  two  qualities  one 
would  expect  to  find  as  a  matter  of  course  in  any 
books  written  by  a  daughter  of  Margot  Asquith 
and_  they  are  both  abundantly  present  in  this  col- 
lection of  short  stories. — New  York  Times. 

Buckrose,  J.  E.,  pseud,  of  Mrs.  A.  E.  F. 
Jameson.  The  privet  hedge.  1922. 

F  20776 

A   pleasantly   quiet   sort   of  story   portraying   Eng- 
•    lish  life  at  a  little  sea-side  resort  during  the  transi- 
tional period  of   readjustment  after  the"  war. 

Chekhov,  Anton.  The  schoolmaster,  and 
other  stories.  1921.  F  11931 

Contents:  The  schoolmaster. — Enemies. — The  ex- 
amining magistrate. — Betrothed.— From  the  diary 
of  a  violent-tempered  man. — In  the  dark. — A  play. 
— A  mystery. — Strong  impressions.- — Drunk. — The 
marshal's  widow. — A  bad  business. — In  the  court. 
— Boots. — Joy. — Ladies. — A  peculiar  man. — At  the 
barber's.- — -An  inadvertence. — The  album. — Oh!  The

ator.— Malingerers. — In  the  graveyard.  —  Hush ! 
— In  an  hotel. — In  a  strange  land. 

Dramatic,  intense  sketches  of  Russian  life  among 
the  upper  classes. — Booklist. 

Child,  Richard  Washburn.  The  hands  of 
Nara.  1922.  F 11935 

The  two  central  figures  are  Nara,  a  Russian 
refugee  whose  beautiful  hands  are  gifted  with 
powers  of  healing,  and  Emlen,  a  young  physician 
who  is  an  arch  enemy  of  the  mystic  cults.  Their 
common  search  for  truth,  hers  through  occultism, 
and  his  through  science,  forms  the  bond  between 
them. 


Collins,  Wilkie.     Basil.      1873. 


F  12066 


Delafield,  E.  M.,  pseud,  of  E  .E.  M.  De  la 
Pasture.  Humbug;  a  study  in  education. 
1922.  F 21 535 

A_  true  and  vivid  picture  of  the  fate  of  an  ex- 
ceptionally conscientious,  extremely  sensitive  child, 
whose  misfortune  it  was  to  be  born  of  ultra- 
sentimental  parents. — New  York  Times. 

Durkin,  Douglas.    The  Lobstick  trail.     1922. 

F 12617 

A  tale  of  the  great  Canadian  north-west  and  the 
redemption  of  Kirk  Brander,  a  reckless,  vacillating 
youth.  His  trials  and  vicissitudes  and  final  triumph 
make  a  story  that  will  greatly  appeal  to  lovers  of 
adventure. 

Fletcher,  J.  S.  The  Rayner-Slade  amalga- 
mation. 1922.  F 22130 

A  murder  mystery,  in  which  the  solution  seems 
evident  in  the  third  chapter.  But  the  anticipated 


solution  does  not  prove  correct  and  the  reader  is 
soon  completely  mystified.  The  climax  comes  as 
a  total  surprise. 

Footner,  Hulbert.     The  Deaves  affair.    1922. 

F  22184 

This  detective  story  deals  neither  with  an  inex- 
plicable crime  nor  an  elusive  murderer,  but  a  sharp 
edged  vein  of  humor  runs  throughout  and  makes 
the  book  an  unusual  one  of  its  kind. 

Hamsun,  Knut.     Wanderers.   1922.     F 13437 
Contents:     Under  the  autumn  star. — A  wanderer 

plays  on   muted  strings. 

A  story  of  Norwegian  gentry.     An  unusual  book 

of     great     power     and     excellent     characterization. 

Readers    will    recognize    the    author's    sincerity    and 

grasp    of   the    complexities   of   life. 

Harker,  L.  Allen.     The  bridge  across.    1921. 

F 13603 

A  continuation  of  the  life  of  Raby  Verdon  now 
grown  to  womanhood.  The  bridge  of  understand- 
ing between  her  youth  and  the  middle  age  of  her 
close  friend  is  handled  well. 


Harrison,    Henry     Sydnor. 
1922. 


Saint 


Teresa. 
F 13612 


Before  America  entered  the  war,  this  beautiful 
heiress  gained  control  of  a  steel  corporation  to  keep 
it  from  selling  munitions.  The  theme  is  the  con- 
flict between  her  will  and  that  of  a  young  patriot 
who  became  her  efficiency  expert  to  learn  how  to 
thwart  her  pacifism.  There  is  argument,  business 
detail,  and  finally  a  hand  to  hand  combat. 

Hext,  Harrington,  pseud.    Number  8-7.     1922. 

F 13778 

The  hero  is  a  man  possessing  the  knowledge  of 
secrets  which  enable  him  to  destroy  human  life 
and  property  without  detection,  and  he  endeavors 
to  regulate  world  affairs  according  to  his  own  ideas. 
A  thrilling  book. 

Heyer,  Georgette.  The  Black  Moth;  a  ro- 
mance of  the  xvm  century.  1921.  F  13773 
You  would  like  to  read  it  to  the  soft  accom- 
paniment of  Beethoven's  Minuet  because  there  is 
something  about  it  that  suits  the  daintiness  and  Old 
World  charm  of  the  music. — Literary  Review. 

Hurst,  S.  B.  H.    Coomer  AH.     1922.  F  23289 

A  story  without  any  love  interest.  There  are 
three  heroes,  a  Mohammedan  fanatic,  a  huge  negro, 
and  a  mysterious  white  man,  who  in  spite  of  their 
exotic  character  are  made  very  real.  The  descrip- 
tions are  vivid  and  colorful,  particularly  one  of 
Mecca  by  night  and  another  of  a  storm  in  the 
Indian  Ocean. 


Johnston,     Sir     Harry. 
1922. 


The     Veneerings. 
F  14395 


A  continuation  of  the  lives  of  Hamilton  Veneer- 
ing and  his  children  who  first  lived  in  Our  mutual 
friend  (1-'4O1).  It  is  a  leisurely  slow-moving  story 
with  small  plot  interest  but  -  some  English  politics. 
The  scene  shifts  between  Calais,  London,  and 
South  Africa. 

Kelland,  Clarence  Budington.  Conflict.  1922. 

F 24194 

Dorcas  Remalie,  a  wealthy  New  York  girl,  com- 
pelled on  the  death  if  her  father  to  live  in  a  little 
village  of  the  Maine  woods  with  her  guardian  who 
controls  her  money,  is  in  conflict  with  the  whole 
of  her  new  life.  A  clever  and  absorbing  story. 


Lagerlof,    Selma.      The    outcast. 


1922. 
F 14613 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


99 


There  is  a  mystical  quality  to  this  book,  as  there 
is  to  other  books  by  Miss  Lagerlof.  It  is  a  passion- 
ate indictment  of  war,  a  plea  that  war  shall  cease 
from  out  the  world,  that  men  shall  make  clean 
their  I  earts  from  the  sin  of  their  brothers'  blood. — 
Hildegarde  Hawthorne  in  New  York  Times. 

MacConnell,    Sarah    Warder.      One.      1922. 

F  24820 

A  conventional  story  of  married  life  among  those 
who  live  only  from  one  social  event  to  another. 
Alethea  marries  Frederick  Haviland  knowing  his 
philandering  habits  but  thinks  she  can  make  a  suc- 
cess of  marriage  in  spite  of  it.  Her  efforts  in  this 
direction  make  up  the  story. 

MacHarg,  William.     Peewee.     1922.    F  24848 

Peewee  is  a  little  street  boy  who  sells  newspapers 
in  Chicago's  crowded  loop  district.  William  Me- 
Harg  has  taken  his  interesting  boy  character  from 
real  life  and  written  an  appealing  story  about  him 
from  the  child's  point  of  view.

Matteson,  H.  H.  The  trap.  1921.  F  25001 
Adventure-loving  Billy  Mitch  resigns  his  position 
in  a  brokerage  office  against  everyone's  advice  and 
starts  for  Alaska  where  he  intends  to  establish  a 
fish  trap.  He  holds  to  his  resolution  "to-  stick 
till  the  crows  sing  soprano,"  and  has  a  great  many 
adventures  with  sea  pirates.

Paris,    John,    pseud.      Kimono.      1922. 

F 15983 

The  story  of  an  international  marriage.  The 
author  has  stripped  Japan  of  the  poetry  and  glamor 
commonly  woven  about  it.  He  has  been  horrified 
by  the  position  of  women  in  .Japan  and  especially 
by  the  cold-blooded  trade  in  vice  licensed  there. 

Perochon,  Ernest.  Nene.  1922.  F 16124 
It  is  a  sad  little  tale  of  a  girl  who  hired  her- 
self to  a  young  widower  and  who  gave  every  drop 
of  the  love  that  filled  her  big  peasant  heart  to  his 
two  babies,  and  then  had  to  give  them  up — and 
killed  herself  because  they  no  longer  loved  her. — 
Fanny  Butcher  in  Chicago  Sunday  Tribune. 


Phillpotts,  Eden. 
1921. 


Eudocia;  a  comedy  royal. 
F 16300 


The  love  story  of  a  soldier  who  in  the  midst  of 
the  effeminate  and  degenerate  Byzantine  Court 
stood  forth  a  man,  and  of  an  Empress  who  was  not 
only  an  intelligent  ruler,  but  a  warm-hearted  and 
very  beautiful  woman. — Literary  Review. 

Roberts,  Charles  G.  D.  In  the  morning  of 
time.  1919.  F 17359 

A  story  of  the  struggles  of  earliest  man  to  over- 
come the  difficulties  of  his  existence.  There  are 
tense  moments  when  fire  is  discovered  and  food 
cooked  for  the  first  time.  Numerous  conflicts  with 
colossal  beasts  of  the  jungle  are  thrillingly  related. 

Sabin,    Edwin    L.      Desert    dust.      1922. 

F 17583 

Early  railroad  building,  boom  towns,  and  a 
journey  across  the  desert  in  a  wagon  train  lend 
romance  and  adventure  to  this  story  of  the  taming 
of  the  wilderness. 

Scott,  Sir  Walter.  The  talisman,  a  tale  of 
the  crusaders;  with  reproductions  in 
colour  of  twelve  original  drawings  by 
Simon  Harmon  Vedder.  1920.  F  17845 


Seltzer,  Charles  Alden. 
erson.      1922.

100 


THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


The  hero  acquired  the  epithet  "Square  Deal" 
from  his  honesty  and  courage,  and  in  this  highly 
colored  western  tale  he  sustains  his  reputation  in

Smith,    Arthur    D.    Howden.      The    Doom 
trail.      1922.  F 18213 

A  swift  moving  tale  of  the  contest  in  America 
about  1725  between  the  French  and  English  to 
secure  the  Indian  fur  trade.  A  slight  love  story 
weaves  its  way  through  the  excitement  of  Indian 
wars  and  strange  adventure. 


Speare, 

1922. 


Dorothy. 


Dancers    in    the     dark. 
F  18286 


A  picture  of  the  modern  "prom  girl"  as  seen 
by  a  young  Smith  graduate.  Three  different  types 
or  girls,  Jerry,  the  excitment  eater,  Sarah,  the 
languid  vamp,  and  Joy,  the  little  New  England 
music  student,  have  an  apartment  together  in  Bos- 
ton. Their  carefree  existence  and  endless  good 
times  make  very  good  reading. 

Stribling,  T.  S.  Birthright.  1922.  F  18433 
Mr.  Stribling,  a  native  of  Tennessee,  presents 
the  problem  of  the  southern  negro  with  the  fidelity 
and  detail  which  make  his  novel  valuable  as  a 
sociological  study,  while  an  absorbing  plot  well 
worked  out  holds  the  attention  of  the  reader  not 
otherwise  interested  in  the  subject. 

Stringer,  Arthur.     The  prairie   child.      1922. 

F  18459 

Continuation  of  The  prairie  wife  (F  18429)  and 
The  prairie  mother  (F  18432),  written  in  the  same 
style  and  dealing  as  much  with  the  marital  troubles 
of  the  child's  parents  as  with  "Dinkie"  himself. 


Thayer,  Lee.     Q.  E.  D.     1922. 


F  27771 


Peter  Clancy,  red-haired  detective,  is  the  hero 
of  this  first-rate  mystery  story.  The  interest  _  in 
the  persons  involved  and  in  the  rapidly  moving 
events  is  well  sustained.  —  Literary  Review. 

Toohey,  John  Peter.  Fresh  every  hour; 
detailing  the  adventures,  comic  and  pa- 
thetic of  one  Jimmy  Martin,  purveyor  of 
publicity,  a  young  gentleman  possessing 


sublime  nerve,  whimsical  imagination, 
colossal  impudence,  and,  withal,  the  heart 
of  a  child.  1922.  F  27830 

Jimmy  is  press  agent  for  an  amusement  park 
in  Coney  Island  and  in  his  attempts  to  advertise 
meets  with  some  rare  experiences. 

Tully,  Jim.  Emmett  Lawler.  1922.  F  27902 
You  will  never  guess  what  Tully's  people  will 
do  next.  They  are  illogical,  inconsistent,  incalcul- 
able— in  a  word,  human  ;  men  and  not  marionettes 
moved  by  wires.  They  are  under  no  bonds  to  do 
the  graceful  thing.  They  do  disgraceful  things, 
without  any  particular  emotion  about  them. — Daily 
News. 

Underwood,  Edna  Worthley,  ed.  and  tr. 
Famous  stories  from  foreign  countries. 
1921.  F  27930 

Contents^      The    little    Blanchefleure,    by    R.    H. 


Wallace,  Edna  Kingsley.     The  stars  in  the

F 18913 

An  exquisite  tale  that  has  the  shimmering  grace

Williams,  Ben  Ames.    All  the  brothers  were 
valiant.      1919.  F 19519 

A  stirring  story  of  a  whaling  boat  in  the  South

— Booklist. 

Wolff,  William  Almon.     Sold  south.     1921. 

F  19723 

Here  is  a  novel  of  Latin  American  adventure 
with  a  singular  difference — it  might  almost  be  classed 
as  propaganda  for  a  better  understanding  of  our 
neighbors. — Literary  Review.

A  collection  of  biographies  of  men  and  women

difficult  to  find.  It  is  quite  contemporary. 


Willoughby,  George  A. 
for  beginners.     1921. 


Practical  electricity 
K  22871 


Fundamentals  set  forth  briefly  in  simple  language 
and  with  numerous  illustrations.  In  addition  to 
general  principles  there  are  chapters  on  battery- 
lighting  circuits,  fuses,  heater-circuit  troubles,  in- 
candescent lamps,  measurements,  and  the  avoidance 
of  danger. — New  York  Public  Library,  New  techni- 
cal books.

A  plea  for  the  preservation  of  the  classics  as

tems in  England.  The  teaching  of  English 
in  England.  1921. 

A  most  important  report  of  this  survey  which 
has  attracted  much  attention  in  England  and  U.  S. 
because  of  the  personnel  of  the  Committee,  its 
thorough  work  and  the  spirit  of  its  recommenda- 
tions.

116.3  1921/12 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Education.  Bulletin,  1921,  no. 
12.) 

An  interesting  study  analyzing  the  causes  of  the 
various  changes  in  educational  ideals  and  theories, 
and  determining  when,  where,  and  by  whom  they 
were  inaugurated.  Contains  a  chronological  list 
of  English  grammars  in  America  before  1800,  and 
an  excellent  bibliography.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate.    Those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  Room;  other 

collections  are  shelved  in  the  Civics  Department,    Music    Room   and   Thomas 

Hughes  Room  and  are  marked  accordingly.    *Ser.    denotes    serials 

which  do  not  circulate.

Laird,   John.     A   study  in   realism.      1920. 

L  13821 

The  author  enters  fearlessly  into  the  most  tech- 
nical and  involved  of  all  philosophical  problems  such 
as  those  relating  to  the  theory  of  perception.  He 
achieves  the  miracle  of  writing  _clearly,  pungently 
and  even  playfully,  about  these  things. — Literary  Re- 
view. 

More,  Paul  Elmer.  The  religion  of  Plato. 
1921.  L 14621 

(The  Greek  tradition,  vol.  I.) 


106 


THE   CHICAGO  PUBLIC   LIBRARY 


Mr.  More  regards  Platonism  as  the  characteris- 
tic expression  of  the  Hellenic  spirit  and  advocates 
the  Christian  tradition  based  upon  it  as  the  gospel 
by  which  alone  the  world  today  may  be  saved  from 
gross  materialism. — Nation. 

Psychology 

Hazlitt,    Henry.      The    way    to    will-power. 
1922.  L 14000 

The  author  explains  that  will  power  is  only  de- 
sire; he  tries  to  show  that  it  is  ideals  and  the  de- 
sire to  reach  them  that  lead  toward  the  ripht  path. 
He  discusses  habit,  thought  control,  concentration, 
moral  courage,  programs  of  work,  also  psychoanaly- 
sis in  a  common-sense  sort  of  way.

Pierce,  Frederick.  Our  unconscious  mind 
and  how  to  use  it.  1922.  L  13109 

The  author  tells  in  simple  untechnical  language 
the  relation  the  unconscious  part  of  our  personality 
plays  to  the  conscious  part.  He  believes  that  prac- 
tical application  can  be  made  of  this  knowledge 
and  that  there  are  very  few  problems  of  character 
or  personality  that  it  can  not  help.

ods for  the  church.  1921.  M  8574

vide generous  financial  support  for  the  work  of  the

M9192 

From  the  failure  of  existing  churches  Dr.  Holmes

1922.  M  9726 

The  rector  of  St.  Bartholomew's  in  New  York- 
believes  that  the  way  to  Christian  unity  in  this 
critical  time  is  to  be  found  through  fellowship 
rather  than  combination. 

Religious  Education 

Betts,  George  Herbert.  The  new  program 
of  religious  education.  1921.  M  8261 

(The  Abingdon  religious  education  texts.) 
A  comparison  of  the  two  currents  of  religious 
opinion,  the  one  laying  emphasis  on  evangelism  and 
the  other  on  training;  and  a  plea  for  the  adoption 
by  the  church  of  a  definite  program  of  religious 
education,  with  properly  trained  teachers  and  the 
cooperation  of  the  children's  parents. — Booklist.

religious  education.  1921.  M  8262 

(Judson  training  manuals  for  the  school  of  the 
church.)

matic and  play  instincts  in  home,  church  and  com-

Cram,  Ralph  Adams.  Towards  the  great 
peace.  1922.  L  15453 

(Dartmouth    Alumni    lectureships.) 

Contents:  Introduction. — A  world  at  the  cross- 
roads.—A  working  philosophy. — The  social  organ- 
ism.— The  industrial  problem. — -The  .political  or- 
ganization of  society. — The  function  of  education 
and  ert. — The  problem  of  organic  religion. — Per- 
sonal responsibility. 

An  earnest  plea  for  a  strong  philosophy  to  coun- 
teract the  moral  disintegration  following  the  Great 
War. 

Lindeman,  Eduard  C.  The  community;  an 
introduction  to  the  study  of  community 
leadership  and  organization.  1921. 

L  15318 

As  a  whole  the  book  affords  a  practicable  chart 
of  community  tendencies  and  organization  currents, 
and  every  worker  in  this  field  will  find  his  objec- 
tives made  easier  by  a  perusal  of  its  pages. — 
Survey. 

Lippmann,   Walter.      Public    opinion.      1922. 

L  15317 

Contents:  Introduction. — Approaches  to  the  world 
outside. — Stereotypes. — Interests. — The  making  of  a 
common  will. — The  imaje  of  democracy. — News- 
oaperS. — Organized  intelligence. 

A  remarkable  analytical  study  of  what  goes  to 
make  up  "public  opinion" — press,  politics,  etc., 
treated  psychologically  and  sociologically. 

Platt,  Charles.  The  psychology  of  social 
life;  a  materialistic  study  with  an  ideal- 
istic conclusion.  1922.  L 15385 

Presents  society  in  its  relation  to  the  individual 
and  considers  the  social  aspects  of  such  instincts 
as  habit,  fear  and  fighting,  sex,  convention,  and 
superstition.  Written  with  humor  and  against  a 
background  of  literary  and  historical  allusions,  it 
makes  an  interesting  diversion  for  the  general 
reader. 

Stoddard,  Lothrop.  The  revolt  against 
civilization;  the  menace  of  the  under  man. 
1922.  L 15355 

A  discussion  of  the  problems  of  social  revolu- 
tion in  the  light  of  biological  discoveries  confirmed 
and  amplified  by  investigations  in  other  fields  of 
science — especially  psychology — with  a  plan  for 
race  betterment  proposed  through  a  eugenic  pro- 
gram.

Boucke,     O.     Fred.      The    development     of

the  systems  to  four,  historism,  naturalism,  utili- 
tarianism, marginism.

logical  as  to  sequence,  and  the  figures  quoted  are 
the  latest  obtainable  (1921).

Chenery,  William  L.  Industry  and  human 
welfare.  1922.  L  16789 

(The    social    welfare    library.) 

Mr.  Chenery  discusses  the  effect  of  the  factory 
system  on  the  welfare  of  the  American  people. 
He  does  not  take  up  industrial  control,  but  rather 
the  effect  of  industry  on  the  worker  and  his  family, 
wages,  hours  of  labor  and  the  hazards  of  in- 
dustry.— Chicago  Daily  News. 

Harvard  Univers'ty.  Graduate  school  of 
business  administration.  Labor  terminol- 
ogy. 1921.  L  20353, 25 

(Bureau  of  Business  Research  bulletin.) 
A   first   attempt   to   give   definite   meanings   to  the 
terms    employed    by    labor    unions    in    their    disputes 


with  employers.     The  object  is  to  simplify  the  solu- 
tion   of   labor   problems   to    the   extent    of   avoiding 
verbal    misunderstandings    between    employers    and 
employees. — Book  Review  Digest. 

Lowe,  Boutelle  Ellsworth.  The  interna- 
tional protection  of  labor.  1921.  L  16787 
Gives  a  complete  survey  of  the  movement  for  the 
international  protection  of  labor  in  the  chronolog- 
ical order  of  events  up  to  1914,  including  its  rela- 
tion to  America  and  to  the  formation  of  the  Inter- 
national labor  organization  of  the  League  of  Na- 
tions. 

Pound,  Arthur.  The  iron  man  in  industry; 
an  outline  of  the  social  significance  of 
automatic  machinery.  1922.  L 16792 

Mr.  Pound  attempts  to  give  an  impartial  discussion 
of  the  industrial  problem,  taking  into  account  the 
effect  of  new  tools  and  methods  upon  human  na- 
ture and  the  social  order.  Though  not  scientific  in 
method  it  is  instructive  and  interesting.

.(The   Cutler  lectures.) 

He  analyzes  it  thoroughly,  showing  where  we  can 
interpret  it  more  fully  to  our  advantage  and  how; 
also  showing  how  it  has  preserved  our  liberties  in 
the  past  and  is  competent  to  achieve  the  same 
result  in  the  future.

Mayo,  Katherine.  Mounted  justice;  true 
stories  of  the  Pennsylvania  state  police. 
1922.  L  18424 

Contents:  The  Coughlin  case. — Between  two  days. 
— "Quick  as  God  will  let  you." — The  patent-leather 
shoes.- — The  hand-picked  job. — The  blazin?  moun- 
tain.— Advertising  mustard. — Under  the  yellow  flag. 
— Cut-beans  and  company. 

The  main  object  of  these  nine  authentic  narra- 
tives is  to  indicate  the  importance  of  the  organiza- 
tion for  the  welfare  and  protection  of  country-  folk. 
Through  former  writings  on  the  subject,  Miss  Mayo 
influenced  New  York  public  opinion  to  demand  a 
similar  organization.

1922.  L 18425

rural  sections  of  our  civilized  United  States.

Johnsen,  Julia  E.,  comp.  Selected  articles 
on  the  negro  problem.  1921.  L  16176 

(The    handbook    series.)

anced symposium. — The  Nation. 


Contents:  The  treaties  with  France,  1778. — The 
treaty  of  independence,  Paris,  1783. — Jay's  treaty, 
1794. — The  convention  of  peace,  commerce,  and 
navigation  with  France,  1800 — The  Louisiana  pur- 
chase, 1803. — The  treaty  of  Ghent,  1814.— The  con- 
vention of  1818  with  Great  Britain. — The  Florida 
purchase,  1819. — The  Webster-Ashburton  treaty, 
1842.— The  treaty  of  Guadalupe  Hidalgo,  1848. — The 
Perry  and  Harris  treaties  with  Japan,  1854  and 
1858. — The  Alaska  purchase,  1867. — The  treaty  .of 
Washington,  1871. — The  treaty  of  Paris  with  Spain, 
1898. — The  Panama  canal  treaties. 

A  source  book  for  general  United  States  history, 
as  well  as  an  aid  for  the  study  of  diplomatic  his- 
tory. Details  of  international  law  are  interwoven  in 
connection  with  the  negotiation  of  treaties  and 
their  results.

Contents:  The  method  of  philosophy. — The

In  the  Harvard  Law  Reporter,  Judge  Hand  calls

of  his  character,  his  acuteness  and  suppleness  of 
mind,  by  his  learning,  his  moderation,  and  his  sym- 
pathetic understanding  of  his  time,  has  won  an  un- 
rivaled esteem  wherever  he  is  known."

Abolishes  class  rooms,  recitation  periods,  lectures, 
daily  assignments,  etc.,  and  the  pupils  do  contract

in  the  social  laboratory  of  the  school  where  they  dis- 
cover their  native  faculties  through  experience.

ners of  wild  animals;  a  book  of  personal

Gives  many  proofs  of  the  amazing  reasoning  of

Tales  about  the  bear,  black  cat,  skunk,  rattle- 
snake, chipmunk,  coon,  bull  moose,  sea  otter,  fox, 
squirrel,  rabbit  and  birds.  They  are  unusual  and 
fascinating  narratives  of  actual  experiences,  show- 
ing keen  observation  of  wild  life. 

Thomson,  J.  Arthur.  The  haunts  of  life. 
1922.  K  21826 

Six  instructive  and  delightful  lectures  on  the 
shore  of  the  sea,  the  open  sea,  the  depths  of  the 
sea,  the  fresh  waters,  the  dry  land,  and  the  air, 
combining  with  the  detailed  knowledge  of  the  ex- 
pert the  rare  gift  of  imagination.

Farnham,  Dwight  T.  America  vs.  Europe 
in  industry;  a  comparison  of  industrial 
policies  and  methods  of  management. 
1921.  L  20730 

A  comparison  of  European  and  American  manu-, 
facturing  methods  showing  Europe's  progress  dur- 
ing the  war  and  her  industrial  advantages.  Mr. 
Farnham  has  visited  the  important  plants  of  Eng- 
land, France,  Italy  and  Germany  and  discussed 
conditions  with  foreign  capitalists  and  labor  leaders. 

Hungerford,  Edward.  The  romance  of  a 
great  store.  1922.  L  20626 

The  history  of  the  development  of  a  small  fancy- 
goods  store  in  New  York  from  its  founding  to  its 
present — now  the  famous  "Macy's" — describing  its 
internal  organization,  business  methods  and  spirit. 
Incidentally,  part  of  the  history  of  the  city  is  re- 
flected in  the  story. 

MacLachlan,  J.  E.  How  to  become  a  pri- 
vate secretary.  1920.  L  20379 

A  practical  handbook,  covering  a  widely  diver- 
gent field  of  work.

Gade,  Felix.  Collecting  antiques  for  pleasure 
and  profit;  the  narrative  of  twenty-five 
years  search  for  antique  furniture,  prints, 
china,  paintings  and  other  works  of  art, 
copiously  pictured  with  many  fine  ex- 
amples. 1922.  K  28956 

Every'  bit  of  this  narrative  is  personal  experi- 
ence and  full  of  sound  advice.  It  covers  a  vast  var- 
iety of  objects  and  treats  collecting  as  an  art,  a 
sport,  and  an  investment.

and  embroidery.  1922.  K  28797 

(The  collectors  series.) 

This  is  intended  for  the  small  buyer  and  collector. 
It  is  an  interestingly  written  book,  tracing  the  his- 
tory of  laces  and  telling  how  they  were  adopted 
from  one  country  to  another.

Music 

Beynon,  George  W.  Musical  presentation  of 
motion  pictures.  1921.  K  29540 

A  practical  handbook  for  the  guidance  of  leaders 
of  music  in  picture  theatres.  Tells  how  to  increase 
the  effectiveness  of  the  picture  by  appropriate  music 
properly  played,  with  suggestions  on  the  choice  and 
arrangement  of  a  musical  library. — Booklist. 

Coleman,  Satis  N.  Creative  music  for  chil- 
dren; a  plan  of  training  based  on  the 
natural  evolution  of  music,  including  the 
making  and  playing  of  instruments,  danc- 
ing—singing—poetry. 1922.  K  29569 

Farrell,  W.  J.     The  true-tone  violin.     1922. 

K 29979 

Written  by  an  Englishman  whose  thesis  is  that 
recently  made  violins  may  be  just  as  good  as  old 
ones.  He  says  that  a  violin  that  is  well  made  will 
have  as  good  tone  at  the  end  of  the  first  year  as 
after  many  years. 

Marafioti,  P.  Mario.  Caruso's  method  of 
voice  production;  the  scientific  culture  of 
the  voice.  1922.  K  29809 

Dr.  Marafioti  is  not  only  an  expert  on  the  physi- 
ology of  the  voice,  but  for  a  decade  and  a  half  he 
was  Caruso's  physician  and  was  enabled  to  make  a 
very  thorough  study  of  voice  production  by  Caruso 
himself. — Chicago  Evening  Post. 

Popley,    Herbert   A.      The    music    of   India. 

1921.  K  29612 

The  history  of  Hindu  music,  its  composition,  in- 
struments, etc.,  popularly  discussed. 

Recreation 

Rainwater,  Clarence  E.  The  play  move- 
ment in  the  United  States;  a  study  of 
community  recreation.  1922.  K  30107 

Professor  Rainwater  has  made  a  thorough  study 
of  the  recent  history  of  the  play  movement  and  in 
this  volume  presents  much  information  that  will 
be  equally  welcome  to  the  growing  number  of  prac- 
tical specialists  in  recreation  and  to  the  student 
of  social  organization. — Survey.

are  penned  with  an  artist's  love  of  color  and  are 
vivid  with  atmosphere.

Hardy,  Thomas.    CHEW,  SAMUEL  C.  Thomas 

Hardy,    poet   and   novelist.      1921.     J  7762 

(Bryn  Mawr  notes  and  monographs.) 

He   carries  the  Hardy  microcosm   in   his   head   as 

if   he  were  an  intelligent  concordance.      In  a  brief 

space  he  packs  more  trustworthy  information  about 

the  greatest  living  English  author  than  can  be  found 

anywhere  else  .  .  .  He  does  the  novels  justice,  and 

yet  he  knows  that  Hardy  is  a  superb  poet. — Carl  Van 

Doren  in  The  Nation.

J7763

novelists,  1900-1920.  1922.  J  7761 

Really  an  analysis  of  contemporaneous  American 
civilization  ...  If  all  the  novels  he  discusses  were 
destroyed,  a  reader  in  the  next  century  could  get 
from  Mr.  Van  Doren's  book  an  intelligent  view  of 
the  sort  of  people  we  Americans  are  at  this  hour. — 
Nation.

Poetry 

Colum,  Padraic,  ed.  Anthology  of  Irish 
verse.  1922.  E  14366 

The  most  representative  and  appealing  Irish  poe- 
try .  .  .  grouped  under  seven  heads,  such  as  poem.-! 
of  the  house,  the  road,  the  field,  the  fair  and 
the  fireside;  then  street  songs  and  country  side 
songs  ;  then  the  Celtic  world  and  the  realm  of  fairy 
poems  of  place  and  of  exile,  satires  and  laments  and 
so  on. — Daily  News. 

Johnson,  James  Weldon,  ed.  The  book  of 
American  negro  poetry,  with  an  essay  on 
the  negro's  creative  genius.  1922.  E  14365 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


He  tells  of  the  negro's  achievement  in  music, 
the  most  distinctive  American  contribution  to  the 
arts  in  the  last  two  or  three  decades,  and  he  tells 
Of  the  growth  of  a  race  soul  which  gives  to  the 
world  in  ever  larger  numbers  men  and  women  who 
count  in  the  emancipation  of  all  humanity. — 
Survey, 

•Le  Gallienne,  Richard,  ed.  The  Le  Gallienne 
book  of  English  verse,  with  an  introduc- 
tion. 1922.  E  14364 

(The  modern  books  of  verse.) 

The  aim  has  been  to  bring  together  as  much  of 
the  best  English  poetry  as  it  is  possible  to  include 
in  one  companionable  volume  .  .  .  The  selections 
beginning  as  far  back  as  the  eleventh  century  are 
brought  up  to  the  present  day. — Book  Review  Digest. 


Bates,    Katharine    Lee.      Yellow    clover;    a 
book  of  remembrance.     1922.  E  10631 

Poems    addressed    to    Katharine    Coman. 

Blunt,  Wilfrid  Scawen.     In  vinculis.      1889. 

E  14499 

Cobb,  Ann.     Kinfolks;   Kentucky  mountain 
rhymes.     1922.  E 14540 

Verse  of  the  Cumberland  mountaineers,  homely 
in  texture,  much  of  it  in  dialect,  with  a  tendency 
to  be  sentimental.  It  is,  however,  illuminant  of 
John  Fox  Jr.'s  country. — Post. 

De  La  Mare,  Walter.  Down-adown-derry; 
a  book  of  fairy  poems.  1922.  E  14582 
Light  fanciful  poems  of  fairies  and  witches.  Their 
lyrical  lilting  rhythms  fit  them  especially  for  read- 
ing aloud  either  to_  adults  or  children,  though  the 
imagery  and  phrasing  are  too  advanced  for  the 
youngest. 

— The  veil,  and  other  poems.     1922. 

E 14584 

These  verses  have,  for  the  most  part,  Mr.  De  La 
Mare's  usual  charm— the  unusual  subject,  the  sen- 
sitive choice  of  words,  the  hint  of  the  elusive  and 
the  mysterious. — Booklist. 

Doolittle,  Hilda,  (Mrs.  Richard  Aldington). 
Hymen,  by  H.  D.     1921.  E  10609 

In  widening  her  poetical  content,  H.  D.  has  lost 
nothing  of  her  beautiful,  astringent  manner  of  pres- 
entation. A  rare  artist,  indeed!  "Hymen"  proves 
her  once  more  among  the  most  important  of  recent 
American  poets. — Amy  Lowell,  in  Literary  Review. 


Freeman,  John, 
poems.     1922. 


Music;  lyrical  and  narrative 
E  14674 


Nature  inspires  most  of  the  poems,  which  are 
simple  and  melodious,  revealing  sensitiveness  of 
feeling  and  a  gentle  melancholy,  but  rarely  passion 
or  strong  emotion. — Book  Review  Digest. 

Gale,  Zona.    The  secret  way.    1921.    E  14691 

Here  is  a  reality  of  the  spirit  far  more  real  than 
the  alleged  reality  of  streets,  hovels  and  unwhole- 
some hearts.  Miss  Gale  is  not  unaware  of  the 
'hovels,  but  her  gaze  includes  stars  as  well  as 
shingles  .  .  .  She  is  one  of  the  pioneers  walking 
far  ahead  on  the  spirit's  splendid  "secret  way." — 
Julia  Cooley  Altrocchi  in  the  Post. 

Jones,  Edward  Smyth.  The  sylvan  cabin; 
a  centenary  ode  on  the  birth  of  Lincoln. 
1922.  E 11101 

Lovelace,  Richard.  Lucasta:  the  poems  of 
Richard  Lovelace,  esquire;  with  an  intro- 
ductory note  Ly  William  Lyon  Phelps. 
1921.  2v.  *E  7845 


McKay,  Claude.  Harlem  shadows;  with  an 
introduction  by  Max  Eastman.  1922. 

E  14852 

The  author  is  a  Jamaica  negro  who,  though  writ- 
ing of  New  York,  breathes  throughout  his  verses 
a  yearning  for  the  colorful  tropics.  The  poems  are 
lyrical  and  imaginative  and  not  written  in  dialect. 
A  few  voice  the  hopes  and  sorrows  of  his  race. 

Marks,  Jeannette.     Willow  pollen.     1921. 

E  14850 

The  poems  show  the  same  tendency  as  her  latest 
short  stories,  which  have  taken  the  form  of  alle- 
gory, for  many  of  them  are  highly  and  often  subtly 
symbolical. — Springfield  Republican. 

Marquis,  Don.  Sonnets  to  a  red-haired 
lady  (by  a  gentleman  with  a  blue  beard), 
and  famous  love  affairs.  1922.  E  14851 

Clever  light  verse,  a  mixture  of  up-to-date  slang 
and  real  poetry,  in  which  Blue  Beard  sounds  the 
praises  of  his  thirty  red-haired  wives.  The  second 
section  burlesques  fourteen  famous  love  affairs,  of 
Paris  and  Helen,  Tristam  and  Isolt,  Antony  and 
Cleopatra,  Othello  and  Desdemona,  etc. 

Mew,    Charlotte.      Saturday    market.      1921. 

E  14874 

Not  without  humor,  the  cumulative  effect  of  the: 
verses  is  a  high  seriousness,  an  intensified  pr 
jection  of  character. — Literary  Review. 

VTixter,  Florence   Kilpatrick.     Out   of  mis 


of  mist. 
E  14873 


"She  has  attempted  a  sonnet  sequence,  vers 
libre,  and  the  simple  rhyming  form  of  verse,  all 
with  considerable  success  .  .  .  The  book  bears  the 
mark  of  a  distinct  and  contributive  personality — 
and  parts  of  it  are  charming." — Margaret  V.  Saun- 
ders  in  The  Daily  News. 

Morse,  Katharine.     A  gate  of  cedar.     1922. 

E  14879 

A  collection  of  fresh  and  musical  poems.  There 
is  loveliness  in  all  of  them,  from  the  nature  poems 
and  verse  for  children  to  the  intimate  group  en- 
titled "For  any  lover,"  and  the  longer  ones  on 
events  of  New  England  history. 


Nathan,   Robert. 


Youth   grows   old.      1922. 
E  14895 


Reflections  on  the  passing  of  time.  The  individ- 
uality of  the  poet  is  characterized  by  "quiet  charm," 
"gentle  humor"  and  "exquisite  descriptions  of  na- 
ture." The  format  is  pleasing  and  tasteful. 


Petrarchino,   pseud.      The 
Petrarchino.     1921. 


sonneteering    of 
E 11409 


This  anonymous  Petrarchino  has  written  a  short 
cycle  of  love  sonnets  in  the  most  restrained  spirit  of 
the  classics,  warm  with  the  real  yearning  of  love 
to  celebrate  in  noble  form  its  passion. — Chicago  Sun- 
day Tribune. 

Porter,  Gene  Stratton.     The  fire  bird.    1922. 

E 11411 

A  narrative  of  an  Indian  maiden's  futile  love, 
possessing  beauty  and  rhythmic  force.  It  shows  the 
author  at  her  best  with  her  perfect  understanding 
of  nature  and  *her  sympathy  for  the  simple,  direct 
things  of  life. 

Pound,    Ezra.      Poems     1918-21,    including 
three   portraits  and   four   cantos.     1921. 

E 14937 

The  bulk  of  his  verse  is  an  attempt  to  escape 
either  by  paraphrasing  the  poetry  of  the  ancient 
or  medieval  time  in  which  he  fancies  that  he  would

THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


have  been  more  at  home,  or  by  execrating  his 
contemporaries  in  acid  little  epigrams. — Literary 
Review. 


Sandburg,  Carl. 
1922. 


Slabs  of  the  sunburnt  west. 
E  14985 


Thirty-odd  new  poems  from  Sandburg,  and  the 
critics  disagree  as  to  their  relative  merits  com- 
pared to  earlier  volumes.  But  they  include  "The 
windy  city"  which  has  been  regarded  as  the  greatest 
literary  interpretation  of  Chicago. 

Squire,  J.   C.     Poems;  second  series.     1921. 

E 15030 

J.  C.  Squire  follows  the  school  of  John  Freeman 
and  Edward  Thomas  a  while,  then  reaches  back  to 
older  classic  forms  and  forward  to  an  easy,  swing- 
ing individual  rhythm  of  his  own  in  embodying  his 
sensitive,  sincere,  and  romance-lit  reactions  to  life. — 
Bookman. 

Stevenson,  Robert  Louis.  Moral  emblems 
and  other  poems,  written  and  illustrated 
with  woodcuts;  first  printed  at  the  Davos 
press  by  Lloyd  Osbourne.  1921.  E  15033 
Poems  which  Stevenson  wrote  for  his  step- 
son Lloyd  Osbourne  and  illustrated  with  wood-block 
cuts  during  their  stay  in  Switzerland.  The  pref- 
ace tells  how  the  boy  printed  the  first  copies  on  a 
handpress  and  sold  them  to  the  guests  at  thfi 
watering  place. 

The  Theater  and  Stage 

Lewisohn,  Ludwig.  The  drama  and  the 
stage.  1922.  E 12529 

Contents:  The  new  dramaturgy. — The  American 
stage. — Contemporaries. — Art,  life  and  the  theatre. 

Brief  and  caustic  comments  on  all  phases  of  the 
theater.  He  is  as  hard  upon  the  critics  as  upon 
the  plays  they  review,  among  which  he  discusses 
Abraham  Lincoln,  Jane  Clegg,  A  bill  of  divorce- 
ment, Medea,  The  skin  game,  Mary  Rose,  The  cir- 
cle, and  many  others. 

Platt,  Agnes.  Practical  hints  on  training 
for  the  stage.  1921.  E  14181 

Thaler,  Alwin.  Shakspcre  to  Sheridan;  a 
book  about  the  theatre  of  yesterday  and 
to-day.  1922.  E  12903 

Showing  "how  much  the  theater  of  our  day  and 
hour  owes  to  that  of  yesterday." 

Walkley,  A.  B.  Pastiche  and  prejudice.    1921. 

E 12974 

A  book  of  what  the  New  York  Times  character- 
izes as  "amazing  essaylets,"  dealing  with  drama  and 
other  forms  of  art  from  the  Crocean  point  of  view.

Chapin,  Harold.  Three  one  act  plays:  It's 
the  poor  that  'elps  the  poor,  The  auto- 
crat of  the  coffee-stall,  Innocent  and 
Annabel.  1921.  E 13591 

(French's  acting  edition.) 

Among  recent  writers  of  short  plays  few  equal 
Harold  Chapin  in  subtlety  and  delicacy  of  charac- 
terization and  a  blending  of  humor  and  humanity. 
— Theatre  Arts  Magazine. 

Colby,  Gertrude  K.  The  conflict;  a  health 
masque  in  pantomime.  1921.  E  11941 

Dane,  Clemence,  pseud,  of  Winifred  Ashton. 
Will  Shakespeare;  an  invention  in  four 
acts.  1922.  E 13473 


The  drama  is  an  achievement,  for  its  poetry  is 
recognizable  and  its  Elizabethan  flavor  excellently 
well  done  .  .  .  but  one  has  a  right  to  regret  that 
Miss  Dane  has  .  .  .  had  to  distort  the  few  known 
facts  of  Shakespeare's  life  for  dramatic  effect. — 
John  Macy  in  the  Literary  Review. 

Dargan,  Olive  Tilford,  and  Peterson,  Fred- 
erick. The  flutter  of  the  goldleaf,  and 
other  plays.  1922.  E  11979 

Contents:  The  flutter  of  the  goldleaf,  by  Olive  T. 
Dargan  and  F.  Peterson. — The  journey,  by  Olive 
T.  Dargan. — Everychild,  by  F.  Peterson  and  Olive 
T.  Dargan. — Two  doctors  at  Akragas,  by  F.  Peter- 
son. 

Four  delicate  one-act  plays  of  psychoanalytic  in- 
terest. "The  journey"  seems  to  contain  the  great- 
est dramatic  value. 

Davis,  Owen.     The  detour;  a  play.     1922. 

E  11980 

The  theme  of  the  play  is  "a  supreme  picture  of 
farm  life,  of  mother  love  and  wifely  rebellion 
where  the  tragedy  is  two  fold  in  the  woman's 
yearning  for  self-expression  through  her  daughter 
and  in  the  daughter's  inadequacy  through  the  in- 
heritance of  her  father's  stodginess." 


Duran,  Leo,  tr. 


Plays  of  old  Japan.     1921. 
E  13675 


Contents:  The  daimyo. — -The  honor  of  Danzo.— 
The  horns. — The  hands  in  the  box. — Forsaken  love 
(a  poetical  comedy). 

In  the  process  of  reincarnation,  Mr.  Duran  has 
most  charmingly  preserved  the  Japanese  influence 
and  viewpoint;  but  we  appreciate  as  well  his  selec- 
tion of  situations  and  affairs  well  within  the  range  of 
our  own  occidental  understanding. — Post. 

Galsworthy,  John, 
acts.     1922. 


A  family  man;  in  three 
E  13767 


A  delightful  realistic  comedy  depicting  the  fam- 
ily life  of  the  middle-class  English  since  feminism 
has  swept  England.  Mr.  Galsworthy  has  set  out  on 
a  campaign  to  strip  off  romantic  pretense  and  show 
the  reality  underneath.  The  play  makes  good  read- 
ing and  is  adaptable  for  amateur  production. 

Glaspell,  Susan.  Inheritors;  a  play  in  three 
acts.  1921.  E 12234 

A  three-act  play  designed  to  emphasize  the  neces- 
sity of  developing  nationalism  of  the  highest  char- 
acter in  modern  America. — Booklist. 

Gregory,  Lady.  The  image,  and  other  plays. 
1922.  E  12277 

Contents:  The  image. — Hanrahan's  oath. — Shan- 
walla. — The  wrens. 

Four  plays  of  the  Abbey  Theatre.  They  are  folk 
plays,  tragic  and  humorous,  portraying  the  Irish 
peasant  characteristics. 

Masefield,  John.    The  tragedy  of  Nan.    1921. 

E 14093 

A  gift-book  edition  beautifully  illustrated  by  pho- 
tographs of  Broad  Oak  farm  and  the  Severn  river 
where  the  scenes  of  this  three-act  tragedy  are  laid. 
— Booklist. 

Peabody,  Josephine  Preston,  {Mrs.  L.  S. 
Marks).  Portrait  of  Mrs.  W.;  a  play  in 
three  acts  with  an  epilogue.  1922. 

E  14182 

A  play  based  on  the  life  of  Mary  Wollstonecraft, 
an  early  champion  of  women's  rights.  The  Boston 
Transcript  says,  "Mrs.  Marks  has  written  a  very- 
remarkable  play;  she  has  drawn  vividly  the  char- 
acter and  personality  of  an  extraordinarily  pictur- 
esque woman."

Orage,  A.    R.     Readers   and   writers    (1917-

Brief  literary  editorials  from  the  New  Age  (Lon-

broadly  conservative.

erick the  Great. — The  Rousseau  affair. — The  poetry 
of  Blake. — The  last  Elizabethan  (T.  L.  Beddoes). — 
Henri  Beyle. — Lady  Hester  Stanhope. — Mr.  Cree- 
vey. 

A  collection  of  biographies  and  essays  in  criticism, 
written  from  1905  to  1919.  They  are  interesting, 
apart  from  the  style  and  subject  matter,  in  their 
indication  of  the  author's  development. 


Tagore,  Rabindranath. 


Creative  unity.    1922. 
E5904 


Hi  O3Uf 

Contents:     Introduction. — The     poet's     religion. — 
ie    creative   ideal. — The    religion    of    the    forest. — 


Untermeyer,  Louis.     Heavens.     1922. 


E6164 

Delicious  parodies  by  this  versatile  writer.  He 
finds  in  the  first  group  several  varieties  of  heavens 
each  one  of  which  is  the  idea  of  bliss  held  by  some 
author  and  inhabited  by  him.  He  also  parodies 
Mr.  Einstein  in  some  very  clever  rhymes. 


Wilkinson,    Marguerite. 
Arcady.     1922. 


The    Dingbat    of 
E6299 


One  of  America's  well  known  poets  here  de- 
scribes a  journey  in  a  flat-bottomed  boat  down  the 
Oregon  river.  To  the  experiences  of  this  camping 
trip  she_  adds  various  incidents  from  similar  trips  by 
boat,  bicycle,  and  the  omnipresent  Ford,  both  iYi 
this  country  and  in  Europe. 

Wright,   Richardson.      Truly   rural.      1922. 

E6315 

Tired  of  city  life  the  Wrights  decided  to  go  out 
and  buy  an  old  country  place  and  metamorphose  it 
into  a  real  home  .  .  .  Mr.  Wright  tells  the  story 
of  their  enterprise  in  this  volume  with  a  wealth  of 
wholesome  humor  and  an  abundance  of  allusions 
to  art,  literature  and  philosophy.— NVw  York  Eve- 
ning Post.

Burroughs,  John.  My  boyhood;  with  a 
conclusion  by  his  son,  Julian  Burroughs. 
1922.  C 21064 

John  Burroughs'  e-xcellent  picture  of  American  life 
before  the  industrial  period  reveals  less  of  the  per- 
sonality of  its  author  than  do  the  letters  from  father 
to  son  contained  in  the  concluding  section. 

Byron,  George  Gordon,  Lord.  Lord  Byron's 
correspondence,  chiefly  with  Lady  Mel- 
bourne, Mr.  Hobhouse,  the  Hon.  Doug- 
las Kinnaird,  and  P.  B.  Shelley;  ed.  by 
John  Murray.  1922.  2v.  C  21083 

Mr.  Murray  has  done  well  to  publish  these  letters 
in  full.  He  has  allowed  them  to  speak  for  them- 
selves ...  A  just  and  careful  edition  of  letters 
that  add  to  our  knowledge  of  Byron  and  to  our 
admiration  for  his  writing. — Times  (London). 

Caruso,  Enrico.  FUCITO,  SALVATORE,  and 
BEYER,  BARNET  J.  Caruso  and  the  art  of 
singing,  including  Caruso's  vocal  exercises 
and  his  practical  advice  to  students  and 
teachers  of  singing.  1922.  C21186 

Sig.  Fucito,  for  five  years  coach  and  accompanist 
to  Enrico  Caruso,  has  here  written  a  sympathetic 
story  about  the  great  Italian  tenor,  first  as  a  tribute 
to  his  memory,  and  secondly  to  provide  an  authen- 
tic document  in  which  Caruso's  methods  of  tone 
production  and  breathing  might  be  permanently  re- 
corded.— Chicago  Daily  News. 

Elizabeth,  Queen  of  England.  CHAMBERLAIN, 
FREDERICK.  The  private  character  of  Queen 
Elizabeth.  1922.  C  21774 

A  special  book  for  the  special  student  of  history. 
Almost  half  of  it  is  occupied  with  medical  details 
proving  that  Queen  Elizabeth  -possessed  very  poor 
health,  and  the  rest  enumerates  and  refutes  the 
charges  against  her  relations  with  Leicester. 

Emerson,  Ralph  Waldo.  SNIDER,  DENTON  J. 
A  biography  of  Ralph  Waldo  Emerson, 
set  forth  as  his  life  essay.  1921.  C  21786 

Eucken,  Rudolf.  Rudolf  Eucken,  his  life, 
work  and  travels,  by  himself.  1922. 

C  21822 

A  careful  and  modest  record  of  an  intellectual 
life  composed  by  a  man  of  seventy-six.  .  .  The 
amiable  philosopher  reviews  the  images  of  this  old 
and  almost  pastoral  time,  which  he  looks  back  upon 
through  the  roaring  years  between  with  a  sort 
of  pensive  amazement. — Spectator. 

Harriman,  E.  H.  KENNAN,  GEORGE.  E.  H. 
Harriman;  a  biography.  1922.  2v. 

C 22330 

An  amazing  recital  of  epochal  events  in  the  his- 
tory of  American  big  business  ventures;  an  intimate 
and  interesting  account  of  a  vigorous,  resourceful 
and  unusual  personality. — Burton  Rascoe  in  the 
New  York  Tribune.

made  by  Mr.  C.  M.  Thompson.  1913. 

C 23004 

"Investigations  to  determine  the  exact  route

Laurier,  Sir  Wilfrid.  SKELTON,  OSCAR  DOUG- 
LAS. Life  and  letters  of  Sir  Wilfrid 
Laurier.  1922.  2v.  C  22878 

This  book  was  listed  in  the  June  Bulletin  (p.  96) 
with  an  incorrect  call  number. 

Lee,  Robert  E.  RILEY,  FRANKLIN  L.,  ed. 
General  Robert  E.  Lee  after  Appomattox. 
1922.  C  22921 

A  semi-centennial  memorial  presented  by  Wash- 
ington and  Lee  University,  revealing  the  Confed- 
erate general  from  every  angle.  It  discloses  the 
beauty  of  his  fine  character,  and  the  adoration  of 
the  South  for  "Marse  Robert"  is  readily  under- 
stood. 

Lenin,  Nikolai,  pseud,  of  V.  I.  Ulianov. 
LANDAU-ALDANOV,  M.  A.  Lenin;  authorized 
translation  from  the  French.  1922. 

C  22944 

A  study  of  the  communistic  revolution  and  of 
the  personality,  writings  and  ideas  of  Lenin.  The 
author  believes  that  no  one  has  done  Russia  more 
harm  than  Lenin  who  has  awakened  a  "lasting 
hatred  of  the  Russian  masses  for  everything  social- 
istic." 

Lincoln,  Abraham.  NEWKIRK,  GARRETT.  Lin- 
coln lessons  for  today.  1921.  C  23024 

Short  chapters  on  various  phases  of  Lincoln's 
life,  as  for  instance  his  heredity,  his  education,  his 
religion,  etc.,  emphasizing  the  lessons  to  be  drawn 
from  them. 

Menz'es,  Amy  Charlotte,  (Mrs.  Stuart  Men- 
zies).  Recollections  and  reflections,  by 
a  woman  of  no  importance.  1922.  C  23366 
Delightful  reminiscences  of  a  woman  who  has 
dwelt  among  many  people  who  are  of  importance 
(or  at  least  prominence)  in  English  and  continental 
circles. 

Poe,  Edgar  Allan.  The  letters  from  George 
W.  Eveleth  to  Edgar  Allan  Poe,  ed.  by 
Thomas  Ollive  Mabbott.  1922.  C  23813 

Roland,  Madame.  BLASHFIELD,  EVANGELINE 
WILBOUR,  (Mrs.  E.  H.  Blashfield).  Manon 
Phlipon  Roland;  early  years.  1922. 

C  23963 

"These  memoirs  furnish  an  unrivalled  opportun- 
ity for  studying  a  mind  in  the  making,"  states  the 
New  Republic.  They  carry  the  life  of  the  heroine 
of  the  French  Revolution  up  to  that  great  event 
with  ease  and  charm,  and  reflect  the  spirit  of  in- 
tellectual France  during  the  period. 

Sabsovich,  H.  L.  SABSOVICH,  KATHARINE. 
Adventures  in  idealism;  a  personal  rec- 
ord of  the  life  of  Professor  Sabsovich. 
1922.  C  24049 

One  of  the  foremost  figures  in  the  field  of  Jew- 
ish social  service,  a  man  endowed  with  rare  ideal- 
ism and  exceptional  devotion  to  his  race.  Written 
by  his  wife  who  sacrificed  herself  to  the  lifework 
of  her  husband. 

Schiff,  Jacob  Henry.  ADLER,  CYRUS.  Jacob 
Henry  Schiff;  a  biographical  sketch.  1921. 

C  24130 

Tagore,  Rabindranath.  THOMPSON,  E.  J. 
Rabindranath  Tagore,  his  life  and  work. 
1921.  C  24478 


A  chronological  account  of  her  career  and  its 
successes,  including  many  anecdotes  of  interesting 
persons  and  places. 

William,  Crown  Prince  of  the  German  Em- 
pire and  of  Prussia.  Memoirs  of  the  Crown 
Prince  of  Germany.  1922.  C  24872 

Lively  and  entertaining,  this  work  contains  little 
of  the  technique  of  war  but  a  great  deal  of  the 
personal  element. 


HISTORY 

Barnes,  Harry  "Elmer.  The  social  history 
of  the  western  world;  an  outline  syllabus. 
1921.  A  2368 

Belloc,  Hilaire.     The  Jews.     1922.       A  2681 

Mr.   Belloc  sums  up  the  history  of  Jews  in  their 
adopted    homes   as   a   "tragic   cycle,"   and   warns  us 
that    eventual   persecution   may    follow    the    growing 
tension  evidenced  by  the  growth  of  a  "whispering 
campaign   of  hostility  in   our  own  country. 

Boak,  Arthur  E.  R.  A  history  of  Rome  to 
565  A.  D.  1921.  A  2785 

The  Roman  contributions  to  government  and  law 
receive  fullest  treatment,  but  ail  phases  of  Roman 
life  and  thought  are  presented  in  a  direct,  effec- 
tive manner.  The  book  combines  concreteness  and 
precision  of  statement  with  continuity  of  thought 
and  development. — Publishers'  note. 

Krey,  August  C.,  ed.  and  tr.  The  first  cru- 
sade; the  accounts  of  eye-witnesses  and 
participants.  1921.  A  6504 

Excellent  translations  of  the  four  main  Latin 
chronicles  of  this  period  remarkably  free  from 
anachronisms.

Tetrazzini,  Mme.  Luisa. 
1922. 


My  life   of  song. 
C 24508 


A  short  story  of  civilization  from  brute  man  to 
the  present  state.  The  author  announces  his  dis- 
agreement with  G.  B.  Shaw  and  H.  G.  Wells,  main- 
taining that  the  world  has  made  more  progress  dur- 
ing the  last  ten  thousand  years  than  it  made  during 
the  first  milliqn  years  of  its  existence. 

Ireland 

Fitzpatrick,  Benedict.  Ireland  and  the  mak- 
ing of  Britain,  with  map  of  medieval 
Ireland  and  Britain.  1922.  A 10252 

A  study  of  the  part  played  by  Irish  scholars  and 
missionaries  in  Christianizing  and  civilizing  Saxon 
England,  which  assembles  evidence  from  authori- 
tative sources.  An  intensely  partisan  work. 

Hackett,  Francis.  The  story  of  the  Irish 
nation.  1922.  A  5524 

A  snap-shot  view  of  the  Irish  nation  from  pre- 
historic times  to  the  present  day.  It  gives  a  fairly 
accurate  idea  of  Irish  history  for  those  who  want 
a  brief  account,  while  it  makes  a  good  outline  for 
those  who  wish  to  consult  authorities  for  various 
periods.

110268

A  5523 

A  record  of  recent  political  events  in  Ireland  as 
seen  by  a  participant  who  is  both  a  keen  observer 
and  a  clear  thinker.

Bryce,  James,  Viscount.  The  study  of

A  brief  study  in  the  author's  clear  style.  Pre-

Chapman,  Charles  E.  A  history  of  Cali- 
fornia: the  Spanish  period.  1921.  B  2876 
This  history  of  Spanish  California  during  the 
Mexican  war  is  the  result  of  thirteen  years  of 
investigation,  and  aims  to  show  the  history  of  Cali- 
fornia in  relation  to  the  whole  country  rather  than 
as  a  matter  of  purely  local  interest.
