Adams,     Adeline.       The     Amouretta     land- 
scape and  other  stories.     1922.        F  10126 

Contents:  The  Amouretta  landscape. — Bits  of 
clay. — The  young  lady  in  blue. — "C'est  une  taupe." 
— Their  appointed  rounds. — Speaking  of  angels. — 
The  marquis  goes  donkey-riding. — The  face  called 
forgiveness.— The  artist's  birthday. 

An  unusually  interesting  collection  of  short 
sketches.  Most  of  the  scenes  are  laid  in  the  art 
colony  of  New  York,  and  sculptors  and  models  play 
important  parts  in  the  stories. 


Alington,     Cyril. 
1922. 


Through     the     shadows. 
F  10494 


Fanny  Butcher,  in  the  Chicago  Tribune,  calls 
this  comedy  of  errors  "a  lighter-than-air  craft 
which  bobbles  around  in  the  breeze  of  a  week-end 
party  at  an  English  country  house."  Its  author  is 
the  personal  religious  adviser  of  the  King  of  Eng- 
land. 

Ames,  Joseph  B.     Shoe-Bar  Stratton.    1922. 

F 10526 

A  good  story  of  the  cattle  country.  The  owner 
of  Shoe-Bar  Stratton  returns  from  the  war  to  find 
his  ranch  in  the  hands  of  an  attractive  girl.  How 
he  hires  out  on  his  own  ranch,  defeats  the  plots  of 
a  gang  of  rustlers  and  wins  the  affection  of  the  girl 
make  an  absorbing  tale. 


Aminoff,    Leonie.      Torchlight. 


1921. 

F  10528 


A  novel  of  the  life  and  times  of  Napoleon  which 
(in  the  words  of  the  New  York  Times)  "makes  a 
vital  and  colorful  picture  of  the  time,  the  events 
and  the  people  of  the  decade  which  it  covers." 

Anker,  Jens,  pseud,  of  Robert  Hansen.    Two 
dead  men,   tr.   from  the   Danish.     1922. 

F  22694 


A  finely  written  detective  tale  of  the  murder- 
mystery  type.  The  setting  is  Copenhagen  and  the 
characterization  is  well  done. 

Babcock,  Bernie.     The  coming  of  the  King. 
1921.  F 10767 

A  story  of  the  life  of  Christ,  emphasizing  his 
human  side. 

Bay,     J.     Christian.       The     chalice     of     the 
chipped  ruby.     1922.  F 20301 

The  ruby  with  the  unaccountable  flaw  is  one  of 
several  precious  stones  ornamenting  a  sacred  chal- 
ice, and  around  it  the  author  has  woven  a  series  of 
romantic  episodes. 

Beck,  L.  Adams.     The  ninth  vibration,  and 
other   stories.      1922.  F  20336 

Contents:  The  ninth  vibration. — The  interpreter. 
— The  incomparable  lady. — The  hatred  of  the  queen. 
— Fire  of  beauty. — The  building  of  the  Taj  Mahal. 
— "How  great  is  the  glory  of  Kwannon!" — "The 
round-faced  beauty." 

A  collection  of  alluring  and  romantic  tales  em- 
bodying the  mystic  beauty  and  colorful  atmosphere 
of  the  Far  East.  The  author  is  an  English  trav- 
eler and  scholar  well  versed  in  oriental  lore. 

Benet,    William    Rose.      The    first    person 
singular.     1922.  F  20382 

A  fanciful  first  novel  from  the  pen  of  an  imag- 
inative poet.  The  plot  begins  in  front  of  the  New 
York  Public  Library,  the  chief  interest  centering 
in  a  mistaken  identity  which  is  solved  in  the  last 
chapter. 

Bennett,    John.      Madame    Margot;    a    gro- 
tesque   legend    of   old   Charleston.     1921. 

F 20386 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


129 


Grotesque  indeed  it  is,  but  very  beautifully  and 
symbolically  told,  as  a  legend  should  be.  Mme.  Mar- 
got,  the  loveliest  of  a|l  Charleston's  lovely  crea- 
tures, and  her  traffic  with  the  devil  that  she  might 
save  her  daughter  Gabrielle  from  love — that  is  the 
legend. — Chicago  Sunday  Tribune. 

Blackwood,  Algernon.     The  bright  messen- 
ger.    1922.  F  20527 

A  psychic  story  in  which  dual  personality  and  its 
possibilities  form  the  theme.  Julius  La  Vallon 
(F  11230)  is  again  the  leading  figure  and  it  is  the 
controlling  of  his  secondary  self  by  Dr.  Ellery,  a 
•physician  interested  in  psychic  tendencies,  that 
makes  the  story. 

Bone,    David   W.     Broken    stowage.      1922. 

F 20631 

A  collection  of  sketches,  essayettes  and  tales  of 
sea-faring  life,  full  of  magic  effect.  They  are  all 
remarkably  different  but  have  the  same  charm  and 
vigor  that  made  The  brassbounder  (F  20630)  a 
masterpiece  of  its  kind. 

Bridges,  Victor.      Greensea   Island;   a   mys- 
tery of  the  Essex  coast.     1922.        F  20721 

It  is  the  manner  in  which  the  author  tells  his 
story,  the  little  twists  in  the  solution  of  the  mys- 
tery, and  the  lovable  character  traits,  which  make 
the  reader  follow  the  story  to  the  end. — New  York 
Times. 

Brighouse,   Harold.     Hepplestall's.      1922. 

F  20727 

A  strong  novel  with  splendid  characterization,  de- 
picting the  human  side  of  the  growth  of  a  great  in- 
dustry. A  long  feud  between  the  mill  owners  and 
a  family  of  the  workers  is  symbolic  of  the  strug- 
gle between  labor  and  capital. 

Buchholtz,  Johannes.     Egholm  and  his  God; 
tr.   from  the   Danish.     1922.  F  20772 

A  gloomy  tale  of  a  religious  fanatic  who  cruelly 
sacrifices  the  necessities  of  his  family  to  the  de- 
mands of  a  grasping  Brotherhood.  The  Literary  Re- 
view calls  Egholm's  God  a  unique  contribution  to 
theology  and  Egholm  himself  an  equally  rare  acces- 
sion to  the  human  family. 

Cade,   Coulson.     The  Cornish  penny.     1922. 

F 11546 

Robin,  brought  up  in  a  sleepy  village  in  Corn- 
wall, when  on  his  way  to  London,  meets  a  stranger 
who  invites  him  to  visit  his  luxurious  home.  Robin 
has  the  time  of  his  life  with  this  stranger,  who  is 
known  as  an  art  connoisseur  but  turns  out  to  be  a 
gentleman  robber. 

Carswell,    Catherine.      The    camomile;    an 
invention.      1922.  F 11666 

The  camomile  typifies  the  heroine's  urge  to 
write,  which  she  strives  to  suppress  by  pursuing 
a  musical  career.  The  story  is  told  through  her 
own  journal,  and  the  charm  of  the  book  lies  in  the 
delightful  style  in  which  her  meditations  find  ex- 
pression. 

Chekhov,     Anton.       The     cook's     wedding, 
and  other  stories.     1922.  F 11932 

Contents:  The  cook's  wedding. — Sleepy. — Chil- 
dren.— The  runaway. — Grisha.-^Oysters. — Home. — A 
classical  student. — Vanka. — An  incident. — A  day  in 
the  country. — Boys. — Shrove  Tuesday. — The  old 
house. — In  passion  week. — Whitebrow.- — -Kashtanka. 
— A  chameleon. — The  dependents. — Who  was  to 
blame? — The  bird  market. — An  adventure. — The 
fish. — Art. — The  Swedish  match. 

He  is  faithful  to  life  like  no  other  writer  ;  there- 
fore his  stories  are  not  gay,  they  are  full  of  frus- 
trated hopes  and  wasted  destinies. — New  Republic. 


Chown,   Alice   A.     The   stairway.      1921. 

F  11948 

In  a  very  readable  journal,  extending  from  1906 
to  1919,  is  set  forth  the  gradual  emancipation  of  a 
modern,  educated  woman  from  the  shackles  of  con- 
vention and  current  prejudices. — Survey. 

Cobb,    Irvin    S.      Sundry    accounts.       1922. 

F  21270 

Contents:  Darkness. — The  eater-cornered  sex.— A 
short  natural  history. — It  could  happen  again  to- 
morrow.— The  ravelin'  wolf. — "Worth  10,000."— 
Mr.  Lobel's  apoplexy. — Alas,  the  poor  Whiffletit!— 
Plentiful  Valley. — A  tale  of  wet  days. 

As  a  narrator  of  happenings  in  small  Kentucky 
towns,  as  a  delineator  of  character  and  characters 
in  that  setting,  Mr.  Cobb  cannot  be  excelled.  He 
writes  with  rare  insight  and  with  a  warm  human 
sympathy. — Literary  Review. 

Connell,  Richard.  The  sin  of  Monsieur 
Pettipon,  and  other  humorous  tales. 
1922.  F  21340 

Contents:  The  sin  of  Monsieur  Pettipon. — Mr.  Pot- 
tle and  the  South  Sea  cannibals. — Mr.  Pottle  and 
culture. — Mr.  Pottle  and  the  one  man  dog. — Mr. 
Pottle  and  pageantry. — The  cage  man. — Where  is 
the  Tropic  of  Capricorn? — Mr.  Braddy's  bottle. — 
Gretna  Greenhorns. — Terrible  Epps. — Honor  among 
sportsmen. — The  $25,000  jaw. 

All  sorts  of  people  are  written  about  and  sym- 
pathetically drawn.  Monsieur  Pettipon,  ah  artist 
in  bed  making,  Mr.  Pottle  and  his  wife  who  seek 
culture,  little  Peter  Mullaney  who  wanted  to  be  a 
cop — all  real  living  people  and  very  enjoyable. 


Connolly,  James    B.     Tide   rips. 


thens. 

A   collection  of   short    sea   stories,    most   of   which

Davey,  Norman.  Guinea  girl;  a  melodrama 
in  three  acts,  together  with  the  incidental 
music,  here  presented  for  the  entertain- 
ment of  the  curious.  1922.  F  12287 
A  sensational  story  of  Monte  Carlo  and  the  demi 
monde,  of  love  and  fortune-seeking,  told  with  the 
charm  of  style  and  delicate  irony,  used  by  the  au- 
thor in  his  more  episodic  Pilgrim  of  a  smile 
(F  12286). 

Dawson-Scott,  C.  A.     The  haunting.     1922. 

F  21500 

An  absorbing  analysis  of  the  mental  conflict  of  a 
man  who  killed  his  brother,  because  h;.-  had  de- 
manded an  unfair  division  of  the  family  fortune. 
His  life  is  spent  in  justifying  the  hallucinations 
with  which  he  is  beset. 

Deland,    Margaret.      The    vehement    flame. 
1922.  F  21539 

The  title  signifies  the  jealousy  of  a  wife  for  her 
husband  twenty  years  younger  than  herself.  It  is 
an  unusual  story  for  Mrs.  Deland  and  not  a  pleas- 
ant one. 

Dell,   Ethel  M.     The  odds,  and  other  stor- 
ies.    1922.  F 21 548 

Contents:  The  odds. — Without  prejudice. — Her 
own  free  will. — The  consolation  prize. — Her  free- 
dom.— Death's  property. — The  sacrifice. 

A  collection  of  stories  of  the  traditional  Dell 
type  with  an  Australian  setting. 


Dodd,     Lee     Wilson. 
1922. 


Lilia     Chenoworth. 
F 21643 


130 


THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


A  powerful  swift-moving  story  filled  with  deli- 
cate characterization.  Lilia  herself  and  Dunster 
Thorpe  are  every  whit  as  good  as  Mark  Sabre.  This 
is  a  carefully  written  novel  that  is  emotionally 
moving  and  technically  satisfying. — Bookman. 

Dodge,   Louis.     Nancy;  her  life  and  death. 
1921.  F  12544 

To  one  who,  like  those  to  whom  the  tale  is  dedi- 
cated, has  "loved  and  lost  a  dog"  .  .  .  this  will  have 
a  peculiar  and  poignant  appeal.  It  pictures  the  fas- 
cinating and  ingratiating  ways  of  Nancy,  only  to 
make  one  more  truly  feel  the  void  her  pitiful  death 
created  in  the  heart  of  the  author. — Chicago  Tri- 
bune. 

Dunn,    T-    Allan.      Rimrock    trail.      1922. 

F 12618 

The  interest  centers  about  an  Arizona  ranch  and 
its  three  cowboy-owners.  Molly  Casey  an  orphan 
of  fifteen  is  adopted  by  the  three  and  sent  East 
to  be  educated.  Through  the  discovery  of  a  mine 
she  becomes  rich,  but  returns  to  marry  one  of  the 
Westerners. 

Durand,    Ralph.      The    mind    healer.      1921. 

F 21816 

A  rollicking  comedy  full  of  ingenious  situations 
and  lively  humor. 

Escholier,    Raymond.      The    illusion    (Dan- 
sons  la  trompeuse).     1922.  F  12773 

A  delicate  sketch  of  a  little  old  woman  who  fills 
her  life  with  deceit  and  falsehood  because  she 
wants  to  play  the  comedy  of  happiness.  The  many 
subterfuges  are  invented  to  cover  the  lack  of  love, 
the  loss  of  fortune,  and  the  first  onslaught  of  age. 

Flaubert,  Gustave.  Sentimental  education; 
the  story  of  a  young  man.  1922.  F  22122 
The  evolution  in  sentiment  of  a  young  man  from 
the  provinces,  who  squanders  his  fortune  and  is 
deceived  and  made  sport  of  until  he  in  turn  be- 
comes the  deceiver  and  hypocrite.  His  faithful  love 
for  a  married  woman  is  the  only  "true"  thing  in  his 
life. 

Ford,  Sewell.     Inez  and  Trilby  May.     1921. 

F  22207 

_  Relates  in  Ford  slang  the  adventuring  of  two 
girls.  While  searching  for  the  rich  uncle  of  Inez 
they  earn  a  living  in  various  ways.  The  uncle  does 
not  take  to  them  until  a  plot  to  cheat  him  out  of  a 
large  sum  of  money  is  discovered  by  Trilby  May. 

Frankau,  Gilbert.     The  love-story  of  Aliette 
Brunton.      1922.  F  22241 

Mr.  Frankau  presents  a  brief  for  a  revisal  of  the 
narrow  divorce  laws  of  England.  His  characters 
are  clean  cut  splendid  English  people  of  the  upper 
class  with  a  strong  adherence  to  the  English  code 
of  "playing  the  game." 

Fyfe,  Hamilton.     The  widow's  cruse.     1922. 

F  22312 

A  clever  study  of  a  widow  who  develops  a  sud- 
den love  for  her  husband  and  deep  appreciation  for 
his  post  mortem  fame  after  he  has  ceased  to  need 
either.  Entertainingly  written,  full  of  humor, 
charm  and  originality. 


Galsworthy,  John. 


The  Forsyte  saga.    1922. 
F  22352 


Combines  as  one  continuous  narrative  "The  man 
of  property,"  "The  Indian  summer  of  a  Forsyte." 
"In  chancery,"  "Awakening"  and  'To  let."  Wil- 
liam Lyon  Phelps  in  the  New  York  Times  consid- 
ers this  work  a  positive,  powerful  and  permanent 
contribution  to  the  social  history  and  literature  of 
England. 

Glasgow,    Ellen.      One    man    in    his    time. 
1922.  F  22460 


Gideon  Vetch  has  finally  arisen  from  a  circus  tent 
to  residp  in  the  stately  executive  mansion  of  Vir- 
ginia. The  story  deals  with  his  life  among  the  old 
conservative  families  and  the  way  he  overcame  their 
prejudices.  A  romance  is  woven  around  his  daugh- 
ter Betty. 

Graham,   R.   B.   Cunninghame.     The   Ipane. 
1899.  F  22612 

Contents:  The  loane. — Un  pelado. — Un  Ange'.ito. 
The  lazo. — The  bolas. — SS.  Atlas.— Bristol  fashion. 
— Tan~er  !•>.  BHnca. — A  survival. — Heather  Jock. — 
Salvagia. — Snaekoll's  saga. — With  the  northwest 
wind. — Niggers. — At  Torfaieh.

Contents:  Success. — The  Gualichu  tree. — Los 
Se^uidores. — Un  infeliz. — From  the  mouth  of  the

Haggard,  H.  Rider.     The  virgin  of  the  sun. 
1922.  F 13415 

Hubert  Hastings  and  Kari  of  the  Incas,  flee  from 
England  and  after  wild  adventures,  arrive  in  the 
land  of  the  Incas  where  they  each  become  king  of 
a  mighty  nation.  A  fascinating  tale  of  romance, 
adventure  and  mysticism. 


Hallet,    Richard    Matthews, 
of  the   Fools.     1922. 


The 


Canyon 
F  22690 


The  usual  ingredients  of  a  story  of  its  kind  are 
not  lacking,  but  so  novel  is  Mr.  Hallet's  method 
of  combining  these  elements  that  the  result  is  sur- 
prising and  amusing. — Boston  Transcript. 

Harris,    Corra.      The    eyes    of    love.      1922. 

F  13615 

It  is  the  struggle  of  Martin  Puckle,  a  dried  up 
old  lawyer,  and  of  Wyndam  Cutmore  and  Betty  his 
wife,  who  live  in  a  small  southern  town.  The 
story  is  made  up  mostly  of  the  clever  things  the 
women  find  to  say  about  the  stupid  men. 


Henshaw,  Nevil. 
Trouve.     1922. 


The   inheritance   of  Jean 
F 13692 


A  quaint  story  full  of  charm.  A  young  American, 
disinherited  by  his  grandfather,  was  befriended  by 
two  hunters  in  the  woods  and  marshes  of  Louisi- 
ana. 


Hopkins,    William    John, 
sparm  at  that!     1922.

blows!    and 
F  14079 


A  whaling  cruise  half  a  century  ago  contained 
many  exciting  experiences  as  described  by  Timmie 
Taycox  in  this  fascinating  tale  of  the  sea. 


Hough, 

1922. 


Emerson. 


The     covered     wagon. 
F  14117 


Story  of  the  emigrants  crossing  from  Missouri 
to  Oregon  in  the  summer  of  1848.  The  hardships 
of  the  trail,  fights  with  the  Indians,  and  the  jealous- 
ies between  the  leaders  are  all  vividly  told.  The 
news  of  the  finding  of  gold  in  California  comes  as 
the  caravan  nears  the  Oregon  line. 

The  house  on  Charles  street.     1921.    F  14133 

A  young  American  war  worker  gets  in  touch  with 
English  politics,  and  although  there  is  a  slight  love 
affair,  the  story  is  more  of  a  character  study  of  the 
English  people  during  the  distressing  period. 


Hudson,  Jay  William. 


Abbe  Pierre.     1922. 
F  23243 


Through  the  fond,  reflective  eyes  of  the  gentle  old 
Abbe  we  look  upon  the  life  of  a  quiet  Gascon  vil- 
lage. It  is  the  village,  or  perhaps  the  fair  Gascon 
land  itself,  which  is  the  real  protagonist,  and  he 
her  tender  lover,  jeatous  too  of  encroaching  modern- 
ity yet  wistfully  anxious  to  be  open  minded. 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


Jordan,    Elizabeth.     The    blue   circle.      1922. 

F 14419 

A  mystery  tale  that  develops  its  tension  with 
considerable  skill  and  preserves  its  suspense  to  the 
end.  It  produces  its  effects  by  suggested  dangers 
rather  than  by  accomplished  deeds  of  violence  and 
creates  an  atmosphere  that  has  sufficient  of  the 
normal  to  emphasize  the  extraordinary. — Literary 
Rei'iew. 

Kenyan,  Camilla.  Fortune  at  Bandy's  Flat. 
1921.  F 24209 

Sally  (delightfully  eighteen)  takes  her  little  bro- 
ther to  a  small  mountain  village  to  recuperate  from 
scarlet  fever.  An  attractive  civil  engineer  and  an 
attractive  bad  man  help  to  make  things  happen. 
A  train  holdup,  a  stolen  letter,  and  mysterious  ru- 
mors keep  things  interesting  to  the  last. 

Kickham,  Charles  J.  Knocknagow;  or, 
The  homes  of  Tipperary.  1921.  F  24237 

Kinross,  Albert.  The  truth  about  Vig- 
nolles.  1922.  F  24258 

Contents:  The  truth  about  Vignolles. — Forbid- 
den fruit. — An  honest  living. — Eve  Darner. — Behind 
the  lines. — The  profiteer. 

A  group  of  adventure  stories  in  a  well  contrived 
combination  of  romantic  and  realistic  treatment.  Vig- 
nolles is  an  eminently  likable  person,  whom  Kipling 
or  Conrad  mipht  have  presented,  and  his  exploits, 
martial,  amorous  or  commercial,  are  diverse  and 
diverting. 


Larminie,  Margaret   Rivers. 


-The    white   peacock.      1921. 


Search.     1922. 
F 24407 

The  novel  is  well  written  and  full  of  little  touches 
of  significant  observation.  Furthermore  the  char- 
acters are  psychologically — one  might  almost  say 
pathologically — true  to  type,  and  the  narrative  has 
in  it  much  of  truth  and  of  beauty. — Saturday  Re- 
view. 

Lawrence,    D.    H.     Aaron's    rod.      1922. 

F 24417 

A  beautifully  written  story  in  Lawrence's  usual 
poetic  style.  There  is  very  little  plot,  but  the 
characters  are  well  _drawn  and  the  book  is  full  of 
ideas  and  philosophical  discussions  about  life,  mar- 
riage, love  and  other  problems  of  modern  life. 

F 24421 

An  exceedingly  vivid  portrayal  of  character  de- 
velopment, with  its  setting  in  the  collier  district  of 
England.  A  wealth  of  colorful  description  of  a 
territory  he  knows  so  well,  and  altogether  Lawrence 
at  his  best. 

Lynde,    Francis.      Pirates'    hope.      1922. 

F  24770 

A  cracking  good  story  from  beginning  to  end 
...  A  20th  century  romance  of  a  desert  island 
with  real  buried  treasure  and  real  pirates.  And, 
of  course,  there  is  a  beautiful  girl  mixed  up  in  the 
imbroglio. — New  York  Times. 

Machen,  Arthur.    The  house  of  souls.     1922. 

F  24849 

Contents:  A  fragment  of  life. — The  white  people. 
— The  great  god  Pan. — The  inmost  light. 

_Psychic  mystery  stories  having  to  do  with  the 
principles  of  alchemy.  Mr.  Machen's  other-world 
people  are  all  inimical  to  humanity,  and  when  in- 
voked the  result  is  disaster. 

Maher,    Richard    Aumerle.      The    works    of 
Satan.     1921.  F  24943 

Satan  is  the  harmless  editor  in  a  sleepy  little 
village  who  starts  a  bit  of  gossip  to  make  things 
happen.  The  town  liar  complicates  matters  and 
Yaleville  is  utterly  upset.  Everything  is  straight- 


ened out  in  the  end  by  Satan's  aid.     A  good  screen 
comedy  with  small-town   atmosphere. 

Mansfield,    Katherine.      The    garden    party, 
and  other  stories.     1922.  F  24953 

Contents:  At  the  bay.  —  The  garden  party.  —  The 
daughters  of  the  late  colonel.  —  Mr.  and  Mrs.  Dove. 
—  The  young  girl.  —  Life  of  Ma  Parker.  —  Marriage 
a  la  mode.  —  The  voyage.  —  Miss  Brill.  —  Her  first 
ball.  —  The  singing  lesson.  —  The  stranger.  —  Bank 
holiday.  —  An  ideal  family.  —  The  lady's-maid. 

A  collection  of  dramatic  short  stories  with  an 
English  setting.  They  are  strong  and  impressive, 
emphasizing  various  phases  of  life,  and  are  mostly 
psychological  studies  of  character. 


Marquand,  J.    P. 
man.     1922. 


The   unspeakable   gentle- 
F 15214 


A  highly  entertaining  story  laid  in  the  United 
States  in  the  colonial  period  involving  an  important 
paper  which  deals  with  treason  against  Napoleon. 
The  action  takes  place  in  three  days  crammed  with 
thrilling  moments  —  duels,  night  attacks,  treachery 
and  much  open  fighting. 

Marsh,  George.     Toilers  of  the  trails.    1921. 

F  24969 

Contents:  For  the  Great  Father.  —  Out  of  the 
mist.  —  A  little  tragedy  at  Coocoocache.  —  When  the 
prince  came  home.  —  With  the  winter  mail.  —  The 
valley  of  the  Windigo.  —  -The  quest  of  Narcisse  La- 
blanche.  —  The  land  of  his  fathers.  —  The  high. 
brotherhood. 

Nine  adventure  stories  of  the  Canadian  north- 
west with  a  background  of  hunters  and  trappers  and 
a  "wild  countree." 


Children     of     the 
F  24994 


Masters,     Edgar     Lee. 
market  place.     1922. 

The  autobiographical  story  of  an  English  lad  who 
comes  to  the  United  States  in  the  1830's  and  lives 
through  the  pioneer  struggles  and  political  experi- 
ences of  the  middle  west  up  to  the  Civil  War. 
Stephen  A.  Douglas  is  the  leading  figure. 

Matthews,    Brander.       Vignettes     of    Man- 
hattan;   outlines    in    local    color.      1921. 

F 25004 

Contents:  Vignettes  of  Manhattan:  In  the  little 
church  down  the  street. — The  twenty-ninth  of  Febru- 
ary.—At  a  private  view. — Spring  in  a  side  street. 
— A  decoration  day  revery. — In  search  of  local 
color.- — Before  the  break  of  day. — A  midsummer

Merwin,    Samuel.      Goldie    Green. 


1922. 
F 15300 


A    story    of    the    new    girl    in    this    new    world    so 
realistically  told  that  its  unreality  is  overlooked. 


Milne,    A.    A. 
1922. 


The     red    house    mystery. 
F 15386 


The  host  at  an  English  country  house  disap- 
pears, and  his  brother  is  found  dead.  The  chance 
caller  who  appears  on  the  scene  at  the  critical  mo- 
ment assumes  the  role  of  an  amateur  Sherlock 
Holmes.  The  solution  is  found  in  the  discovery  of 
a  secret  passage  after  many  hair-raising  experiences. 

Moorhouse,  Hopkins,  pseud,  of  Herbert 
Joseph  Moorhouse.  The  gauntlet  of  Al- 
ceste.  1922.  F 15492 


132 


A    murder    mystery   story    with    a    good    plot   and 
satisfactory  solution   not  easily  foreseen. 

Nicholson,    Meredith.      Best    laid    schemes. 
1922.  F  15669 

Contents:  The  Business  of  Susan. — The  girl 
with  the  red  feather. — The  Campbells  are  coming. 
— Arabella's  house  party. — The  third  man. — Wrong 
number. 

Mr.  Nicholson's  gay  humor,  light  touch,  and  neat 
turn  for  the  ingenious  combine  to  make  of  these 
half  dozen  stories,  which  first  saw  the  light, in  the 
pages  of  a  magazine,  a  consistently  entertaining  col- 
lection.— Literary  Review. 

Oppenheim,    E.    Phillips.      Nobody's    man. 
1921.  F 25763 

An  account  of  Andrew  Tallente's  uncertain  po- 
litical views  and  perplexing  career,  interspersed  with 
elements  of  ambition,  love  and  speculation. 


Pattee,    Fred    Lewis. 
black  ring.      1905. 


The    house    of    the 
F  16058 


A  mystery  story  not  lacking  in  humor  and  with 
a  readable  plot  centering  around  a  mysterious  cabin 
among  the  caves  and  mountains  of  Pennsylvania. 

Paul,  Elliot  H.     Indelible;   a  story  of  life, 
love,  and  music,  in  five  movements.    1922. 

F  16077 

A  very  promising  first  novel  on  the  development 
of  a  musical  genius  written  by  a  musician  who 
shows  himself  to  be  a  literary  artist  as  well.  New 
England  prejudice  comes  in  contact  with  the  ar- 
tistic temperament  of  Jewish  immigrants  from  Rus-

1921. 


Nina    Wilcox. 


West    Broadway. 
F  16703 


A  movie  star  makes  an  auto  trip  from  New  York 
to  California  and  records  in  slangy  diction  her  ob- 
servations of  conditions  as  she  finds  them  on  "West 
Broadway,"  which  extends  all  the  way  to  the  Golden

Raine,  William  MacLeod.     Man-size.    1922. 

F 16811 

Starts    with   a   rush,    continues    with   a    roar,   and 

•    ends    with    a   bang.      In    other    words   it    is   a    good 

Western   yarn    a   trifle   above   the   average    in   point 


of    prose    narrative    and    with    characters    that    carry 
conviction. — New    York    Times. 

Rees,    Arthur    J.      The    moon    rock.      1922. 

F 17009 

A  mystery  story  with  the  scene  laid  along  the 
rocky  Cornish  coast.  The  victim  is  murdered  just 
as  he  succeeds  in  proving  himself  heir  to  a  title 
after  a  genealogical  break  of  four  centuries. 


Rhodes,   Eugene   Manlove. 
Trail.     1922. 


Copper    Streak 
F  17118 


It  is  a  tale  of  men's  wits  and  the  best  man  wins. 
Arizona  forms  the  background  and  several  rough 
crooks  pitted  against  Pete  Johnson  and  his  tender- 
foot partner  from  New  York  provide  the  plot. 

Rideout,  Henry  Milner.  The  winter  bell. 
1922.  F 17209 

A  well  told  story  of  a  despondent  man,  falsely 
imprisoned,  who  regains  interest  _in  life  through 
having  his  attention  turned  to  reading. 

Rolland,  Remain.     Pierre  and  Luce.     1922. 

F 17412 

The  story  of  a  pair  of  youthful  lovers  in  war- 
time Paris.  Throughout  the  book  the  shadow  of 
bitterness  and  impending  death  hovers  over  them, 
a  dark  background  for  their  tragic,  impassioned 
love. 

Rousseau,  Victor,  pseud,  of  Victor  Rousseau 
Emanuel,  The  Big  Muskeg.  1921. 

F 12728 

A  Canadian  romance  of  adventure  of  the  sen- 
sational type. 

Ruck,    Berta,    (Mrs.    Oliver    Onions).      The 

wrong   Mr.    Right.      1922.  F  15783 

A   story  describing  the   adventures   of   a  business 

firl  in   London   combined  in   a  lively  puzzling  plot, 
t  deals  with  the  consequences  of  a  lie  which  pur- 
sues the   heroine   until   she   untells   it. 

Schnitzler,  Arthur.  The  shepherd's  pipe, 
and  other  stories.  1922.  F  17767 

Contents:  The  shepherd's  pipe. — The  murderer. 
— The  blind  Geronimo  and  his  brother. 

The  first  English  translation  of  these  very  read- 
able stories,  which  are  all  of  a  psychological  char- 
acter. 

Sedgwick,  Anne  Douglas,  (Mrs.  Basil  de 
Selincourt).  Adrienne  Toner.  1922. 

F 17907 

A  story  with  an  unusual  character  portrayal  of  a 
woman,  sympathetic  and  generous,  whose  influence 
dominates  everyone  with  whom  she  comes  in  con- 
tact. Nevertheless  there  is  a  disastrous  marriage, 
and  then  the  war. 


Shands,      Hubert      Anthony, 
black.     1922. 


White     and 
F  17952 


A    serious    novel    of    the    negro    problem    with    the

Singmaster,    Elsie. 


Bennett    Malin.  '    1922. 
F  18169 


A  novel  of  characterization  rather  than  plot.  A 
theological  student  gives  up  the  ministry  to  follow 
his  secret  ambition  to  become  a  writer.  The  girl 
he  marries  sacrifices  everything  to  his  struggling 
ambition — hopelessly.  To  their  son,  however,  came 
the  gift  of  authorship  without  effort. 

Smith,    Gordon    Arthur.      There    goes    the 
groom.     1922.  F 18217 

Four  old  bachelors  try  to  find  a  wife  for  George, 
the  nephew  of  one  of  them.  The  plot  develops  into 
part  mystery,  part  idyll  and  part  plain  fun,  and 
ends  with  a  truly  surprising  conclusion.

Tarkington,    Booth. 


Gentle    Julia.       1922. 
F 27704 

Simple  is  this  story,  but  it  manages  to  keep  you 
amused  and  alert  every  moment,  and  it  rouses  you

as  adorable. — New  York  Times.

we  live  in.  1921.  K  18596 

A  good  foundation  and  background  of  the  story

of  the  ancients  and  of  savage  people,  and  concludes 
with  the  story  of  the  planets  and  the  solar  system.

Flint;   the  story 
H7383 


Flint  is  a  young  Iroquois  Indian  of  the  Mohawk 
tribe.      His    father,    the   Bear-man,    adopts   a    young 
Indian   boy  and   later  an   Indian  girl   comes  to   their 
tribe.      The    story   is   made   up    of   their   adventures 


interwoven    with    a    great    deal    of    Indian    tradition 
and   folk-lore.

1922. 


Spotted     Deer. 
H8302 


A  thrilling  story  of  the  capture  of  Spotted  Deer,

joyous  guests.     1921.  H  4826

Rolt-Wheeler,       Francis       William.         The 
wreck-hunters.     1922.  H  6140 

The  wreck-hunters  rescue  ships  in  danger  of 
sinking,  save  the  cargoes  of  wrecked  vessels  and 
dive  for  sunken  treasures.  The  story  will  interest 
boys  who  like  stories  of  pirates  and  adventure. 

Singmaster,    Elsie.      John    Baring's    house. 
1920.  H  6571 

A  story  of  two  grandchildren  of  John  Baring, 
who  attempt  to  prove  the  innocence  of  their  grand- 
father in  the  Civil  War.  Thrilling  adventure  makes 
the  book  one  that  boys  will  like.

Our  Library 

1897-1922 

OTALWART  as  some  stern  fortress,  and  alone — 

^^   How  many  thousands  mount  these  steps  each  day, 

Both  young  and  old ;  and  happier  come  away. 

Inside  this  grim  exterior  of  gray  stone 

Vast  treasure  in  a  marble  palace  lies. 

Students  learn  more  than  ancient  sages  knew; 

Come  connoisseurs  of  entertainment,  too, 

And  enterprising  youth  with  shining  eyes. 

Those  suffering  in  body  and  in  mind — 
People  to  whom  the  world  has  been  unkind ; 
Dreamers   and   men   called    failures,   come    and    find 
Solace  and  comfort,  friends  who  understand, 
Service  more  great  than  they  who  built  had  planned — 
A  sanctuary  in  an  alien  land. 

— Helen  Forman. 
of  the  Library  Staff 


138  THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

Our  Silver  Jubilee 

TWENTY-FIVE  years  ago,  on  Saturday,  October  9,  1897,  the  Central  Li- 
brary building  was  dedicated  "to  the  use  and  benefit,  the  education  and 
elevation  of  the  people,"  "to  the  spread  of  light  and  truth  and  knowledge 
through  the  generations  that  are  to  come."  On  the  following  Monday  it  was 
open  for  service.  The  enormous  task  of  moving  was  accomplished  in  one 
week,  and  was  performed  for  the  most  part  by.  library  employees. 

The  ground  upon  which  the  building  stands  was  the  old  Dearborn 
Park,  part  of  the  original  military  reservation  intended  by  the  early  City 
Fathers  to  be  retained  as  "public  ground,  forever  to  remain  vacant  of  build- 
ings." However,  changed  conditions  brought  new  ordinances,  and  after  serv- 
ing the  public  well  for  twenty-five  years  under  the  most  adverse  circum- 
stances and  without  an  abiding  place  the  Library  was  finally  to  rejoice  in  a 
habitation  of  its  own. 

The  building  was  erected  at  a  cost  of  two  million  dollars  and  is  a  special 
monument  to  the  probity,  economy  and  efficiency  of  the  Board  of  Directors 
in  that  it  was  constructed  well  within  the  time  and  appropriation  called  for  by 
the  contract.  The  .daily  and  periodical  press  of  that  day  was  full  of  descrip- 
tions of  its  architecture  and  ornamentation.  Its  appearance  has  become  a 
matter  of  our  common  experience,  being  part  of  the  familiar  background  oi 
the  city  life  of  the  present  generation,  but  it  is  gratifying  to  our  local  pride 
to  bear  in  mind  that  with  all  its  points  of  excellence  "in  at  least  one  par- 
ticular this  edifice  is  of  international  interest,  and  is  unique  in  the  history  of 
modern  architecture,"  as  Mr.  Forrest  Crissey  wrote  in  a  contemporary  issue 
of  The  Outlook.  "Its  interior  walls  are  embellished  with  a  greater  quantity 
of  fine  glass  and  marble  mosaic  work  than  has  been  put  into  any  other  build- 
ing since  the  completion  in  the  thirteenth  century  of  the  celebrated  cruci- 
form cathedral  at  Monreale,  Sicily."  These  mosaics  are  not  subject  to  de- 
struction by  gas  and  smoke  as  painting  and  other  more  usual  modes  of  dec- 
oration would  be,  but  possess  the  same  elements  of  indestructibility  which  have 
kept  the  walls  of  St.  Sophia  in  Constantinople  standing  for  nearly  fourteen 
hundred  years.  Those  who  visit  the  Library  today  find  the  brilliance  of  its 
Carrara  walls  and  the  luster  of  the  green  and  gold  mosaics  undimmed. 

Many  of  our  citizens  will  still  remember  the  dedicatory  exercises,  which 
were  very  interesting  and  impressive.  The  Hon.  Azel  F.  Hatch,  then  presi- 
dent of  the  Board,  formally  tendered  the  building  to  the  City/  and  in  an  ad- 
dress which  sums  up  most  admirably  the  history  of  the  Chicago  Public  Li- 
brary up  to  that  time,  rendered  an  account  of  the  stewardship  so  faithfully 
performed. 

Dr.  Emil  G.  Hirsch  had  been  a  member  of  the  Library  Board  throughout 
the  entire  period  of  the  construction  of  the  building  and  was  its  president  just 
preceding  Mr.  Hatch.  On  giving  the  address  of  the  evening  he  spoke  most 
eloquently  of  the  relation  of  the  Library  to  the  people  of  the  city  and  of  the 
responsibilities  it  assumed  in  serving  them.  A  few  scattered  paragraphs  from 


BOOK  BULLETIN  139 

this  address  may  help  to  show  the  inspiring-  idealism  that  characterized  Dr. 
Hirsch's  utterances  as  it  characterizes  all  the  interests  of  this  public-spirited 
leader. 

"There  is  scarce  a  home  in  this  million-hearted  city,  whatever  the  dialect  in 
which  the  ditty  is  sung  over  the  cradle  it  rocks,  whatever  the  tongue  in  which 
protection  is  invoked  from  the  God  it  worships,  be  its  furnishings  humble 
or  luxurious  its  draperies,  but  rejoices  with  us  tonight  that  the  Library's 
days  of  wandering  in  the  desert  are  over,  that  it  has  at  last  crossed  the  Jordan 
and  entered  upon  a  new  career  in  the  land  of  a  larger  promise." 

"If  I  aprehend  the  policy  of  the  Board  correctly,  in  all  these  years  the  guid- 
ing principle  in  the  administration  of  the  trust  has  been  the  remembrance 
that  like  every  university,  this  the  people's,  too,  has  the  appointment  to  foster  the 
humanities  and  to  inculcate  patriotism.  Neither  of  these  elements  can  be  spared 
in  the  national  consciousness  of  a  strong  nation." 

"Here  then  is  the  one  opportunity  of  the  Library.  Its  shelves  offer  to  the 
thousands  what  the  college  holds  for  a  score.  The  cultured  man  or  woman  is 
he  or  she  who  is  at  home  first  in  this  world.  The  heavens  spell  endless  se- 
ries of  riddles  and  the  rocks  hide  multiplied  mysteries.  It  is  man's  privilege 
to  feel  the  burden  of  this  ignorance  and  in  ever  widening  measure  to  lighten 
it." 

"Our  Library  provides  a  feast  of  entertainment  that  leaves  no  bitter  taste  in 
the  mouth  of  those  who  accept  the  invitation  to  partake  of  its  hospitalities.  A 
people  that  reads  even  for  amusement  is  guarded  against  falling  into  traps 
but  too  easily  sprung.  Had  our  Library  done  even  more  for  its  novel-devour- 
ing readers  than  it  has,  it  would  have  deserved  well  of  the  community. 
Austere  disciplinarians  and  ascetic  fanatics  may  repress  for  a  time  the  natur- 
al ebullitions  of  the  lighter  moods  of  a  people.  Virtue  and  a  smiling  counte- 
nance are  not  dwellers  of  opposite  poles.  The  despotism  of  straight-laced 
moral  pessimism  will  engender  by  sheer  recoil  the  irritated  assertion  of  baser 
passions.  It  will  fan  into  a  devouring  flame  the  sensual  instincts,  and  indi- 
vidual and  community  alike  will  suffer.  The  novel  has  a  place  by  the  side  of 
the  Bible." 

"Let  us  not  forget  that  our  America  is  no  longer  New  England.  It  is  in- 
deed the  New  World.  To  the  stream  of  its  life  all  the  rivers  of  Europe,  and 
before  Europe,  of  Asia,  have  contributed.  Our  history  is  the  history  of  the 
world.  .  .  No  one  can  understand  the  peculiar  destiny  of  the  American  nation 
who  is  not  at  home  thoroughly  in  the  history  of  the  whole  human  race.  .  .  . 
Out  of  the  best  that  the  different  races  and  nations  possess,  here  on  this 
broad  continent  of  freedom  will  be  evolved  a  new  type  of  humanity,  and  the 
public  library  which  places  within  easy  reach  of  the  mind  hungry  for  infor- 
mation the  knowledge  of  the  quality  of  the  different  constituent  elements  and 
their  peculiar  endowments,  will  be  a  mighty  factor  in  awakening  that  broader 
Americanism  which,  rising  above  petty  prejudices  and  ignorant  superstitions 
of  early  impressions  to  the  altitude  of  an  inclusive  and  broader  fellowship, 
will  not  ask  where  the  cradle  stood,  but  what  loyalty  there  burns  in  the  heart 
and  what  devotion  to  public  duty  and  private  virtue  is  the  guidance  to  ac- 
tion and  the  support  to  sentiment.  We  have  to  learn  of  other  nations  as  other 
nations  mav  learn  of  us.  No  Chinese  wall  can  shut  out  the  vital  influence 


140  THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

of  the  world  beyond.  A  public  library  which  would  pursue  a  provincial 
policy  will  stifle  patriotism  instead  of  stimulating  it." 

"We  cannot  repeat  on  our  soil  Pantheon  or  Parthenon.  .  .  .  But  books  as 
messengers  of  culture  are  independent  of  location,  social  condition  or  climatic 
circumstances.  In  the  most  portable  form,  the  best  that  the  world  has  thought 
and  the  best  for  which  the  world  has  striven  may  be  brought  to  the  thresh- 
old of  every  searching  mind  and  hopeful  soul. 

"Our  public  Library  has  grasped  this  opportunity.  It  stands  today  the  friend 
of  thousands,  the  helper  of  millions.  It  is  one  of  the  sheet-anchors  upon 
which  the  safety  of  our  communal  life  is  dependent.  It  supplements  the 
grand  work  done  by  our  schools.  It  is  often  an  ally  to  our  university  and 
the  growing  technical  schools  round  about  us.  It  is  indispensable  to  the  news- 
paper, and  confirms  or  corrects  contentions  of  those  that  every  day  court  and 
hold  the  attention  of  this  city;  it  offers  opportunities  for  self  culture  to 
thousands  who  are  denied  the  privilege  of  systematic  training;  it  is  conse- 
crated to  the  cause  of  humanity,  and  at  the  same  time  it  holds  aloft  the  blaz- 
ing light  of  a  true  world  including  loyalty  to  our  American  institutions.  In 
its  new  home,  with  its  larger  opportunities,  it  will  do  still  grander  service." 

"Chicago,  synonym  for  energy  and  industries,  push  and  ambition, — Chicago, 
swarming  hive  of  millions,  of  many  tongues,  and  many  hearts,  but  one  pur- 
pose and  one  flag, — Chicago  owns  no  title  to  distinction  more  legitimate  and 
more  lustrous  than  its  possession  of  this  grand  Public  Library, — its  own.  What 
its  future  will  be  none  may  foretell ;  but  that  it  will  in  ever  growing  measure 
be  a  mighty  factor  for  good  in  the  community,  none  may  doubt.  We  look 
hopefully  forward  to  the  coming  day;  for  we  realize  that  to  even  our  in- 
stitution apply  Victor  Hugo's  words :  "A  library  implies  an  act  of  faith  which 
generations  still  in  darkness  hid  sign  in  their  night  in  witness  of  the  dawn."

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate.    Those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  Room;  other 

collections  are  shelved  in  the  Civics  Department,   Music   Room  and  Thomas 

Hughes  Room  and  are  marked  accordingly.    *Ser.    denotes   serials 

which  do  not  circulate.

Civilization 

Sanger,  Margaret.  The  pivot  of  civilization. 
1922.  L 14316 

_  A_  plea  for  birth-control,  contrasting  the  old 
civilization  which  needed  unrestricted  breeding  to 
meet  the  normal  waste  of  life  through  war,  pesti- 
lence, etc.,  with  the  modern  which  uses  it  to  ex- 
ploit child  labor,  causes  the  land  to  be  populated 
with  the  feeble-minded,  and  gives  occasion  for 
charity. 

Tinker,  Chauncey  Brewster.  Nature's  sim- 
ple plan;  a  phase  of  radical  thought  in 
the  mid-eighteenth  century.  1922.  A  2370 

(Princeton  University.  The  Louis  Clark  Vanu- 
xem  foundation  lectures.)

A  description  of  the  first  successful  airplane

Persia  and  India.

Noyes,  Alfred.     Watchers  of  the  sky.     1922. 

E  14899, 1 

Contents:  Prologue. — Copernicus. — Tycho  Brahe. 
— Kepler.  —  Galileo.  —  Newton.  —  William-  Herschel 
conducts. — Sir  John  Herschel  remembers. — Epilogue. 
Narrative  poetry  showing  the  march  of  astronomy 
from  the  days  of  Copernicus,  on  through  the  ages 
to  the  presert  time.  It  is  the  first  of  a  trilogy 
to  be  called  The  torchbearers.

three  acts.      1922.  E 12099 

The  drama  is  based  on  the  antagonism  between 
father  and  son.  The  climax  comes  when  the  ship 
builder  is  unable  to  go  on  the  maiden  voyage  of 
his  new  ship  and  pleads  with  his  son,  who  is  un- 
willing to  carry  on  the  family  tradition,  to  take 
his  place.

An  extremely  entertaining  narrative  of  the  au- 
thor's South  American  adventures.  His  varied  ex- 
periences from  miner  to  diplomatic  attache,  his  jour- 
ney across  the  Andes  and  down  the  Amazon,  and 
his  travels  in  Bolivia,  Peru  and  Brazil,  make  very 
diverting  reading.

Treves,  Sir  Frederick.  The  Riviera  of  the 
Corniche  road.  1921.  111905 

Equipped  with  an  observant  eye,  a  keen  ear  for 
the  legends  and  folklore  of  the  district,  a  good 
camera,  and  an  easy,  graceful  pen,  Sir  Frederick 
Treves  has  produced  a  book  on  the  famous  Grande 
Corniche  which  is  not  likely  to  be  displaced  for  a 
good  many  years  to  come. — Times  (London).

Asia,  Africa 

China's    place   in    the    sun. 
110667 


A  survey  of  China's  human  and  material  forces, 
of  her  civilization  and  the  growth  of  her  national 
consciousness,  of  her  industrial  and  intellectual 
renaissance,  of  her  religious  condition,  of  the  strug- 
gle the  Chinese  people  are  making  to  fit  them- 
selves for  a  position  of  leadership  m  international 
affairs. — Bock  Review  Digest. 

Hunt,  Frazier.  The  rising  temper  of  the 
East  sounding  the  human  note  in  the 
world-wide  cry  for  land  and  liberty. 
1922.  1 10616 

_  Contents:  Gandhi  and  his  India. — The  new  reli- 
gious nationalism  of  the  East. — Young  China. — 
Kagawa  of  Kobe,  the  story  of  the  new  Japan. — 
Struggling  Korea. — Ivan  the  Jap  killer. — White  Aus- 
tralia.— Our  own  little  India. — Whose  country  is 
Haiti? — Our  restless  brothers  below  the  Rio 
Grande. — The  lamp  bearers. — The  world's  under- 
dogs, a  conclusion. 

States  the  ideals  of  the  colored  peoples  (black, 
brown  and  yellow),  their  hatred  of  the  white  man's 
domination  and  desire  for  self-government,  giving 
credit  to  the  white  when  it  is  due.  Contains  a 
chapter  on  the  work  of  the  missionaries. 

Scott,  A.  MacCallum.  Barbary;  the  romance 
of  the  nearest  East.  1921.  18953 

A  description  of  the  author's  journey  through 
Algeria  and  Tunis,  with  historical  references  enter- 
tainingly interspersed  with  facts  and  anecdotes,  and 
profusely  illustrated. 

North  America 
United  States,  Canada 

Drake,  Durant.  America  faces  the  future. 
1922.  1 4334 

Contents:  Liberty.  —  Equality. — Democracy. — Effi- 
ciency.— Patriotism. 

A  plea  for  our  national  standards.  The  author 
is  idealistic  and  very  confident  that  the  principles 
upon  which  our  government  was  founded,  if  loyally 
maintained,  will  suffice  for  all  the  ills  of  the  pres- 
ent social  unrest — labor  strifes,  racial  hatreds,  etc. 

Graham,  Stephen.  Tramping  with  a  poet 
in  the  Rockies.  1922.  111778 

A  record  of  a  tramjping  tour  through  Glacier 
National  Park  and  the  Canadian  Rockies  by  Vachel 
Lindsay  and  the  author.  It  reveals  the  poet's  per- 
sonality in  a  delightful  manner;  and  the  descrip- 
tions of  the  surroundings  is  quite  what  would  be 
expected  from  these  two  lovers  of  nature. 

Hollister,  Paul  M.  Famous  colonial  houses; 
with  an  introduction  by  Julian  Street. 
1921.  *P4047 

Mr.  Hollister  gives  these  interesting  old  homes 
individuality  and  charm.  He  relates  incidents  con- 
nected with  them,  historical  and  imaginary,  in  which 
the  humorous  element  is  not  lacking. 

Longstreth,  T.  Morris.  The  Laurentians, 
the  hills  of  the  habitant.  1922.  14215

151 


It  is  more  than  a  fine  description  of  a  lovely  re- 
gion ;  it  is  a  real  travel  book,  all  through,  of  that 
wild  and  not  very  well  known  mountainous  country 
of  French  Canada.  The  author's  friendship  with 
the  Indians,  fishermen  and  hunters  of  the  wilder- 
ness and  the  "habitants"  themselves,  makes  it  read 
like  a  story. 

Meredith,  Hugh.     An  account  of  the  Cape 
Fear    country,    1731.      1922.          B  5714, 36 
(Heartman's  historical  series.) 

Two_  letters  written  by  Meredith  to  Benjamin 
Franklin,  his  former  partner  in  the  printing  busi- 
ness, and  printed  by  Franklin  in  the  Pennsylvania 
Gazette  in  1731. 

Rollins,  Philip  Ashton.  The  cowboy;  his 
characteristics,  his  equipment,  and  his 
part  in  the  development  of  the  West. 

I  11739 

Performs  an  especial  task  and  one  that  has  for 
a  long  while  been  waiting  for  accomplishment:  sym- 
pathetically, humorously  but  with  sufficient  gravity 
it  paints  a  true  image  of  a  type  that  for  two  dec- 
ades has  held  the  imagination  of  the  world. — Book- 
man. 

Polar   Regions 

Markham,  Sir  Clements  R.  The  lands  of 
silence;  a  history  of  Arctic  and  Antarctic 
exploration.  1921.  111327 

Ponting,  Herbert  G.  The  great  white  South; 
•being  an  account  of  experiences  with 
Captain  Scott's  South  Pole  expedition, 
and  of  the  nature  life  of  the  Antarctic. 
1922.  1 11352 

More  interesting  than  most  novels  is  this  account 
of  Scott's  ill-fated  expedition  written  by  the  offi- 
cial photographer  of  the  party.  Excellent  illustra- 
tions give  a  clear  idea  of  the  immensity  and  beauty 
of  the  antarctic  continent. 

Rasmussen,  Knud.  Greenland  by  the  Po- 
lar sea;  the  story  of  the  Thule  expedi- 
tion from  Melville  Bay  to  Cape  Morris 
Jesup;  tr.  from  the  Danish.  1922. 

111329 

The  leader  of  this  expedition  was  born  in  Green- 
land, lived  there  as  a  boy  and  had  mastered  the 
language.  He  is  a  great  admirer  of  the  Eskimo 
and  tells  a  lively  interesting  story.  In  contrast  to 

E  reviews    expeditions    this    one    traveled    over    the 
ind    instead .  of    on   sea-ice,    thus    involving   greater 
risks. 


HISTORY 
The  World  War 

Cummings,  E.  E.  The  enormous  room. 
1922.  A  7197 

A  vivid  account  of  the  author's  experiences  in  a 
French  prison.  The  conditions  of  the  prison,  the 
petty  graft,  the  stupidity  of  some  in  authority,  are 
clearly  and  graphically  presented,  while  the  prison- 
ers are  drawn  with  vigorous  characterization. 

Germany.  Foreign  Office.  The  German 
army  in  Belgium,  the  white  book  of  May 
1915,  tr.  by  E.N.Bennett,  with  a  foreword 
on  military  reprisals  in  Belgium  and 
Ireland.  1921.  A  7196 

History  of  the  First  Division  during  the 
world  war,  1917-1919.  1922.  A  7200 


Hunton,  Addie  \V.,and  Johnson,  KathrynM. 
Two  colored  women  with  the  American  Ex- 
peditionary Forces.  1920.  A  7199 
Not  merely  a  collection  of  personal  experiences  of 
the  authors,  but  aims  to  present  also  a  full  account 
of  the  work  done  in  France  by  negro  regiments, 
combatant  and  non-combatant. — New  York  Evening 
Post.

Gleaves,  Albert.  A  history  of  the  transport 
service;  adventures  and  experiences  of 
United  States  transports  and  cruisers  in 
the  world  war.  1921  A  7202 

The  stories  are  taken  from  the  official  reports 
of  the  men  on  the  transports  and  given  in  their 
own  words.  The  Boston  Transcript  considers  the 
book  "one  of  the  most  important  contributions  to 
our  knowledge  of  the  war." 

Panaretoff,  Stephen.  Near  Eastern  affairs 
and  conditions.  1922.  A  5252 

(Williams  College  institute  of  politics  publica- 
tions.) 

A  series  of  lectures  by  the  ambassador  who  for 
years  has  represented  Bulgaria  in  the  United 
States.  The  author  desires  a  Balkan  confederation 
and  shows  the  conditions  that  have  hitherto  in- 
terfered.

of  political  liberty;  a  source  book  of  Eng- 
lish  history.      1922.  Open  Shelf 
(Everyman's  library.)

story  of  the  struggle  for  amnesty.  1922.

Asia,  Africa 

Chung,  Henry.  The  case  of  Korea;  a  col- 
lection of  evidence  on  the  Japanese  dom- 
ination of  Korea,  and  on  the  development 


152 


THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


of    the    Korean    independence    movement. 
1921.  A  4787 

The  case  of  Japan  becomes  the  case  of  England, 
of  France,  of  the  United  States.  The  lesson  to  be 
drawn  from  this  complete  manual  of  imperial  con- 
quest and  behavior  is  that  no  nation  is  good  enough 
or  great  enough  to  rule  another. — New  Republic. 

Harris,  Walter  B.  Morocco  that  was.    1921. 

A  4751 

An  entertainingly  written  acount  of  Morocco's 
last  years  as  an  independent  country.  The  author 
was  correspondent  for  the  London  Times  there  and 
gleaned  his  information  at  first  hand. 

Powell,  E.  Alexander.  Asia  at  the  cross- 
roads: Japan,  Korea,  China,  Philippine 
Islands.  1922.  A  4824 

A  simple  and  informal  discussion  of  problems, 
financial  and  political,  which  the  Literary  Review 
calls  a  "comprehensive  and  up-to-the-minute  study 
of  the  welter  of  suspicions,  intrigues,  and  cross- 
purposes  which  make  up  the  baffling  problem  of  the 
Pacific." 

Reinsch,  Paul  S.  An  American  diplomat 
in  China.  1922.  A  8116 

Contents:  Old  China  and  the  new  republic. — The 
passing  of  Yuan  Shih-Kai. — The  war  and  China. — 
Last  year  of  war  and  aftermath. 

Dr.  Reinsch  was  minister  to  China  from  1913  to 
1919,  so  that  his  experiences  ought  to  be  of  vital 
importance  to  those  interested  in  world  politics.

(University  of  Iowa  studies  in  the  social  sciences.) 

Lawrence,  Robert  Means.  Old  Park  Street 
and  its  vicinity.  1922.  B  7946 

Old  Park  Street,  Boston,  began  its  fascinating 
career  as  a  muddy  lane  on  which  stood  an  alms- 
house  and  a  granary.  It  later  became  the  home 
of  many  distinguished  Bostonians.  The  reminis- 
censes  of  a  former  resident  add  greatly  to  the 
interest  of  the  picture.

Bacheller,  Irving, 
ard.     1922. 


In  the  days  of  Poor  Rich- 
F 20095 


An  historical  romance  American  in  spirit  but 
international  in  scope,  centering  around  the  per- 
sonality of  Franklin,  idealized.  There  are  glimpses 
of  Washington,  Patrick  Henry,  Benedict  Arnold, 
George  III  and  Louis  XVI.  The  love  story  of 
the  Yankee  boy  and  English  girl  typifies  the  greater 
love  between  man  and  liberty. 

Batten,   H.   Mortimer.     Many   trails.     1921. 

F  20289 

Stories  of  animal  life. 

Burnett,    Frances    Hodgson.      Robin.      1922. 

F  20787 

Sequel  to  The  head  of  the  House  of  Coombe, 
F  20786. 

All  lovers  of  romance  will  welcome  and  deliglit 
in  this  tale  of  young  love,  which  is  the  story 
of  Donal  Muir  and  Robin. — New  York  Times. 

Caven,  Stewart.     A  pair  of  idols.     1922. 

F 11702 

A  charming  comedy  centering  about  Irish  arche- 
ology, with  good  local  background  not  overdrawn. 
It  is  a  love  story  with  plenty  of  Irish  wit  and 
ingenuity.

Short  stories  of  the  Sein  Feiners  and  their 
great  heroism  while  working  for  the  freedom  of

phere that  lifts  them  from  the  merely  clever"

Fletcher,  J.  S.     Ravensdene  Court.     1922. 

F 22131 

A  combination  mystery,  detective  and  adventure 
tale,  much  above  the  average.  Cold  shivers  chase 
themselves  up  and  down  one's  spinal  column,  and 
one's  attention  never  wavers.

F  22532 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


153 


An  ironical  study  of  civilization  as  seen  through 
the  eyes  of  a  satyr  who  leaves  his  happy  carefree 
life  and  lured  by  a  woman  goes  to  live  in  a  French 
town.  His  views  of  the  modern  world  of  morals 
and  conventions  are  delightfully  refreshing. 

Howard,  F.  Morton.    Happy  rascals.     1922. 

F 14136 

Ten  short  sea  stories. 

Huxley,  Aldous.     Mortal  coils.     1922. 

F  23309 

Contents:  The  Gioconda  smile. — Permutations 
among  the  nightingales,  cne  act  play. — The  Tillot- 
son  banquet. — Green  tunnels. — Nuns  at  luncheon. 

A  collection  of  five  ironical,  satirical,  startlingly 
brilliant  pieces,  at  times  very  unpleasant  and  pessi- 
mistic, but  humorous  always.  "The  Gioconda  smile" 
has  been  ranked  with  the  best  of  Maupassant. 

Malet,  Lucas,  pseud,  of  Mary  St.  Leger  Har- 
rison. Da  Silva's  widow,  and  other 
Stories.  1922.  F  22703 

Contents:  Da  Silva's  widow. — Boy's  love. — The 
higher  education  of  Mr.  Besley  Wright. — The  privet 
hedge. — The  birth  of  a  masterpiece. — Julia's  mar- 
riage.— On  the  boat  deck. — How  they  met  again. — 
The  pill-box. 

A  hint  of  tragedy  balanced  by  a  vein  of  optimism 
characterizes  each  of  the  vital,  intensely  human 
sketches  contained  in  this  collection.  A  dramatic 
force  underlies  the  telling  of  the  stories  and  tinges 
each  with  an  element  of  suspense. 


Parsons,  Elsie  Clews,  ed.    American  Indian 
life,  by  several  of  its  students.     1922. 

•  F  16044 

Each  outstanding  tribe  of  North  American 
Indians  is  represented  with  a  story  typical  of  its 
people,  authentic  as  to  historic  background,  and 
accurate  in  details  of  daily  life  and  routine. 

Payne,  Elizabeth   Stancy.     All   the  way  by 
water.     1922.  F 16071 

An  exciting  adventure  story.  The  pursued  hero- 
ine is  rescued  by  a  gallant  yacht,  and  the  cruise 
becomes  thrilling,  with  secret  service  men,  gun 
fights,  an  abduction,  and  the  development  of  a  love 
story. 


Rinehart,     Mary     Roberts, 
point.     1922. 


The     breaking 
F  17257 


A  story  centering  around  the  nephew  of  an  old 
village  doctor,  who  lives  quietly  and  happily  con- 
tented with  his  work  as  assistant  to  his  uncle.  The 
revealing  of  a  submerged  identity  gives  rise  to  an 
unusual  plot,  with  both  mystery  and  romance  com- 
bined.

of  the 


Chicago  Public  Library 


Volume  12  November,  1922  No.  9 

Children's  Book  Week 

November  12-18 

TVURING  four  years  of  united  effort  of  parents  and  teachers,  librarians  and 
*-^  the  makers  and  sellers  of  books,  a  common  basis  of  sympathy  and  under- 
standing has  been  established  which  makes  this,  the  fourth  annual  Chil- 
dren's Book  Week,  a  celebration  worthy  of  special  mention.  Great  stress  has 
been  placed  on  the  advancement  of  the  theory  that  the  selection  of  the  best 
books  for  children's  reading  is  an  important  factor  in  American  education. 
For  it  is  now  an  undisputed  fact  that  there  are  really  good  books  for  and 
about  lifelike  boys  and  girls  instead  of  merely  books  about  impossibly  good 
or  unnaturally  bad  children,  as  was  too  long  the  case.  A  child's  taste  is 
naturally  good,  and  it  is  only  from  perversion  or  neglect  that  he  learns  to 
prefer  the  lurid  or  exaggerated  to  simple  and  true  presentations  of  life  and 
beauty. 

Various  means  of  concentrating  attention  during  this  week  on  books 
which  will  give  children  the  greatest  pleasure  "from  nursery  rhyme  to  grown- 
up time"  have  been  devised  by  booksellers,  teachers,  and  librarians.  The 
Chicago  Public  Library  will  observe  Children's  Book  Week  in  book  displays 
in  the  Central  Building  and  all  branches,  and  in  the  distribution  of  special 
children's  lists.  The  Central  Library  will  have  an  attractive  exhibit  in  the 
corridor  adjoining  the  Thomas  Hughes  Room  for  children,  located  on  the 
fourth  floor,  Washington  Street  side.  In  the  Hughes  Room  as  well  as  in  all 
out-lying  branches  the  suggestive  lists  on  the  following  page  may  be  had  and 
the  books  recommended  on  them. 


158  THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 

Lists  for  Free  Distribution 

Boys'  Books.  A  list  of  about  thirty  books  for  boys  in  the  grade  schools  with 
brief  descriptive  notes.  They  are  standard  books  of  adventure  and  achieve- 
ment which  all  boys  like  to  read,  distributed  by  the  Chicago  Public  Library 
during  Boys'  Week. 

A  Shelf  of  Books  for  a  One-Room  School.  The  twenty-five  books  selected  as 
most  desirable  for  this  purpose  by  ballots  taken  at  the  annual  national  con- 
ferences of  teachers  and  librarians.  They  are  arranged  in  order  of  the  num- 
ber of  votes  .they  received  and  are  annotated. 

Children's  Books  for  Christmas  Presents.  The  titles  are  arranged  in  three 
groups:  Picture  books  and  other  books  for  little  children;  Books  for  young 
children;  and  Books  for  older  boys  and  girls.  Very  brief  notes  have  been 
added  from  various  library  book-lists. 

Chicago  Public  Library  book-marks.  Five  brief  graded  lists  with  alluring  notes 
and  special  references  in  each  case  to  some  other  equally  attractive  title. 

Graded  lists,  1-8.  Typewritten  lists  selected  for  recreational  reading  in  connec- 
tion with  class-room  libraries. 

Lists  for  Sale 

Christmas;  published  by  ithe  Chicago  Public  Library.  Lists  of  books  and  articles 
on  Christmas  legends,  traditions  and  customs  classified  by  countries,  and 
on  community  Christmas;  lists  also  of  Christmas  music,  Christmas  carols, 
poems,  plays,  monologs,  entertainments,  essays,  and  stories.  Price  5  cents. 

The  Bookshelf  for  Boys  and  Girls.  A  new  edition  delightfully  illustrated,  re- 
vised lo  August  1922,  carefully  annotated,  classified  and  indexed.  It  contains 
a  special  list  for  Boy  Scout  readers  edited  by  F.  K.  Mathiews,  Boy  Scout 
librarian.  Price  5  cents. 

Lists  for  Library  Reference 

Books  for  Boys  and  Girls.  A  suggestive  purchase  list  prepared  by  the  Boston 
Bookshop  for  Boys  and  Girls. 

A  List  of  Books  for  Girls  and  Boys.  Suggested  for  purchase  by  the  Children's 
Book  Shop  of  New  York. 

Books  to  grow  on.  A  valuable  list  of  suggestions  for  high-school  recreational 
reading  published  by  ithe  Buffalo  Public  Library. 

Reading  for  Pleasure  and  Profit.  A  similar  high-school  list  prepared  by  the 
Newark  Free  Public  Library. 

Graded  List  of  Books  for  Children.  Prepared  by  the  School-Library  Committee 
of  the  N.E.A.,  and  containing  120  pages  of  lists  followed  by  title  and  subject 
indexes. 

Graded  List  of  Stories  to  Read  Aloud  to  Children.  Published  by  the  Rosenberg 
Library  of  Galveston,  Texas. 

Stories  to  Tell  to  Children.  A  list  of  stories  for  young  children,  arranged  pro- 
gressively by  ages  and  used  with  success  in  library  story-hours. 

Fairy  Stories  to  Tell  and  Suggestions  for  the  Story-Teller,  by  Nina  Brotherton. 

Plays  for  Children.  An  annotated  index  arranged  alphabetically,  prepared  by 
Alice  Hazeltine  of  the  St.  Louis  Public  Library.  There  are  also  lists  of 
plays  suitable  for  all  holidays,  for  other  occasions,  and  of  operettas,  panto- 
mimes and  other  special  forms. 

Lists  of  Stories  and  Programs  for  Story  Hours.  Compiled  by  Effie  L.  Power 
for  the  St.  Louis  Public  Library. 

Reading  Lists  for  Special  Days.     Prepared  by  the  Cleveland  Public  Library. 

Heroism.  A  reading  list  for  boys  and  girls,  published  by  the  New  York  Public 
Library. 

Eighty  Tales  of  Valor  and  Romance.    Published  by  the  Cleveland  Public  Library. 

Other    suggestive    lists    for    children'ls    reading,    including    many    of    a    more 
specific  character,  may  be  consulted  at  the  Library  and  its  branches.

New  Books  for  the  Blind 

This  list  includes  books  for  the  blind  which  the  Library  has  received  since  May,  1921. 
Except  when  the  words  "full  spelling"  are  added,  the  type  includes  contractions  of  grade 
1  112  or  2.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 


Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate.    Those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  Room;  other 

collections  are  shelved  in  the  Civics  Department,    Music    Room   and   Thomas 

Hughes  Room  and  are  marked  accordingly.     *Ser.    denotes    serials 

which  do  not  circulate.

PHILOSOPHY 

Avalon,  Arthur,  pseud  of  Sir  J.  G.  Wood- 
roffe,  tr.  Principles  of  Tantra,  with  an 
introduction  and  commentary.  1914-1916. 
2v.  L  14635 

The  Tantras  are  the  bible  of  Shaktism  and  are 
sometimes  called  the  fifth  Veda. 

Croce,  Benedetto.  PICCOLI,  RAFFAELLO.  Ben- 
edetto Croce;  an  introduction  to  his  phi- 
losophy. 1922.  L  14715 


A  historical  sketch  and  criticism  of  the  philosophy 
of  this  Italian  philosopher  and  critic  with  whom  the 
author  is  decidedly  in  sympathetic  accord. 

Lucretius.  Of  the  nature  of  things;  a  metri- 
cal .translation  by  William  Ellery  Leonard. 
1922.  Open  Shelf 


(Everyman's  library,  v.   2931) 


Windelband,  Wilhelm. 
philosophy.      1921. 


An  introduction  to 
L 12909 


A  general  view  of  the  chief  problems  of  phil- 
osophy and  a  discussion  of  the  various  attempts  to 
solve  them,  together  with  a  wide  survey  of  all  the 
possibilities  in  the  way  of  solution. 

Psychology,   Occultism 

Maeterlinck,    Maurice.      The    great    secret. 
1922.  L  13376 

An  analysis  of  the  relation  of  occultism  to  the 
religions  of  India,  Egypt,  Persia  and  pre-Socratic 
Greece.  Maeterlinck  concludes  that  "the  great 
secret  is  that  all  things  are  secret  .  .  .  that  the 
only  divinity  which  we  can  understand  is  in  the 
depths  of  our  own  souls." 

Warren,  Howard  C.  A  history  of  the  asso- 
ciation psychology.  1921.  L 13989 
Dr.  Warren's  latest  work  marks  a  great  advance 
in  the  historical  study  of  psychology.  It  is  the 
first  thoroughly  adequate  treatment  of  one  of  the 
most  significant  phases  in  the  development  of  the 
science. — Literary  Review. 

Ethics,  Success 


Marden,  Orison  Swett. 
attract  it.     1922. 


Prosperity,  how  to 
L  14404 


A  collection  of  platitudes  on  How  to  increase 
your  ability,  How  to  make  your  dreams  come  true, 
How  to  make  your  subconscious  mind  work  for 
you,  How  to  make  yourself  lucky,  and  so  on. 

Family  and  Sexual  Ethics 

Ramus,  Carl.    Marriage  and  efficiency.    1922. 

L 14312 

A  frank  and  sensible  analysis  of  conditions  that 
threaten  marriage.  The  author's  solutions  are 
quoted  from  scientific  authorities  to  whom  the  edu- 
cated should  be  willing  to  listen.  It  is  thoroughly 
rational  in  tone  and  also  well  written. 

Royden,  A.  Maude.  Sex  and  common-sense. 
1922.  L 14471 

The  style  of  these  essays  is  admirably  clear,  and 
the  attitude  of  mind  logical,  tolerant  and  open  to 
discussion.  They  will  certainly  be  valuable  to  many 
readers  as  a  frank  exposition  of  the  stupendous 
difficulties  surrounding  the  relationship  of  the  sexes. 
— Saturday  Review. 

Wise,  Stephen  S.  Child  versus  parent;  some 
chapters  on  the  irrepressible  conflict  in 
the  home.  1922.  L 14319 

A  plea  for  deeper  understanding  between  parents 
and  children,  urging  the  parents  to  provide  not  only 
physical  care  but  real  companionship.

RELIGION 

Bente,  F.  American  Lutheranism.  1919. 
2v.  M  7854 

v.  1.  Early    history    of    American    Lutheranism    and 

the  Tennessee  synod. 

2.  The  United  Lutheran  Church;   General  Synod, 
General   Council,   United    Synod  in   the   South. 

Bible.  Selections.  The  English  Bible;  being 
a  book  of  selections  from  the  King  James 
version,  edited  with  introduction  and  ex- 
planatory notes,  by  Wilbur  Owen  Sypherd. 
1921.  M  6403 

Ellwood,  Charles  A.  The  reconstruction 
of  religion;  a  sociological  view.  1922. 

M8902 

This  book  undoubtedly  is  the  ablest  and  most 
stimulating  discussion  of  religion  in  its  relation  to 
modern  life  that  has  yet  appeared.  The  style  is 
direct  and  clear.  The  thought  is  logically  presented. 
In  spirit  it  is  fearless,  outspoken  and  even  tem- 
pered.— Annals  of  the  American  Academy  of  Po- 
litical and  Social  Sciences. 

Irvine,  Alexander.  The  Carpenter  and  His 
kingdom.  1922.  M  7145 

An  interpretation  of  the  life  of  Jesus  in  which 
the  author  attempts  to  show  how  our  present-day 
standards  of  society  conflict  with  the  teachings  of 
Christ. 

Mackay,  W.  Mackintosh.  Bible  types  of 
modern  men:  1st  ser.  1920.  M  4937 

Originally  published  under  the  title,  The  man  in 
the  street. 

Moffatt,  James.  Jesus  on  love  to  God,  Jesus 
on  love  to  man;  two  lectures  delivered 
before  the  University  of  Pennsylvania, 
March  27  and  28,  1922.  1922.  M  7115 

(The  George  Dana  Boardman  lectureship  in 
Christian  ethics.) 

Vedder,  Henry  C.  The  fundamentals  of 
Christianity;  a  study  of  the  teaching  of 
Jesus  and  Paul.  1922.  M  4379 

The  author  believes  that  man's  conception  of  God 
is  developing  toward  the  ideal  contained  in  the 
teaching  of  Jesus,  which  is  the  standard  by  which 
all  Christian  teaching  must  be  judged  and  the 
authority  on  which  Christian  doctrine  and  method 
must  rest.

THE  SOCIAL  SCIENCES 

Giddings,  Franklin  H.    Studies  in  the  theory

nile Highbrows  and  to  our  Little  Groups  of  Serious 
Thinkers. — New  York  Times. 


Economics 

Henderson,  Hubert  D.  The  cotton  control 
board.  1922.  L 16795 

(Economic  and  social  history  of  the  world  war: 
British  series.) 

Hungerford,  Edward.  Our  railroads  to- 
morrow. 1922.  L  20235 

After  studying  the  railroads  of  the  U.  S.  for 
many  years,  the  author  presents  the  problem  of 
their  future  and  the  practical  solution  to  which  he 
has  arrived  after  conferences  with  railroad  men 
of  all  degrees.

Potter,  Pitman  B.  An  introduction  to  the 
study  of  international  organization.  1922. 

L  21458 

(The  Century  political  science  series.) 
This  book  presents  suggestions  for  improvements

ment. Modern  diplomacy,  state-systems,  treaties 
and  international  arbitration  are  some  of  the  sub- 
jects discussed.

Darrow,    Clarence, 
treatment.     1922. 


Practical  Sociology 
Crime,  Prohibition 
Crime;    its 


cause    and 
L  18883 


After  forty  years  in  American  courts,  Mr.  Darrow 
feels  that  the  public  attitude  toward,  and  treatment 
of,  the  criminal  is  erroneous.  Rather  revolutionary 
in  thought  and  deserves  study. 

Manufacturers  Record.  The  prohibition 
question  viewed  from  the  economic  and 
moral  standpoint;  the  views  of  hundreds 
of  leading  men  of  affairs,  manufacturers 
of  iron  and  steel,  cotton  and  other  prod- 
ucts, bankers,  lawyers,  college  and  univer- 
sity officials,  as  expressed  in  letters  to  the 
Manufacturers  Record  in  reply  to  a  ques- 
tionnaire on  the  subject.  1922.  L 14537 

Of  these  replies  98.5%  were  in  favor  of  prohibi- 
tion in  some  form,  and  85.5%  for  absolute  pro- 
hibition. Their  editor  says:  "In  these  letters  will 
be  found  unanswerable  arguments,  based  on  actual 
facts,  as  to  the  value  of  prohibition  from  every 
possible  standpoint."

SCIENCE 

Goldenweiser,  Alexander  A.  Early  civili-

1922.  K  20859 

A  detailed  and  scholarly  work  on  the  develop-

USEFUL  ARTS 
Hygiene 

American  Medical  Association.  Journal. 
Nostrums  and  quackery;  articles  on  the 
nostrum  evil,  quackery  and  allied  matters 
affecting  the  public  health.  1912-1921. 
2v.  N 1529 

Daniels,  R.  R.,  and  others.  Taking  it  on 
high;  body  strength  and  brain-power. 
1921.  N  2562 

A  practical  set  of  hygiene  rules  in  which  the 
body  is  compared  to  an  automobile.  It  emphasizes 
the  importance  of  keeping  one's  physical  machine 
in  good  condition. 

Lee,  Gerald  Stanley.  Invisible  exercise; 
seven  studies  in  self  command  with  prac- 
tical suggestions  and  drills.  1922.  N  2563 
The  author's  experiences  in  experimenting  with 
a  new  set  of  simple  exercises  that  can  be  used_  at 
your  desk,  or  reclining  in  your  chair,  or  standing 
in  the  street.  It  is  entertainingly  written,  original, 
and  offers  many  practical  suggestions. 

O'Shea,  M.  V.,  and  Kellogg,  J.  H.  Building 
health  habits.  1921.  N  78 

(The  everyday  health  series.) 

—Keeping  the  body  in  health.     1921.  N  80 

(The  everyday  health  series.) 

The  first  book  lays  stress  on  the  importance  of 
forming  correct  health  habits  during  childhood;  the 
second  is  written  from  a  physiological  standpoint 
in  relation  to  health. 

Peters,  Lulu  Hunt.  Diet  and  health,  with 
key  to  the  calories.  1922.  N  2504 

Rich,  Mrs.  Kenneth  F.  A  Community  Trust 
survey  of  prenatal  care  in  Chicago.  1922. 

N2179 

"A  study  of  the  importance  of  prenatal  care  and 
of  the  existing  facilities  in  local  hospitals  and 
clinics,  with  an  outline  of  standards  and  some 
recommendations."

Military  Science 

Hamilton,  Sir  Ian.  The  soul  and  body  of 
an  army.  1921.  K  31101 

This  is  a  brilliant  book  by  perhaps  the  most 
brilliant  of  British  generals.  The  author's  style  is 
easy,  colloquial,  and  even  slangy  at  times,  but  not 
seldom  he  rises  into  real  eloquence,  and  he  has 
the  gift  of  vivid  description. — Literary  Review.

Outdoor  Sports 
Golf 

Golf  for  occasional  players,  by  a  veteran. 
1922.  K  30591 

The  author  cautions  the  player  against  trying  to 
imitate  world-famed  champions  and  thus  becoming 
discouraged.  The  book  explains  difficulties  and  their 
prevention  and  is  full  of  simple,  helpful  advice. 

Massy,  Arnaud.     Golf.     1922  K  30607 

A  detailed  description  of  each  stick,  each  stroke 
and  each  position,  with  clear  explanations  of  all 
terms  used  in  golf  and  a  lucid  analysis  of  the  game. 

Vardon,  Harry.     The  gist  of  golf.     1922. 

K  30618 

A  non-technical  book  devoted  entirely  to  the  use 
of  clubs.  It  explains  fully  the  grip  and  swing  of 
the  driver,  cleek,  niblick,  mashee,  midiron  and 
putter.

Poetry 

Croce,  Benedetto.  The  poetry  of  Dante. 
1922.  E  14579 

A  thorough  criticism  and  analysis  of  Dante's 
Divine  Comedy,  with  comments  on  his  life,  the 
character  of  his  poetry,  and  a  discussion  of  other 
Dantean  criticism  by  Italy's  foremost  living  critic. 

Graves,  Robert.  On  English  poetry;  being 
an  irregular  approach  to  ithe  psychology 
of  this  art,  from  evidence  mainly  sub- 
jective. 1922.  J7975 

A  sympathetic  analysis  of  the  mental  contortions, 
the  conflicting  emotions,  of  a  poet,  that  harnessed 
and  moulded  into  certain  forms  produce  poetry.  A 
treatise  that  is  psychological  and  scholarly. 


Blunden,  Edmund.  The  shepherd,  and 
other  poems  of  peace  and  war.  1922. 

E  14497 

These  are  very  lovely  pastoral  poems  with  a  depth 
of  musical  cadence  reminiscent  of  Rupert  Brooke. 

Frothingham,  Robert,  ed.  Songs  of  chal- 
lenge, an  anthology.  1922.  E 14370 

A  well  selected  collection  of  inspirational  poems 
rarely  sentimental,  covering  such  a  wide  field  that 
it  embraces  both  Frangois  Villon  and  S.  E.  Kiser. 

Fuess,  Claude  Moore,  and  Stearns,  Harold 
Crawford,  comps.  The  little  book  of  so- 
ciety verse.  1922.  E  14368 

An  anthology  which  includes  among  many  other 
names  Dobson,  Landor,  Locker-Lampson,  Moore, 
Cowper,  Praed  and  Thackeray. 


Pocock,  Guy  N.,  ed.     Modern  poetry.     1921 

E  14369 

(The   kings   treasuries    of  literature.)

poetry;  translations  into  alliterative  verse 
with  introductions  and  notes.  1922. 

E  14367 

An  excellent  collection,  well  translated,  of  poetry 
written  between  the  time  of  the  Anglo-Saxon  in- 
vasion and  the  Norman  conquest.

E  14371 

Mr.  Talley  has  accompanied  these  delightful

origin  and  development.

Contents:  The  double  miracle,  by  R.  Garland. —
