Essays 

Broun,  Heywood.  Pieces  of  hate  and  other 
enthusiasms.  1922.  E  2049 

This  book  of  humorous  essays  is  inspired  fooling 
almost  from  beginning  to  end.  The  author  has  the 
novel  idea  of  prefacing  the  essays  with  an  unfavor- 
able review  of  them  written  by  himself,  in  which  he 
remarks  gravely:  "We  wish  we  could  speak  with 
unreserved  enthusiasm  about  this  book." 

Eaton,  Walter  Prichard.  Penguin  persons 
and  peppermints.  1922.  E2578 

These  are  short,  informal  papers  in  different 
moods,  and  most  of  them  are  the  sort  of  thing  that 
one  cannot  resist  calling  whimsical ;  yet  the  book 
is  not  one  to  pick  up  if  you  have  only  a  few 
minutes  to  read,  'because  it  is  too  hard  to  put 
down. — Bookman. 

TRAVEL  AND  DESCRIPTION 

Europe  and  Asia 

Fraprie,  Frank  Roy.  The  spell  of  the  Rhine. 
1922.  1 7520 

(The  spell  series.) 

The  war  is  forgotten  and  one  remembers  only 
the  old  romantic  Rhine  in  reading  this  book,  which 
would  be  an  ideal  companion  for  a  trip  on  that 
famous  old  river. 

Maxwell,  Donald.  Unknown  Kent;  being 
a  series  of  unmethodical  explorations  of 
the  county,  illustrated  in  line  and  colour 
by  the  author.  1922.  110369 

Compact  with  charm.  It  is  written  in  a  delight- 
fully discursive  style  and  its  chapters  dealing  with 
the  idle  wanderings  of  the  author  through  the  many 
pleasant  by-ways  of  Kent  are  most  engrossing. — 
New  York  Times. 


Snowden,  Mrs.   Philip, 
in   Europe.      1921. 


A  political  pilgrim 
1 11271 


The  author  has  for  a  number  of  years  lectured 
in  the  U.  S.  on  woman  suffrage.  As  a  member 
of  the  British  labor  movement  she  gives  an  account 
of  how  she  found  the  political,  economic  and  social 
conditions  during  her  two  years  in  Europe.  Her 
meetings  with  well  known  people  are  gossipy  and 
vividly  told. 

Stein,  Aurel.  Serindia;  detailed  report  of 
explorations  in  Central  Asia  and  western- 
most China  carried  out  and  described  un- 


der  the   orders   of  H.  M.  Indian  govern- 
ment.    1921.     5v.  *P4006 

A  scholarly  and  scientific  account  of  the  author's 
second  expedition  into  China.  The  fourth  volume 
consists  of  175  plates,  many  of  which  are  in  color. 

North  and  South  America 

Argentine  Republic.  Ministry  of  Public 
Works.  Northern  Patagonia;  character 
and  resources.  1914.  111186 

"A  study  of  the  elements  of  development  in  the 
region  tributary  to  the  National  Railway  from  Port 
San  Antonio  to  Lago  Nahuel  Huapi  and  the  exten- 
sion to  Valdiva,  Chile;  including  the  Andean  lake 
district. 

Paris,  John  T.  Seeing  the  eastern  states. 
1922. 


Panamqric  sketches  of  scenic  and  historical  inter- 
est, starting  with  Maine  and  working  south. 

Graham,  R.  B.  Cunninghame.  The  con- 
quest of  New  Granada;  being  the  life  of 
Gonzalo  Jimenez  de  Quesada.  1922. 

C  24972 

Not  only  valuable  for  important  information  about 
Colombia  and  its  explorer  but  equally  interesting  as 
a  dramatic  narrative.  The  stark  realism  of  death 
and  pestilence  and  the  heroic  accomplishment  of 
stupendous  tasks  form  a  story  of  pure  romance. 


James,    George    Wharton. 
of    blossoming    valleys. 


Utah,    the    land 
1922.         111777 


"The  story  of  its  desert  wastes,  of  its  huge  and 
fantastic  rock  formations,  and  of  its  fertile  gardens 
in  the  sheltered  valleys;  a  survey  of  its  rapidly 
developing  industries;  an  account  of  the  origin, 
development,  and  beliefs  of  the  Mormon  church; 
and  _  chapters  on  the  flora  and  fauna,  and  on  the 
scenic  wonders  that  are  a  heritage  of  all  Americans."

Raskob-Green  record  book.     1921.     C  20405 

A  genealogy  of  the  Raskob  and  Green  families 
of  Maryland. 

Rosenberge.r,  Jesse  Leonard.  Through 
three  centuries;  Colver  and  Rosenberger 
lives  and  times,  1620-1922.  1922.  C  20408 

Contents:  Colvers  and  New  England  in  early 
days. — Rev.  Nathaniel  Colver,  D.  D.,  and  his  day. — 
The  life  of  Rev.  Charles  Kendrick  Colver.— The 
life-story  of  Susan  Esther  Colver. — Jesse  Leonard 
Rosenberger  and  his  times. 

Eight  members  of  the  Colver  family  have  con- 
tributed to  this  genealogy.  Courage,  determina- 
tion and  adventure  were  leading  characteristics  of 
these  pioneers,  while  the  growth  of  the  Baptist 
faith  in  this  country  owes  much  to  their  services 
and  devotion.

Individual  Biography 

Baring,  Maurice.  The  puppet  show  of 
memory.  1922.  C  20751 

The  author  has  written  his  reminiscences  in  a 
most  engaging  manner.  He  covers  a  variety  of 
subjects  but  reaches  the  heights  in  his  chapter  on 
the  fascination  of  Russia. 

Chimay,  Princess  do.  A  daughter  of  Na- 
poleon; memoirs  of  Emilie  de  Pellapra, 
Comtesse  de  Brigode,  Princess  de  Chi- 
may, with  an  introduction  by  Princess 
Bibesco.  1922.  C 21225 

Memoirs  9f  an  illegitimate  daughter  of  Napoleon. 
Her  impressions  of  an  unhappy  childhood,  her  many 
suitors,  her  marriage  at  the  age  of  sixteen  to  M. 
de  Brigode  who  was  old  enough  to  be  her  father, 
his  death  and  her  second  marriage,  are  simply  and 
naturally  told. 

Duncan,  Joseph.  PUTNAM,  ELIZABETH  DUN- 
CAN. The  life  and  services  of  Joseph 
Duncan,  governor  of  Illinois,  1834-1838. 
1922.  C  21657 

Farquhar,  A.  B.  The  first  million  the  hard- 
est; an  autobiography,  in  collaboration 
with  Samuel  Crowther.  1922.  C  21869 

The  author  is  a  wealthy  manufacturer  of  farm 
machinery  who  began  life  as  a  poor  boy,  and  his 
book  is  of  special  interest  as  a  record  of  the 
development  of  the  present  complex  social  and  in- 
dustrial conditions  from  pioneer  beginnings. 

Grant,  Ulysses  S.  JONES,  FRANK  H.  An 
address  delivered  before  the  Chicago 
historical  society  at  the  celebration  of 
the  100th  anniversary  of  the  birth  of 
General  Grant,  Thursday,  April  27.  1922. 

C 22159 

Lloyd  George,  David.  PUNCH.  Lloyd 
George  by  Mr.  Punch.  1922.  C  23023 
This  collection  of  cartoons  which  traces  the  career 
of  the  "little  Welshman"  from  his  first  entrance 
into  political  life  to  the  present  ...  is  an  unfail- 
ingly good  humored,  even  if  sometimes  critical, 


portrayal  of  his  evolution  as  a  statesman. — Literary 
Review. 

Mainwaring,  Sir  Henry.  MANWARING,

A  3389,  54,  56 

(Publications  of  the  Navy  Records  Society.) 

Moore,  George.  FREEMAN,  JOHN.  A  por- 
trait of  George  Moore  in  a  study  of  his 
work.  1922.  '  C  23461 

An  introspective  analysis  of  George  Moore 
through  his  novels,  his  confessions  and  his  life.  _It 
will  appeal  to  his  admirers  and  serve  as  an  in- 
troduction to  those  who  do  not  know  him.

The  interesting  biography  of  a  New  England

Chronicles 
C  23849 


Saint-Saens,  Camille.  HERVEY,  ARTHUR. 
Saint-Saens.  1922.  C  24063 

Reveals  how  from  his  earliest  youth  Saint-Saens 
showed  his  marvelous  musical  talent  and  tells  of 
his  friendships  for  Berlioz,  Wagner,  Gounod  and 
Liszt.  The  book  further  contains  an  analysis  of 
his  compositions  and  of  his  literary  works,  espe- 
cially those  of  musical  criticism. 

St.  Vincent,  John  Jervis,  Earl  of.  Letters 
of  admiral  of  the  fleet,  the  Earl  of  St. 
Vincent,  whilst  first  lord  of  the  admiral- 
ty, 1801-1804;  ed.  by  David  Bonner  Smith. 
1922.  A  3389,55 

(Publications  of  the  Navy  Records   Society.) 

Salisbury,  Robert,  Marquis  of.  CECIL,  Lady 
GWENDOLEN.  Life  of  Robert,  marquis  of 
Salisbury.  1921.  2v.  C  24064 

v.  1.   1830-1868. 

2.   1868-1880. 

The  life  here  presented  is  not  merely  adequate; 
in  its  obvious  honesty,  its  sustained  vigor  and 
interest,  its  courage  and  conviction,  it  is  worthy 
of  its  subject — and  that  is  high  praise. — Literary 
Review. 

Wells,  H.  G.  DARK,  SIDNEY.  An  outline 
of  Wells,  the  superman  in  the  street; 
with  an  introduction  by  Heywood  Broun. 
1922.  C  24782 

The  life  and  character  of  this  many-sided  man 
as  made  known  through  a  wide  familiarity  with  his 
works  is  presented  in  a  journalistic  style  noteworthy 
for  clearness  and  vigor.

Sergt.  York  and  his  country"  (Chicago  Post).

HISTORY 

Europe,  United  States 

Guest,  L.  Haden.  The  struggle  for  power 
in  Europe,  1917-1921;  an  outline  econo- 
mic and  political  survey  of  the  central 
states  and  Russia.  1921.  A  5973 

Hanford,  James  Holly.  Our  heritage;  a 
Situdy  through  literature  of  the  American 
tradition.  1920.  .  B  5707 

(North   Carolina   University   extension   leaflets.) 

The  pomp  of  power.     1922.  A  7203 

Military  and  diplomatic  disclosures,  together  with 
sketches  of  the  leading  war  and  post-war  figures 
of  Great  Britain,  France  and  America,  by  a  man 
intimately  associated  with  Lloyd  George  in  war  and 
in  peace. — Publishers'  Weekly. 

Robinson,  James  Harvey,  and  Breasted, 
James  Henry.  A  general  history  of  Eu- 
rope, from  the  origins  of  civilization  to 
the  present  time;  with  the  collaboration 
of  Emma  Peters  Smith.  1921.  A  5974 

A  statement  of  the  conditions  under  which  men 
lived  for  long  periods,  of  the  ideas  which  they  held, 
and  of  the  manner  in  which  conditions  and  ideas 
have  undergone  great  changes  in  man's  slow  rise 
from  his  original  savage  estate. — Literary  Review. 

Skinner,  Alanson.  Material  culture  of  the 
Menomini.  1921.  B  8505 

(Indian   notes   and   monographs.) 

FICTION 

Armstrong,  Harold  Hunter.  For  richer, 
for  poorer.  1922.  F 10666 

Story  of  a  young  and  ineffectual  lawyer  who 
marries  a  capable  and  not  unbeautiful  girl,  and  of 
their  struggles,  both  marital  and  financial.  Through 
his  wife's  illness  the  husband  finally  shows  himself 
a  man.  By  the  author  of  Zell  (F  10403),  now 
using  his  real  name. 

Balmer,  Edwin.  The  breath  of  scandal. 
1922.  •  F  20183 

The  scene  is  laid  in  Eyanston  and  Chicago. 
Marjorie  Hale,  a  sheltered  girl,  discovers  that  her 
father  whom  she  thought  above  reproach  was  living 
a  double  life.  The  book  shows  how  she  readjusted 
'herself  to  others  after  learning  about  her  father. 

Banning,  Margaret  Culkin.  Spellbinders. 
1922.  F 20210 

Helen  Flandon  who  is  a  member  of  a  feminist 
organization,  is  confronted  by  her  husband's  jeal- 
ousy of  her  outside  interests.  This  is  typical  of 
the  problem  many  couples  are  facing  today. 


Benet,    Stephen    Vincent, 
pride;   a  novel.     1922. 


Young    people's 
F 20381 


A  swiftly  moving  story  of  young  people,  their 
problems,  ambitions  and  their  loves,  against  a  back- 
ground of  literature  and  art. 

Bindloss,  Harold.     Northwest!     1922. 

F  11132 

The  hero  of  this  story  sows  pecks  of  wild  oats 
before  he  finally  settles  down,  marries  a  pretty 
Canadian  girl,  and  goes  back  to  England  where  an 
inheritance  awaits  him. 

Bower,  B.  M.,  pseud,  of  Mrs.  Bertha  M.  Sin- 
clair.    The  trail  of  the  white  mule.     1922. 

F  18168 


Sequel   to   Casey   Ryan,    F  18167. 

In  this  story,  Casey  attempts  city  life,  but  the 
traffic  cops  are  such  a  restraining  influence  that 
he  heads  back  to  the  desert  to  get  a  breath  of  air. 
There  he  is  accused  of  moqnshining,  vindicates 
himself,  and  becomes  a  prohibition  agent. 

Bruun,  Laurids.  The  promised  isle,  trans- 
lated from  the  Danish.  1922.  F  20755 

Sequel  to   Van   Zanten's  happy  days,   F  20754. 

A  satirical  tale  of  three  young  Dutch  artists — 
a  poet,  painter  and  musician — who  go  to  an  unin- 
habited island  in  the  South  Seas  to  avoid  society. 
After  a  year  of  adventure,  they  become  homesick 
and  finally  return  to  home  and  fame. 

Buchholtz,  Johannes.  The  miracles  of 
Clara  Van  Haag;  translated  from  ithe 
Danish.  1922.  F  20773 

This  sequel  to  Egholm  and  his  God  (F  20772) 
continues  the  life  of  the  Egholm  family  in  the 
little  village  of  Knarreby,  to  which  comes  Fru 
Clara,  a  girl  sweetheart  of  the  inventor,  who  en- 
deavors to  help  the  impoverished  family.  Although 
the  characters  seem  unreal,  they  are  simple  and 
pathetic  and  curiously  appealing. 

Cabell,  James  Branch.  Gallantry;  dizain  des 
fetes  galantes.  1922.  F 11525 

This  is  a  revised  edition  of  an  earlier  work.  The 
English  and  French  setting  in  the  Jacobite  period 
gives  plenty  of  opportunity  for  Cabell's  colorful  and 
sparkling  romance. 

Gather,  Willa.     One  of  ours.     1922.    F 11650 

A  war  story,  written  to  show  that  the  boys  who 
died  in  the  height  of  their  idealistic  sacrifice  are 
much  better  off  than  those  who  returned  from 
overseas  to  the  disillusionment  of  post-war  condi- 
tions. The  first  half  showing  the  hero's  background 
previous  to  the  war  is  Miss  Gather  at  her  best. 


Comstock,    Harriet  T. 
1922. 


At    the    crossroads. 
F 21329 


To  recover  his  lost  health  a  young  writer  leaves 
home  and  settles  in  a  village  at  the  cross  roads 
where  he  finally  regains  interest  in  life  through 
Mary  Clare,  a  beautiful  character  surrounded  by 
mystery.  After  his  return  from  the  war  a  solution 
is  reached  which  permits  a  culmination  of  the 
romance. 

Crane,    Stephen.      Men,    women    and   boats. 
1921.  F 21401 

(The  modern  library  of  the  world's  best  literature.) 

Contents:    Stephen  Crane:  an  estimate. — The  open 

*    boat. — The     reluctant     voyagers. — The    end     of    the 

battle. — The   upturned   face. — An   episode   of  war. — 

An    experiment    in    misery. — The   duel    that   was    not 

fought. — A      desertion. — A      dark-brown      dog. — The 

pace    of   youth. — Sullivan    County    sketches:    A    tent 

in    agony.      Four    men    in    a    cave.      The    mesmeric 

mountain. — The    snake. — London    impressions. — The 

Scotch   express. 

Crane  stands  alone  as  a  realist  who  was  able  to 
bring  both  bitterness  and  humor  to  the  one  end 
of  hard,  unrelenting,  and  at  the  same  time  poetic 
and  not  wholly  unkind  irony. — Chicago  Evening 
Post. 

Curwood,  James   Oliver.     The   country  be- 
yond; a  romance  of  the  wilderness.     1922. 

F 21421 

A  stirring  romance  of  the  Canadian  wilds,  in 
which  the  hero,  in  escaping  from  the  Northwest 
Mounted  Police,  meets  with  the  most  hair-raising 
experiences. 

Dell,  Ethel  M.     Charles  Rex.    1922.    F  21549 

Sequel  to   The  hundredth   chance,   F  12464. 

A  noble,  ugly,  wicked,  sinister  hero  comes  to  the 
rescue  of  an  ill-treated  bell-boy  in  a  Monte  Carlo 
hotel.  On  the  way  home  they  are  shipwrecked,  and 


BOOK  BULLETIN 


173 


the  boy  of   14  _turns  out   to   be  a  girl   of   19.      The 
usual  complications  follow  and  all   ends   happily. 

D'Ennery,  Adolphe,  pseud,  of  Eugene  Phil- 
lippe.     The    two    orphans.  F 16247 

Dennis,  Geoffrey.    Mary  Lee.    1922.    F  21561 

The  story  of  a  middle  nineteenth-century  child 
whose  environment  is  one  of  extreme  religious 
fanaticism.  Through  her  agony  of  body  and  soul, 
her  inner  life  expands  and  grows  richer.  Unusually 
powerful  for  a  first  novel. 

Derieux,    Samuel    A.      Frank    of    Freedom 
Hill.      1922.  F  21566 

Stories  of  a  brown  setter,  his  master,  and  Tommy, 
his  playmate.  It  is  better  than  the  average  animal 
book  as  the  dog  is  not  given  exaggerated  human 
qualities. 

Farjeon,    Eleanor.      Martin    Pippin    in    the 
apple    orchard.      1922.  F 12826 

A  fairy  tale  for  grown-ups.  The  story  is  founded 
on  an  old  (Sussex  folk  song,  "The  spring-green 
lady,"  and  contains  six  episodes.  The  style  is 
unusually  beautiful  and  will  be  appreciated  by  ad- 
mirers of  De  la  Mare,  for  its  delicate  whimsicality 
resembles  him. 

Fox,    John,    jr.      A    Cumberland    vendetta. 

F8714 

A  knight  of  the  Cumberland   (Hell  fer 

sartain).  F  13033 

These  titles  are  here  repeated  as  a  correction   to 

bring    the    entries  in    the   main    Fiction    catalog    up 
to  date. 

Gabriel,  Gilbert  W.     Jiminy.     1922. 

F  22327 

A  delightful,  whimsical  little  story  of  a  young 
poetess  and  her  artist  husband  who  seek  for  the 
lost  sonnets  of  Raphael  as  a  symbol  of  love  and 
happiness. 

Grey,  Zane.     The  day  of  the  beast.     1922. 

F  22616 

Zane  Grey  departs  from  his  usual  western  tale 
in  the  powerful  story  dedicated  to  "the_  American 
men  who  gave  themselves  to  the  service  in  the 
great  war."  It  is  a  study  of  after-war  conditions, 
devoting  particularly  harsh  attention  to  the  flapper. 

Hutchinson,  A.  S.  M.     This  freedom.     1922. 

F  23301 

The  story  of  a  woman  who  continued  her  busi- 
ness career  after  her  marriage,  with  disastrous 
results  to  her  home  and  family.  It  opens  most 
charmingly  with  her  childhood  in  rural  England. 

Hutchinson,  Vere.     Sea  wrack.     1922. 

F 23305 

The  lives  of  two  foundlings  furnish  the  story 
which  is  laid  in  northern  England  with  the  atmos- 
phere of  a  sea  coast  village.  As  a  first  novel  it 
has  both  power  and  originality  and  is  an  altogether 
creditable  achievement  from  the  sister  of  A.  S.  M. 
Hutchinson. 


Jackson,  Charles  Tenney. 
1922. 


Captain  Sazarac. 
F 14302 


Relates  the  strange  plot  to  rescue  Napoleon  from 
St.  Helena  formed  in  New  Orleans  about  a  century 
ago.  However,  the  ship  changes  its  mission  and 
goes  on  an  expedition  of  adventure.  Conspiracy, 
mutiny,  Spanish  encounters,  and  the  rescue  of  a 
beautiful  girl  from  a  British  ship  supply  the  action. 


Jepson,   Selwyn. 
1922. 


The   qualified  adventurer. 
F  24070 


The  adventures  of  a  man  _who  himself  was  an 
expert  in  adventure-story  writing.  Fate  places  him 
on  board  a  ship  bound  for  China  in  search  of  lost 


treasure,  where  he  experiences  a  variety  of  adven- 
ture and  romance.  Humor,  wit  and  thrills  combine 
to  give  the  reader  good  entertainment. 

Keable,  Robert.     The  mother  of  all  living; 
a  novel  of  Africa.     1922.  F  24167 

Better  artistically  and  technically  than  Simon 
called  Peter.  The  characters  are  English,  the  set- 
ting South  Africa,  which  gives  Mr.  Keable  ample 
opportunity  for  beautiful  descriptions,  of  which  he 
is  a  master. 

Kyne,  Peter  B.     Cappy  Ricks  retires.    1922. 

F 14607 

Sequel  to   Cappy   Ricks,   F  14590. 

Incidents  in  the  life  of  this  old  sea-dog  who 
decides  many  times  to  quit  the  sea.  The  New  York 
Times  likens  this  quaint  old  man's  retirements  to 
a  prima  donna's  farewell  tours — they  don't  happen. 

Laing,  Janet.     Wintergreen;   a  tale   of   the 
reconstruction.     1922.  F  24379 

The  book  shows  what  idle  gossip  can  _  do,  and 
how  a  strong  character  can  solve  many  difficulties. 
Wintergreen,  a  middle-aged  woman  posing  as  a 
servant,  brings  about  a  general  reconciliation. 

Lewis,  Sinclair.  Babbitt.  1922.  '  F  24519 
A  cruel  and  heartless  but  distinctly  humorous 
characterization  of  a  typical  middle-class  American 
business  man  written  by  the  author  of  Main  Stree_t, 
who  has  been  called  "the  greatest  photographer  in 
America." 


McCutcheon,    George    Barr. 
1922. 


Viola    Gwyn. 
F  24826 


Romance  of  early  Indiana,  containing  a  little 
mystery,  a  villain  of  course,  and  a  most  beautiful 
hero  and  heroine. 


Machen,   Arthur. 


The   secret  glory.     1922. 
F  24851 


Selections  from  the  notebook  of  Ambrose  Mey- 
rick,  a  dreamer,  form  the  high  light  of  the  book. 
It  is  a  peculiar  combination  of  satire  on  English 
public  schools  and  mysticism  from  the  author  of 
The  angels  of  Mons. 

McKenna,   Stephen.     The   confessions    of  a 
well-meaning  woman.     1922.  F  24860 

A  clever  story  told  mostly  in  letters,  in  which 
Lady  Ann  in  reduced  circumstances  tries  to  marry 
off  her  good-for-nothing  son  to  a  large  amount  of 
money. 

Maran,  Rene.     Batouala.     1922.          F  24956 

An  impression  of  negro  life  in  the  French  Congo, 
as  seen  through  negro  eyes.     "Itdoes  not  accuse 
"it  records."     According  to  the  Nation  it  is  fiercely 
though  naively  animal.     The  book  was  awarded  the 
Goncourt  prize  of   1921   for  its  originality. 


Marshall,  Edison. 
1922. 


The  sky  line  of  spruce. 
F 24980 


Another  wild  and  woolly  western  story  set  in 
British  Colombia.  A  shell-shocked  ex-soldier  who 
had  lost  his  memory  is  discovered  in  a  prison  gang 
by  an  old  friend  who  works  the  governor  for  a 
oardon.  There  is  a  wolf-dog  who  is  not  the  least 


pa 

interesting   character. 

Maxwell,   W.    B. 

1922. 


Spinster    of   this    parish. 
F 25026 


A  woman  brought  up  in  a  Victorian  atmosphere 
falls  in  love  with  an  explorer  tied  to  an  insane 
wife  Throwing  off  the  bonds  of  convention  she 
follows  him  across  the  Andes.  The  book  portrays 
the  conflict  between  the  new  and  the  old  order 
of  things,  also  presenting  a  phase  of  the  English 
divorce  problem. 

Norris,    Kathleen.      Certain    people    of    im- 
portance.    1922.  F  25613 


174 


THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


A  story  of  the  fortunes  of  the  Crabtree  family 
in  their  migrations  from  New  England  to  Cali- 
fornia. A  remarkable  story  with  much  careful 
character  drawing  and  a  detailed  picture  of  life 
in  San  Francisco  in  the  last  part  of  the  nineteenth 
century. 


Phillpotts,  Eden. 


Saltus,  Edgar.     The  ghost  girl. 


Pan  and  the  twins.     1922. 
F 16301 

A  pleasant  result  of  his  exercising  his  imagina- 
tion, his  philosophy,  and  his  sense  of  humor.  There 
is  a  strong  philosophical  slant  to  the  book,  but  one 
never  loses  sight  of  the  twinkle  in  the  author's 
eye.^Bookman. 

Punshon,  E.  R.     The  Bittermeads  mystery. 
1922.  F 16691 

The  usual  type  of  sensational  mystery  story  only 
a  trifle  more  bloodthirsty,  where  the  villain  is 
made  to  suffer,  the  rightful  heir  gains  his  own, 
and  the  hero  marries  the  much  abused  stepdaughter. 

Reed,  Charles  Bert.     Duke.     1922.       E  5377 

(Chicago   Literary   Club   papers.) 
Story  of  a   dog. 

Sabatini,  Rafael.     Captain  Blood,  his  Odys- 
sey.    1922.  F  17563 

A  splendid  pirate  yarn  of  the  days  of  James  II. 
Peter  Blood,  physician,  becomes  Peter  Blood,  pirate, 
and  a  wonderful  one  he  is.  His  merits  as  a  seaman 
finally  win  him  a  commission  from  the  king,  a 
governorship,  and — the  girl. 

1922. 
F 17603 

A  mystery  tale  of  exceptional  brilliance  as  to 
style  and  unusual  interest  as  to  plot.

Seltzer,   Charles  Alden.     West!     1922. 

F 17917 

A  western  story  well  loaded  with  gunpowder  and 
plentifully  sprinkled  with  corpses.  The  main  idea 
of  the  book  seems  to  be  the  conflict  between  the 
standards  and  ideas  of  East  and  West. 

Stocking,    Charles    Francis.      Thou    Israel. 
1921.  F 18405 

A  dramatically  written  Christian  Science  •  story  of 
the  mental  and  spiritual  struggle  of  a  wealthy  young 
Englishman  during  the  war.  His  intellectual  free- 
dom is  gained  through  the"  help  of  a  girl  of  rare 
spiritual  powers. 

Tarbell,    Ida    M.      He    knew    Lincoln,    and 
other  Billy  Brown  stories.     1922.      F  27695 


Contents:  He  knew  Lincoln. — Back  in  '58. — 
Father  Abraham. — In  Lincoln's  chair. 

The  simplicity,  straightforwardness,  kindness  and 
honesty  of  Mr.  Lincoln  shine  out  in  these  sketches 
with  a  brilliance  well  deserved. — Springfield  Re- 
public. 

Terhune,    Albert    Payson.      Further    adven- 
tures of  Lad.     1922.  F  27746 

Sequel  to  Lad:  a  dog,  F  18536. 

The  author's  love  and  understanding  of  dogs  is 
evident  in  this  continuation  of  the  adventures  of 
that  delightful  thoroughbred,  Lad. 


Tyson,   J.   Aubrey. 
1922.

F  27918 


A  vivid  and  thrilling  detect!

"This  is  a  swashbuckling  story  of  pirate  days 
.  .  .  Follow  and  you  shall  see  brave  adventuring,

Willsie,    Honore. 
valley.     1922. 


Judith    of    the    godless 
F 19592 


Judith  lived  among  the  mountains  of  the  South- 
west in  Lost  Chief  Valley  among  pioneer  conditions. 
She  craved  better  surroundings,  where  there  might 
be  room  for  beauty  and  ideals.  Her  seven  years 
of  struggle  were  endurable  only  because  she  had 
Douglas,  who  is  the  more  important  character. 

Young,  Francis   Brett.     The   red  knight;   a 
romance.     1922.  F 19941 

The  author  uses  a  Graustark  method  in  this 
romance  but  the  theme  is  modern  and  treats  of 
struggles  and  revolutions  in  the  imaginary  land  of 
Trinacria. 


YOUNG  PEOPLE'S  BOOKS 

Miscellaneous 

Belloc,  Hilaire.  More  beasts  for  worse 
children.  1922.  H  7342 

Sequel   to   The  "bad   child's   book    of   beasts. 

A  collection  of  animal  verses  by  the  author  of 
Cautionary  tales  for  children  (H  7343).  The  gro- 
tesque illustrations  are  as  interesting  as  the  ones 
in  the  earlier  book. 

Bragg,  Sir  William.  The  world  of  sound; 
six  lectures  delivered  before  a  juvenile 
auditory  at  the  Royal  Institution.  1922. 

K 19503 

They  treat  of  the  theory  of  sound,  which  is 
discussed  in  many  phases  such  as  sound  in  music, 
sounds  of  the  town,  the  part  sound  played  during 
the  war,  etc. 

Lafayette,  Marquis  de.  HOLLAND,  RUPERT 
SARGENT.  Lafayette,  for  young  Americans. 
1922.  C  22831 

It  gives  a  clear  and  interesting  account  of  France 
at  the  time  of  Lafayette.  For  the  children  in  the 
upper  grades. 

Quennell,  Marjorie  and  C.  H.  B.  Everyday 
life  in  the  old  stone  age.  1922.  K  20782 

(The   everyday   life   series.) 

A  popular  history  of  prehistoric  man  intended 
especially  to  appeal  to  youthful  readers.  The 
artistic  skill  of  men  living  hundreds  of  centuries 
ago  is  shown  in  the  many  quaint  illustrations  and 
will  surprise  and  entertain  them.

Books  Added  to  the  Library 

Books  marked  *  do  not  circulate.    Those  with  the  letter  P  are  in  the  Art  Room;  other 

collections  are  shelved  in  the  Civics  Department,    Music    Room   and   Thomas 

Hughes  Room  and  are  marked  accordingly.    *Ser.    denotes   serials 

which  do  not  circulate.

THE   CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


PHILOSOPHY 

Sinclair,  May.     The  new  idealism.   1922. 

L 13833 

Miss  Sinclair  published  a  "Defence  of  idealism" 
in  1917  against  the  "new  realism"  of  the  followers 
of  James,  Royce  and  Bergson  who  were  protesting 
against  the  tyrannies  of  formal  absolutism.  She 
bases  her  "new  idealism"  on  the  distinction  between 
primary  and  secondary  consciousness. 

Mental  Hygiene,  Psychoanalysis 

Paton,    Stewart.      Signs    of    sanity   and    the 
principles  of  mental  hygiene.     1922. 

L 13233 

The  author  discusses  mental  and  bodily  fitness 
and  how  they  are  to  be  secured.  He  brings  forward 
the  idea  that  civilization  can  be  controlled  only 
through  soundness  of  mind. 

Stekel,  William.     The   depths   of  the   soul; 
psycho-analytical    studies.      1922      L  13982 

Palmistry 

Rem,  Henri.     What  your  hand  reveals.     1922 

L 13465 

A  popularly  written  and  comprehensive  study  of 
the  hand  for  the  purpose,  says  the  author,  of  teach- 
ing us  to  know  ourselves  and  others,  "to  determine 
the  natural  talents  of  our  children  ;  to  raise  a  corner 
of  the  veil  which  hides  destiny  and  the  future." 

Ethics 
Courage,   Leadership 

Barrie,  J.    M.      Courage.     1922.  L 14162 

A  rectorial  address  delivered  at  St.  Andrews 
University  last  May.  The  Literary  Review  calls  it 
one  of  the  fine  essays  of  our  decade.  "It  is  an 
appeal  to  the  new  generation  to  go  into  partnership 
with  the  old  that  helped  bring  on  the  war  and  had 
to  endure  it." 


Slattery,  Margaret, 
ship.     1920. 


The  highway  to  leader- 
L  14144 


A  series  of  inspirational  essays  on  the  necessity 
oi  leadership  and  the  qualifications  of  a  true  leader. 

RELIGION 

Hastings,  James,  and  others,  ed.  Encyclo- 
paedia of  religion  and  ethics.  1908-1922. 
12v.  *R  3428 

This  valuable  work  of  reference  is  now  complete 
with  the  exception  of  the  index  volume  which  is  in 
preparation. 

McConnell,  Francis  John.  Church  finance 
and  social  ethics.  1920.  M  9539 

With  candor  and  consistency  all  too  rare,  the 
author  insists  that  church  funds  are  to  be  "socially 
justified"  no  less  exactingly  than  those  of  other 
vested  interests,  and  "without  wilfully  or  uncon- 
sciously squaring  to  donors'  points  of  view". — 
Survey. 

Meditations  on  the  life  and  passion  of 
Christ,  from  British  Museum  addit  ms- 
11307,  by  Charlotte  D'Evelyn.  1921. 

*J  1175, 158 

(Early  English  Text  Society.  Original  series.) 
"The  poem  which  is  printed  for  the  first  time  in  the 
following  pages,  might  be  described  briefly  as  a 
compendium  of  the  lyric  themes  of  Middle  English 
religious  poetry." 


Moxcey,  Mary  E.  Some  qualities  asso- 
ciated with  success  in  the  Christian  min- 
istry. 1922.  L  10284, 122 

(Columbia  University.  Teachers  College  contribu- 
tions to  education.) 

Spence,  Lewis.  An  introduction  to  my- 
thology. 1921.  L 21261 
A  treatise  on  comparative  mythology  from  the 
earliest  times  to  the  present,  defining  the  various 
kinds  of  myths  and  covering  a  wide  expanse  of 
material. 

Stout,  John  Elbert.  Organization  and  ad- 
ministration of  religious  education.  1922. 

L 19897 

(The  Abingdon  religious  education  texts.  Com- 
munity training  school  series.) 

Wilson,  L.  G.,  and  others.     The  church  and 

landless  men.     1921.  M  4472 

(University  of  North  Carolina  extension  bulletin.) 

THE   SOCIAL  SCIENCES 

Bonnett,  Clarence  E.  Employers'  associa- 
tions in  the  United  States;  a  study  of 
typical  associations.  1922.  L 16796 

A  detailed  study  of  thirteen  associations  classified 
into  three  types  with  reference  to  their  attitude 
toward  organized  labor.  Each  one  is  treated  in  its 
historical  as  well  as  its  immediately  pressing  prac- 
tical side. 

Brewer,  Daniel  Chauncey.  The  peril  of 
the  republic;  are  we  facing  revolution  in 
the  United  States?  1922.  L 17562 

Brandt,  Ralph  Van  Kirk  Von.  The  icon- 
oclast's message;  folio  no.  17-32.  1916- 
1917.  L 15401 

Essays  on  social  problems. 

Wingfield-Stratford,  Esme.  Facing  reality. 
1922.  L  15370 

The  author  feels  that  the  mental  and  moral  state 
of  society  today  is  at  low  ebb  and  far  behind  the 
progress  in  mechanics,  and  that  it  will  not  improve 
until  men  learn  to  think  for  themselves  instead  of 
molding  their  opinions  on  advertisements  and  the 
press. 

Woods,  Robert  A.,  and  Kennedy,  Albert  J. 
The  settlement  horizon;  a  national  esti- 
mate. 1922.  L 17155 

An  extremely  interesting  and  comprehensive 
treatment  of  the  settlement  .  .  .  and  a  much  needed 
statement  of  the  principles  behind  the  movement. — 
Nation. 

Economics 

Dickinson,  Zenas  Clark.  Economic  mo- 
tives; a  study  in  the  psychological  foun- 
dations of  economic  theory,  with  some 
reference  to  other  social  sciences.  1922. 

L  6587,24 

(Harvard  economic  studies.) 

Introductory  to  his  psychological  study  of  eco- 
nomics Professor  Dickinson  includes  a  very  fine 
sketch  of  the  recent  development  of  psychology  and 
its  applications. 

Edie,  Lionel  D.  Principles  of  the  new 
economics.  1922.  L 16567 

(Crowell's   social   science   series.)

Liber,  Benzion.  The  child  and  the  home; 
essays  on  the  rational  bringing-up  of  chil- 
dren. 1922.  L  14313 

Dr.  Liber  feels  that  the  restraint  practised  upon 
children  by  parents  is  much  too  great  and  results  in 
repression.  He  urges  freedom  for  the  child  to  work 
out  his  own  problems  and  difficulties.

Recreation,  Fishing 

Meyer,  Harold  D.  The  rural  playground. 
1921.  K 30493 

(University  of  North  Carolina  extension  bulletin.) 

Smith,  O.  W.    The  book  of  the  pike.     1922. 

K  30849 

A  well  planned  and  excellently  illustrated  book  for 
pursuers  of  the  pike,  pickerel  and  muskellunge. . . . 
We  are  told  how  to  fish  for  them — an  especial 
chapter  being  given  to  ice  ^fishing  and  another  to 
"the  fine  art  of  pike  cooking." — Chicago  Evening 
Post. 

LITERATURE 

Hibbard,   C.  A.     Studies   in   Southern   liter- 
ature;    a    program     for     women's     clubs. 
1922  J  7049 

(University  of  North  Carolina  extension  bulletin.) 

Manly,  John  Matthews,  and  Rickert,  Edith. 
Contemporary  American  literature,  bibli- 
ographies and  study  outlines.  1922.  J  7051 
An  excellent  reference  book  covering  more  im- 
portant writers  since  1914.  It  contains  a  short 
biography  of  each  author,  suggestions  for  reading, 
a  chronological  bibliography,  annotations,  and  many 
valuable  lists. 

Pattee,  Fred  Lewis.     Side  lights  on  Ameri- 
can   literature.      1922.  J  7050 
Contents:    The  age  of  O.   Henry. — A  critic  in  C 
major    (H.    L.    Mencken). — The   prophet  of  the   last 


frontier  (Jack  London). — The  epic  of  New  England. 
— On  the  terminal  moraine  of  New  England  Puri- 
tanism (Mary  E.  Wilkins  Freeman). — The  shadow 
of  Longfellow. — The  modernness  of  Philip  Freneau. 
— The  centenary  of  Bryant's  poetry. — Foe's  "Ula- 
lume". 

While  the  book  is  chiefly  a  literary  criticism  of 
various  American  writers,  we  are  also  given  inti- 
mate pictures  of  their  personal  lives,  especially  as 
their  circumstances  affected  their  literary  output. 
Mr.  Pattee  has  an  easy?  vivid,  narrative  style  which 
makes  interesting  reading. 

Saintsbury,  George,  ed.  A  letter  book; 
with  an  introduction  on  the  history  and 
art  of  letter-writing.  1922.  E  5564 

Has  used... the  best  letter-writers  but  not  their 
most  famous  letters.  His  book  is  not  to  be  im- 

R roved  upon.  Another  would  have  done  it  different- 
/;  no  one  else  alive  could  have  done  it  so  well, 
with  such  abundance  of  knowledge,  with  so  fine  a 
judgment. — Saturday  Review. 

Schelling,  Felix  E.  Appraisements  and  as- 
perities as  to  some  contemporary  writers. 
1922.  J  7991 

Serious  and  whimsical  essays  on  outstanding  con- 
temporary writers  in  drama,  poetry  and  criticism. 
They  go  more  thoroughly  into  the  personalities  and 
inherent  merit  of  the  authors  than  is  customary  in 
average  reviews. 

The  Scriptores  historiae  Augustae;  with 
an  English  translation.  1922.  L 12273 
(The  Loeb  classical  library.) 

Latin  and   English  text. 

Contents:  Hadrian. — Aelius. — Antoninus  Pius. — 
Marcus  Aurelius  Antoninus. — Lucius  Verus. — Avi- 
dius  Cassius. — Commodus. — Pertinax. — Didius  Ju- 
lianus. — Septimius  Severus. — Pescennius  Niger. — 
Clodius  Albinus. 

Authorship 

Clark,     Glenn.      A     manual     of     the     short 

story  art.      1922.  J  7768 

An   informing  book   full   of   constructive   criticism 

and  helpful  suggestions.     Mr.   Clark  is  obviously  in 

sympathy   with   struggling  young   authors. 

White,  Henry  Adelbert.  English  study  and 
English  writing.  1922.  J8110 

Poetry 

Anthony,     Edward.       Merry-go-roundelays. 

1921.  E 10628 
Many  of  his  verses  have  such  a  singable  quality 

that  the  reader  feels  like  playing  Sullivan  to  the 
author's  Gilbert  by  humming  to  the  words  an  ac- 
companiment of  his  own  origination. — Literary  Re- 
view. 

Conkling,  Hilda.     Shoes  of  the  wind.     1922. 

E  14548 

These  verses  by  one  who  is  still  a  little  girl  shows 
how  she  continues  to  develop  and  grow  since  writ- 
ing her  first  book  of  poems.  The  technique  is 
naturally  perfect  and  the  imagery  exquisite. 

Dargan,    Olive    Tilford.     Lute   and    furrow. 

1922.  .      E 14580 

A  book  of  striking  contrasts  as  its  name  implies. 
Here  are  lyrics  of  careful  technique,  and  many  of 
them  with  true  singing  rhythms,  and  here  are  also 
rugged  genre  pictures  of  life  and_  character  in  the 
southern  mountains  where  the  writer  spends  much 
of  her  life. — Chicago  Daily  News. 

Hardy,  Thomas.  Late  lyrics  and  earlier, 
with  many  other  verses.  1922.  E 14739 


190 


THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


The  poetry  would  in  itself  be  sufficient  in  quality 
and  quantity  to  give  Mr.  Hardy  high  rank  amongst 
our  immortals  had  he  never  written  a  line  of  prose. 
— London  Mercury. 

Lawrence,  D.  H.     Tortoises.  1921.     E  11180 

A  satire  in  free  verse  on  the  human  race  as 
symbolized  by  the  tortoise.  It  is  a  philosophical  and 
thought-provoking  piece  of  work. 

Le  Buffe,  Francis  P.    The  hound  of  heaven; 
an  interpretation.     1921.  E  8732 

Father  Le  Buffe  has  spent  many  years  of  thought 
on  the  poem  and  his  notes  show  it.  They  are 
clarifying  and  suggestive . . .  and  one  cannot  follow 
them  without  acquiring  a  deeper  love  and  under- 
standing of  Thompson's  masterpiece. — Springfield 
Republican. 

More,  Brookes.    The  beggar's  vision.     1921. 

E  14881 

These  poems  are  surrounded  by  an  atmosphere  of 
mysticism,  many  of  them  are  very  beautiful,  while 
their  workmanship  leaves  nothing  to  be  desired. 


Morley,  Christopher. 
Chinese.      1922. 


Translations  from  the 
E  14880 


This  free  verse  is  written  in  humorous  vein  and 
represents  thoughts  and  philosophies  which  Mr.  Mor- 
ley imagines  a  Chinese  mandarin  would  have  upon 
visiting  our  country  and  leading  a  typical  American 
life. 

Punch.  Poems  from  Punch,  1909-1920;  with 
an  introductory  essay  by  W.  B.  Drayton 
Henderson.  1922.  E  14372 

A  delightful  collection  of  light  verse  not  all 
comic  but  varied  here  and  there  by  a  gently  pensive 
or  romantic  touch.  There  are  also  a  few  poems 
which  strike  a  deeper  note,  as  "In  Flanders  fields" 
which  few  remember  appeared  first  in  Punch. 


West,  Henry  Litchfield,  comp. 
links.     1921. 


Lyrics  of  the 
E  14373 


A  collection  of  short  poems  and  parodies  for  golf 
fans,  touching  various  phases  of  their  interests. 
Among  the  authors  represented  are  John  Kendrick 
Bangs,  Edgar  A.  Guest,  Andrew  Lang,  and  Ring 
Lardner. 

Drama 

Alden,  Raymond   Macdonald.     Shakespeare. 
1922.  E 10041 

(Master  spirits  of  literature.) 

A  compendium  of  the  known  facts  regarding 
Shakespeare,  and  of  the  prevailing  critical  judgments 
of  modern  scholarship,  sufficiently  untechnical  for 
the  purposes  of  the  general  reader,  and  at  the  same 
time  uncolored  by  any  desire  to  prove  a  case. — 
Preface. 

The  best  plays  of  1921-1922,  and  the  year- 
book of  the  drama  in  America.     1922. 

E  13492, 1921-1922 

Contents:  Anna  Christie,  by  O'Neill. — A  bill  of 
divorcement,  by  Dane. — Dulcy,  by  Kaufman  and 
Connelly. — He  who  gets  slapped,  by  Andreyev. — Six 
cylinder  love,  by  McGuire. — The  hero,  by  Emery. — 
The  Dover  road,  by  Milne. — Ambush,  by  Richman. 
— The  circle,  by  Maugham. — The  nest,  by  Geraldy. 

Brooke,    Rupert.      Lithuania;    a    drama    in 
one   act.     1915.  E 13514 

(Stewart  Kidd  modern  plays.) 

A  tragedy  possessing  much  dramatic  power.  A 
long  lost  son  returns  unrecognized  to  his  desperate 
poverty-stricken  family.  The  action  then  moves 
swiftly  to  a  tense  and  disastrous  climax. 


Brooks,   Charles  S.     Frightful  plays!     1922. 

E  13515 

Contents:  By  way  of  explanation. — On  choosing 
a  title. — Wappin'  wharf. — At  the  sign  of  The 
Greedy  Pig. 

_  We  are  given  here  a  delightfully  quaint  combina- 
tion of  essay  and  drama.  It  is  a  book  to  read 
aloud  and  not  to  act,  as  in  production  the  author's 
humorous  comments  on  his  characters  would  be  lost. 


Chapman,    John    Jay. 
Shakespeare.      1922. 


A     glance     toward 
E 10062 


Mr.  Chapman  has  a  keen  eye  and  a  most  effec- 
tive style.  His  thirteen  essays  average  eight  pages 
apiece.  In  this  little  room  he  has  his  say  on  the 
plays  as  poetry,  and  the  plays  on  the  stage,  on  the 
comedies,  Shakespeare's  types,  the  sonnets,  and 
seven  or  eight  separate  dramas. — Literary  Review. 

Chekhov,  Anton.    Uncle  Vania;  scenes  from 
country  life  in  four  acts.     1922.       E  13176 
(Poet  Lore  plays.)

Agate,  James.  Alarums  and  excursions. 
1922.  E  1763 

A  collection  of  bright  entertaining  essays  dealing 
mostly  with  people  and  subjects  relating  to  the 
stage.  The  style  is  clever,  and  the  author's  in- 
dividuality evident  throughout, 

Broun,  Heywood,  and  others.  Nonsenseor- 
ship;  sundry  observations  concerning  pro- 
hibition, inhibitions  and  illegalities.  1922. 

E4765 

.  Contents:  We  have  with  us  today,  by  G.  P.  P. — 
Evolution,  another  of  those  outlines,  by  G.  S.  Chap- 
pell. — Nonsenseorship,  by  H.  Broun. — Literature 
and  the  bastinado,  by  B.  Hecht. — The  woman's 
place,  by  Ruth  Hale. — Owed  to  Volstead,  by  W. 
Irwin. — The  censorship  of  thought,  by  R.  Keable. — 
The  uninhibited  flapper,  by  Helen  B.  Lowry. — The 
wowzer  in  the  South  Seas,  by  F.  O'Brien. — Re- 
formers: a  hymn  of  hate,  by  Dorothy  Parker. — Pro- 
hibition, by  F.  Swinnerton. — A  guess  at  unwritten 
history,  by  H.  M.  Tomlinson. — In  vino  demi-tasse, 
by  C.  H.  Towne.— Bootleg,  by  John  V.  A.  Weaver. 
— And  the  playwright,  by  A.  Woollcott. — The  oracle 
that  always  says  "no",  by  the  author  of  the 
"Mirrors  of  Washington". 

A  collection  of  protestations  vs.  prohibitions  of 
various  sorts  by  some  of  the  cleverest  of  contem- 
porary writers. 


Burroughs,  John.     The  last 


harvest.     1922. 
E2131 

Contents:  Emerson  and  his  journals. — Flies  in 
amber. — Another  word  on  Thoreau. — A  critical 
glance  into  Darwin. — What  makes  a  poem? — Short 
studies  in  contrasts. — Day  by  day.— -Gleanings. — 
Sundown  papers. 

Posthumous  essays  including,  besides  papers  on 
Emerson,  Thoreau,  Darwin  and  Bergson,  philosoph- 
ical and  literary  reflections  which  possess  a  notable 
significance  as  the  latest  conclusions  of  a  long  life. 

Egan,  Maurice  Francis.  Confessions  of  a 
book-lover.  1922.  J  7648 

Contents:  My  boyhood  reading. — Poets  and 
poetry. — Certain  novelties. — Letters,  biographies, 
and  memoirs. — Books  at  random. 

Here  is  a  pleasant  volume,  indeed.  In  it  a  fine 
scholar  with  a  gentle  fancy  sets  down  his  memories 
of  books  in  boyhood  and  age. — Chicago  Evening 
Post. 

Masson,  Thomas  L.,  comb.  Listen  to  these. 
1922.  E4457 

Mr.  Masson  was  managing  editor  of  Life  for 
twenty-five  years  so  his  experience  justifies  and 
equips  him  in  publishing  collections  of  funny 
stories  like  this. 

Mencken,  H.  L.  Prejudices;  third  series. 
1922.  J  6439,  3 

Mr.  Mencken  delights  in  irritating  his  readers 
and  this  book,  which  will  be  devoured  as  eagerly 
as  the  others,  is  no  exception. 

Traprock,  Walter  E.,  pseud  of  George  Shep- 
ard  Chappell.  My  northern  exposure;  the 
Kawa  at  the  Pole.  1922.  E2140 

A  clever  parody  of  recent  North  Pole  literature, 
very  bright  and  funny  in  spots,  which  will  be  ap- 
preciated by  people  who  read  the  Polar  books.

lanies: Wilde  v.  Whistler;  Impressions 
of  America;  Sebastian  Melmoth.  1920.

book  is  worthy  of  wide  distribution  for  the  infor- 
mation it  carries  in  brief  form  and  for  its  incen- 
tive to  our  many  foreign  citizens. — Springfield  Re- 
publican. 

Wade,  Mary  H.     Real  Americans.     1922. 

C  20124 

Contents:  Theodore  Roosevelt. — Herbert  Clark 
Hoover. — Leonard  Wood. — John  Burroughs. — Mark 
Twain. — Edward  Everett  Hale. 

Individual   Biography 

Burroughs,  John,  JOHNSON,  CLIFTON.  John 
Burroughs  talks;  his  reminiscences  and 
comments.  1922.  C  21071 

Much  of  the  book  is  reminiscent,  but  there  are 
also  comments  that  run  far  afield  into  the  realms 
of  politics,  religion,  philosophy,  science  and  litera- 
ture. 

De  Morgan,  William  and  Evelyn.  STIRLING, 
A.  M.  W.  William  De  Morgan  and  his 
wife.  1922.  C  21565 

A  biography  of  unusual  interest  on  several  counts. 
It  is  written  by  the  sister  of  Mrs.  DeMorgan,  a 
pre-Raphaelite  artist,  and  besides  giving  a  most  sat- 
isfying report  of  DeMorgan's  literary  period,  does 
full  justice  to  his  life  work  as  inventor  and  Eng- 
land's greatest  ceramic  artist. 

Longfellow,  Ernest  Wadsworth.  Random 
memories.  1922.  C  23032 

An  anecdotal  record  by  the  artist  son  of  Henry 
W.  Longfellow.  In  it  he  describes  his  impressions 
on  art  and  literature,  which  verge  unmistakably  on 
the  Victorian. 

Mitchell,  Donald  G.  DUNN,  WALDO  H.  The 
life  of  Donald  G.  Mitchell,  Ik  Marvel. 
1922.  C  23407 

Attaining  distinction  as  an  actor,  practical  farmer, 
landscape-gardener,  art  critic  and  author.  Ik  Marvel 
passed  the  greater  part  of  his  life  in  the  retirement 
of  Edgewood  Farm  near  New  Haven. 

Morgenthau,  Henry.    All  in  a  life-time.    1922. 

C  23476 

The  autobiography  of  the  man  who  admirably 
filled  the  difficult  post  of  American  representative 
in  Constantinople,  1914-1917. 

Page,  Walter  H.  HENDRICK,  BURTON  J.  The 
life  and  letters  of  Walter  H.  Page.  1922. 
2v.  C  23665 


This  journalist,  ambassador,  and  eminent  student 
of  government  and  of  men,  has  given  us  in  his  let- 
ters at  once  a  valuable  and  scholarly  contribution 
to  American  letters,  and  an  authoritative  book  on 
the  great  war.

phy. 


FRANK,  TENNEY. 
1922. 


Vergil,   a  biogra- 
C  24700 


Sensible  research — first  staff  of  criticism — has 
made  it  possible  for  Tenney  Frank,  in  his  compact, 
spare,  fascinating  Vergil  to  make  the  saint  and 
wizard  out  a  human  being  and  a  comprehensible 
artist. — Nation.

Europe 

B.     The  blocking  of  Zee- 
A7205 


A  complete  and  accurate  account  of  a  thrilling 
sea  adventure,  stirringly  told  by  the  leader  of  the 
expedition. 

Cresson,  W.  P.  The  holy  alliance;  the  Eu- 
ropean background  of  the  Monroe  doc- 
trine. 1922.  A  5975 

(Publications  of  the  Carnegie  endowment  for  in- 
ternational peace.) 

Elliot,  Sir  Henry  G.  Some  revolutions  and 
other  diplomatic  experiences.  1922. 

A  5978 

The  reminiscences  have  been  compiled  from  a 
diary  and  private  letters  and  give  an  account  of 
various  diplomatic  experiences,  beginning  as  attache 
to  the  embassy  at  St.  Petersburg  in  1841  and  con- 
tinuing as  minister  to  Naples,  Athens,  Turin  and 
Constantinople,  up  to  1876. — Book  Review  Digest. 

Gilliard,  Pierre.  Thirteen  years  at  the  Rus- 
sian court;  a  personal  record  of  the  last 
years  and  death  of  the  Czar  Nicholas  II. 
and  his  family.  1922.  A  7875 

It  is  a  vivid  record  which  has  been  written  by  a 
former  tutor  in  the  Czar's  court.  He  knew  Nicholas 
intimately  and  admired  him,  but  mentions  his  lack 
of  force.  An  interesting  and  authoritative  account. 

Gibbons,  Herbert  Adams.  An  introduction 
to  world  politics.  1922.  A  5976 

(The  Century  political  science  series.) 
Dr.  Gibbons'  book  is  notable  for  the  thoroughness 
of  its  presentation  and  a  scientifically  objective 
attitude  that  makes  intelligible  the  actions  of  Ger- 
many and  others  during  the  World  War.— Book- 
man. 


Mowrer,     Edgar    Ansel. 
1922. 


Immortal     Italy. 
A  5113 


"A  history  of  modern  Italy  from  the  formation 
of  the  United  Kingdom  in  1870,  through  the  war, 
to  a  summary  of  present  problems." — Booklist. 

Zimmern,   Alfred    E.      Europe    in   convales- 
cence.    1922.  A  5977 

A  discussion  of  political  conditions  in  England 
from  prewar  times.  The  author  feels  the  serious- 
ness of  the  situation  and  believes  that  Europe's 
hope  lies  in  the  young  men  who  have  lived  through 
the  war  and  see  things  as  they  are.

Hebard,  Grace  Raymond,  and  Brininstool, 
E.  A.  The  Bozeman  trail;  historical  ac-

routes  into  the  northwest  and  the  fights

Lathrop,  Stanley  Edwards.  A  historical 
sketch  of  the  "Old  Mission,"  and  its  mis- 
sionaries to  the  Ojibway  Indians,  on  Ma- 
deline Island,  Lake  Superior,  Wisconsin. 
1905.  B  4689 

Mace,  William  H.,  and  Bogardus,  Frank  S. 
Mace-Bogardus  history  of  the  United 
States.  1921.  B  5851 

Van  Tyne,  Claude  H.  The  causes  of  the 
war  of  independence;  being  the  first  vol- 
ume of  a  history  of  the  founding  of  the 
American  republic.  1922.  B  6016, 1 

An  impartial  study  in  which  the  historian  works 
from  a  psychological  basis;  and  instead  of  writing 
from  the  viewpoint  of  the  lovers  or  detractors  of 
England  today,  he  has  succeeded  in  preserving  a 
distinctly  18th  century  attitude. 

FICTION 

Andrews,  Nelson.  Finding  youth;  a  human 
experience.  1922.  F 10581 

Having  lost  interest  in  life  a  man  of  sixty  finds 
himself  old  and  laid  on  the  shelf.  He  suddenly 
braces  up,  takes  a  job  and  finds  his  life  richer 
and  fuller  than  it  had  ever  been  before. 

Angellotti,  Marion  Polk.  Three  black  bags. 
1922.  F 10578 

A  mystery  story  based  on  a  plot  to  reinstate  the 
German  Emperor  after  the  Armistice.  "Written  by 
an  author  with  a  real  knack  for  the  contriving 
and  the  working  out  of  a  tale  of  complications, 
adventures,  perils,"  says  the  New  York  Times. 

Austen,  Jane.  Love  and  freindship,  and 
other  early  works  now  first  published 
from  the  original  ms.  1922.  F  20066 

Contents:  Love  and  freindship. — Lesley  Castle. 
— The  history  of  England. — Collection  of  letters. 
— Scraps. 

These  precocious  burlesques  show  us  a  youthful 
Jane  Austen,  differing  in  many  ways  from  our  old 
conception  of  her. 


Belloc,     Hilaire. 
1922. 


The     mercy     of     Allah. 
F  20364 


An  aged,  profusely  religious  Mohammedan  re- 
lates to  his  penniless  nephews  the  daring  exploits 
and  shrewd  graft  by  which  he  amassed  his  huge 
fortune.  An  unusual  story  told  in  a  clever  satiri- 
cal vein. 


Benson,   E.   F.     Peter.     1922. 


F 20390 


A  story  in  which  the  author  draws  a  striking 
contrast  between  the  young  people  of  England  and 
their  elders,  much  to  the  latter's  disadvantage.  The 
characterizations  are  well  drawn  in  a  delicate  and 
finished  style. 

Bottome,  Phyllis.     The  kingfisher.     1922. 

F  20645 

An  ex-convict,  befriended  by  a  clergyman  and 
sent  to  Cambridge,  becomes  a  champion  of  labor, 
and  falls  in  love  with  his  employer's  daughter. 
An  entertaining  story  although  rather  drab  in  tone. 

Boyle,  C.  Nina.    What  became  of  Mr.  Des- 
mond.    1922.  F  20660 


The  unexpected  disappearance  of  an  eminently 
respectable  father  of  a  family  and  his  equally  un- 
expected appearance  fifteen  years  later  is  the  baf- 
fling mystery  to  be  solved  in  this  absorbing  book. 

Byrne,  Donn.     The  wind  bloweth.     1922. 

F  20830 

Seven  closely  related  episodes  recounting  the 
adventurous  life  of  Shane  Campbell  in  terms  of  the 
women  who  influenced  him.  They  are  told  in  "a 
style  which  paints  wonderfully  the  atmospheres  of 
far  off  colorful  cities,  and  the  moods  of  men's  souls" 
(New  York  Tribune). 


Christie,     Agatha. 
1922. 


The     secret     adversary. 
F 11952 


A  story  of  political  intrigue  and  mystery  that 
is  unusually  interesting.  An  ex-soldier  and  a  clergy- 
man's daughter  advertise  themselves  as  adventurers 
for  hire.  Their  surprising  experiences  hold  one 
breathless  to  the  end. 

Cobb,     Irvin    S.      J.     Poindexter,     colored. 
1922.  F 21271 

The  amusing  story  told  in  dialect  of  his  experi- 
ences in  extricating  his  new  young  master  from 
the  difiiuclties  into  which  he  is  continually  getting 
while  in  New  York. 

Cohen,  Octavus  Roy.     Assorted  chocolates. 
1922.  F  21307 

Contents:  Noblesse  obliged. — The  evil  lie. — Bird 
of  prey. — The  night-blooming  serious. — Gravey. — 
Oft  in  the  silly  night. — H2O  boy. — Chocolate 
grudge. 

Amusing  stories  of  negro  life  in  Birmingham, 
Alabama.  Some  of  the  same  characters  run 
through  the  book  but  the  stories  are  independent. 

Cournos,  John.     The  wall.     1921.         F  21376 
The  wails  erected  by  social   and  economic  condi- 
tions seem   to  keep  happiness  and  success  from  the 
grasp    of    Gombarov,    a    young    Russian    whose    life 
parallels  the  author's  in  many  respects. 


Cullum,  Ridgwell. 
1922. 


The  man  in  the  twilight. 
F 21411 


Dart,    Edith.      Sareel.      1922. 


A  love  story  of  the  forest  regions  of  northeastern 
Canada,  centering  around  the  rivalry  between  two 
wood-pulp  mills  of  that  region.  "There's  plenty  of 
mystery,  more  action  than  a  six-reel  movie,  and  a 
lot  of  good  entertaining  talk,  flavored  with  native 
humor"  (New  York  Tribune). 

F 12284 

Sareel  is  a  workhouse  girl  whose  love  for  the 
wild  English  moor  is  the  one  compensating  touch 
of  beauty  in  her  miserable  existence  on  a  lonely 
moorland  farm.  "On  the  whole,  a  touching  and 
winning  story,"  says  the  New  Republic. 

Dickens,  Charles.     Barnaby  Rudge.    F  12486 

"A  reprint  of  the  first  ed.,  with  the  illustrations, 
and  an  introduction,  biographical  and  bibliographical, 
by  Charles  Dickens  the  younger." 


-Christmas  books. 


F  12487 

Contents:  A  Christmas  carol. — The  chimes. — 
The  cricket  on  the  hearth. — The  battle  of  life. — 
The  haunted  man  and  the  ghost's  bargain. 

Great  expectations,  and  Hard  times. 

F 12488 

Deeping,   Warwick.     The    house    of   adven- 
ture.    1922.  F 21524 

A  deserter  from  the  army  finds  himself  in  a 
devastated  French  town  where  he  begins  to  recon- 
struct the  shattered  homes  and  slowly  reorganize  the 
life  of  the  village.  He  overcomes  many  difficulties 
and  victory  is  his.

Dos  Passes,  John. 
1917.     1922. 


One  man's   initiation — 
F 21684 


One  man's  reaction  to  the  war  in  a  series  of 
vivid,  powerful  descriptions.  His  impressions  are 
saturated  with  the  gnmness  and  horror  of  it  all, 
but  portrayed  with  power  and  beauty. 

Fairbank,     Janet    A.       The     Cortlandts     of 

Washington   square.     1922.  F 12817 

New   York   before   and    during  the   Civil  War   is 

t^e    background    for    this    good    old-fashioned    story 

of  Anne   Byrne  and  the  three  men  who  loved  her. 

Fitzgerald,  F.  Scott.    Tales  of  the  jazz  age. 
1922.  F 22106 

Contents:  The  jelly-bean. — The  camel's  back. — 
May  day. — Porcelain  and  pink. — The  diamond  as  big 
as  the  Ritz. — The  curious  case  of  Benjamin  But- 
ton.— Tarquin  of  Cheapside. — O  russet  witch! — The 
lees  of  happiness. — Mr.  Icky. — Jemina. 

Short  stories  and  playlets  of  unequal  merit.  The 
New  York  Tribune  calls  the  author  "a  prose  ar- 
tist _of  abundant,  exuberant,  irresponsible,  and  in- 
corrigible talents." 

Fletcher,  J.  S.    The  middle  of  things.    1922. 

F  22132 

An  English  detective<  story  of  the  present  time 
in  which  the  complications  are  ingeniously  devised 
and  furnish  some  tense  dramatic  moments. 

Forman,  Henry  James.    The  man  who  lived 
in   a  shoe.     1922.  F  22220 

A  bachelor,  beloved  by  all,  seems  to  have  a 
mania  for  self-sacrifice.  He  gives  up  his  freedom 
and  comfort  to  care  for  his  dead  sister's  children 
and  just  escapes  giving  up  the  girl  he  loves  to 
his  nephew. 

Frazer,     Elizabeth.       The     secret     partner. 
1922.  F 22244 

A  mystery  story  in  which  the  distinctly  original 
plot  is  handled  consistently  and  cleverly.

Holland,  Bryan  T. 


A  vagrant  tune.     1922. 
F  14045 


A  sincerity,  a  genuiness  of  English  village  at- 
mosphere and  a  delicate  touch  of  humor  here  and 
there... No  book  could  be  more  cheerful  in  tone, 
more  different  from  the  majority  of  modern  novels. 
— Chicago  Daily  News. 

Lincoln,  Joseph  C.     Fair  Harbor.     1922. 

F  24532 

Cap'n  Sears,  a  gruff,  kindly  ex-sailor,  accepts 
the  trying  position  as  manager  of  a  home  for 
"Mariners  Women."  The  humorous  complications 
of  such  a  situation,  the  breezy  salt  water  dialogue, 
and  the  living  quality  of  the  people  make  a  story 
all  Lincoln  readers  will  enjoy. 

Locke,    William    J.      The    tale    of    Triona. 
1922.  F  24574 

Alexis  Triona  becomes  enmeshed  in  a  great  tangle 
of  lies  which  result  in  his  down-fall.  However  he 
works  himself  up  from  the  bottom  and  at  last  finds 
his  true  level. 

Mansfield,     Katherine.       Bliss,     and     other 
stories.     1921.  F  24954 


Contents:  Je  ne  parle  pas  franc.ais. — Bliss. — 
The  wind  blows. — Psychology. — Pictures. — The  man 
without  a  temperment. — Mr.  Reginald  Peacock's  day. 
— Sun  and  Moon. — Feuille  d'album. — A  dill  pickle. 
— The  little  governess. — Revelations. — The  escape. 

Short,  clever,  rather  cynical  stories,  hardly  more 
than  sketches.  They  cover  but  a  short  period  in 
the  lives  of  the  characters,  sometimes  only  one  or 
two  hours. 

Maugham,  W.  Somerset.    The  making  of  a 
saint;  a  romance  of  mediaeval  Italy.    1922. 

F  25010 

The  story  of  a  wandering  fighter  who  has  been 
exiled  from  his  native  province.  There  are  feuds, 
adventure,  and  enough  gore  to  satisfy  the  most 
bloodthirsty. 


Oppenheim,  E.  Phillips. 
1922. 


The  evil  shepherd. 
F  25764 


A  melodramatic  mystery  story  with  a  London 
background.  The  hero  is  a  criminal  lawyer  and 
the  supposed  villain  proves  to  be  otherwise. 

Porter,  Rebecca  N.    The  Rest  Hollow  mys- 
tery.    1922.  F  16508 

A  well  _  told  exciting  mystery  story  based  on  a 
plot  to  withhold  an  estate  from  a  young  man  who 
has  lost  his  memory  through  shell-shock.  He  re- 
gains it  and  becomes  involved  in  a  series  of  adven- 
tures leading  to  a  solution  of  the  mystery. 

Putnam,    Nina    Wilcox.      Laughter    limited. 
1922.  F 16704 

Bonnie  Delane,  motion  picture  star,  tells  the 
story  of  her  career  in  a  humorous,  slangy  style. 

Seymour,  Beatrice  Kean.     Intrusion.     1922. 

F 17930 

An  English  after-the-war  novel  in  which  Allan 
Suffield  marries  a  beautiful  girl  without  brains  or 
morals.  The  interest  is  centered  more  in  the  char- 
acter analysis  than  in  plot. 

Snaith,  J.   C.     The  Van  Roon.     1922. 

F 18272 

A  mystery  tale  which  centers  about  a  valuable 
painting  by  the  artist  Van  Roon.  It  has  been 
missing  for  years,  is  discovered,  and  after  much 
adventure  finally  restored. 

Terhune,    Albert    Payson.      Black    Caesar's 
clan;   a   Florida  mystery  story.      1922. 

F  27747 

The  author  of  the  dog  stories  has  written  a 
thrilling  tale  of  old  pirate  land  invaded  by  mod- 
ern treasure  hunters,  in  which  a  Collie  and  a 
Persian  cat  play  important  parts. 

Tormay,   Cecile.     The   old  house;   tr.   from 
the    Hungarian.      1922.  F  27834 

A  sombre,  tragic  tale  in  which  the  past  glory 
and  present  decay  of  the  old  house  on  the  Danube 
symbolize  the  life  of  the  family  which  it  sheltered 
for  so  many  years,  and  from  which  its  associations 
were  inseparaole. 

Towne,  Charles  Hanson.     The  chain.     1922. 

F  27841 

Literary  New  York  in  the  late  '90s  is  charmingly 
depicted  in  this  story  which  is  a  first  novel  by 
a  well  known  editor  and  poet.  The  character 
sketching  recalls  Tarkington's  method. 

West,  Rebecca.     The  judge.     1922.     F 19198 

As  exquisitely  written  a  piece  of  prose  as  one 
can  imagine.  The  settings  are  Scotch  and  English, 
and  the  story  sets  forth  the  conflict  between  filial 
and  marital  love.  The  characterizations  are  excel- 
lent, especially  of  the  women.

Abbott,    Jane.      Red-Robin.      1922. 

A  mystery  story  for  boys  and  girls.  Red  Robin 
is  adopted  by  a  wealthy  mill  owner  and  she  devotes 
her  time  and  effort  to  welfare  work  among  the 
factory  people. 

Abramsz,  S.     De  gelaarsde  kat;  een  sprook- 
ja   met   silhouetten   van  Jan   Wiegman. 

Adapted  from  Perrault's:  Le  maistre  chat;  ou,  Le 
chat  botte. 

The  Dutch  version  of  the  Puss-in-boots  fairy  tale. 

Bannerman,  Helen.     Histoire  du  petit  negre 
Sambo.     1921. 

Little  Black  Sambo  translated  into  French,  with 
the  same  adventures  in  the  jungle  of  tigers. 


Barbour,  Ralph  Henry. 
1922. 


The  Turner  twins. 


Ned  and  Laurie,  were  so  much  alike  that  no  one 
could  tell  one  from  the  other.  That  remarkable 
likeness  proved  to  be  the  basis  of  the  excitement 
and  mystery  in  this  story  of  their  school  life. 


Blanchard,  Lucy  M.  Chico;  the  story  of  a 
homing  pigeon.  1922. 

A  beautiful  story  of  modern  Venice.  The  train- 
ing of  the  carrier  pigeon  and  its  service  afterwards 
in  the  Great  War  will  fascinate  young  readers. 

Bosschere,  Jean   de.     Weird  islands.     1922. 

An  interesting  animal  story  supplemented  by  some 
lovely  illustrations. 

Brown,  Edna  A.    The  Chinese  kitten.     1922. 
The    adventures    of    Lucy    and    Dora    Merrill    in 
play,  school   and   travel.     For  girls  about  ten. 

Burgess,  Gellett.  Goops  and  how  to  be 
them;  a  manual  of  manners  for  polite 
infants,  inculcating  many  juvenile  virtues 
both  by  precept  and  example.  1900. 

De  Huff,  Elizabeth  Willis.  Taytay's  tales, 
collected  and  retold.  1922. 

American  Indian  tales  told  in  the  words  of  the 
Indian  grandfather  and  illustrated  with  unusual 
drawings  by  an  Indian  artist.  The  stories  bear 
striking  similarities  to  the  folk  lore  of  other  coun- 
tries, containing  such  beloved  tales  as  Red  Riding 
Hood  and  the  Tar  Baby  in  Indian  settings. 

Dunlop,  Agnes,  and  Jones,  Robinson  G. 
Playtime  stories.  1921. 

Evarts,  Hal  G.     Fur  sign.     1922. 

Two  boys  from  _the  slums  of  the  city  undertake 
a  trapping  expedition  which  proves  thoroughly  suc- 
cessful. The  book  is  primarily  instructive  and  the 
explanation  of  the  trapping  of  various  animals 
will  be  of  interest  to  boys. 

Gregor,  Elmer  Russell.  Three  Sioux 
scouts.  1922. 

The  adventurous  life  of  the  three  Sioux  scouts 
including  White  Otter,  the  hero  of  one  of  the 
author's  earlier  books.  The  defeat  of  the  Pawnees 
was  accomplished  under  the  leadership  of  these 
warriors. 

Griffis,  William  Elliot.  Korean  fairy  tales. 
1922. 

Myths   and   folk-lore   of   Korea. 

Heyliger,  William.     Dan's  to-morrow.     1922. 

The  seventeen-year-old  owner  of  a  factory  and 
the  son  of  the  strike  leader  in  the  factory,  spend 
a  summer  together  in  camp.  Their  various  experi- 
ences teach  them  the  necessity  of  union  in  action, 
a  lesson  which  proves  profitable  to  both  boys. 

Hyer,  Frank  S.  The  Laurel  readers.  1917. 
3v. 

v.  1.     The   Laurel   primer. 

2.  The  Laurel  first  reader. 

3.  The  Laurel  second  reader. 

Kempton,  Kenneth  Payson.  Phantom  gold. 
1922. 

An  English  cabin  boy  is  stranded  on  this  side 
of  the  Atlantic,  and  after  many  escapades,  he  ships 
on  a  boat  whose  captain  is  really  a  pirate.  A  very 
probable  story.  For  boys  from  twelve  to  fourteen. 

Lamb,  Charles  and  Mary.  Tales  from 
Shakespeare,  with  numerous  illustrations, 
by  Louis  Rhead.  1918. 

Latham,  Harold  S.  Jimmy  Quigg,  office  boy. 
1920. 

Jimmy   converts  a   band    of   strikers   into   a    group 

of    true    citizens.  The    theme    of    Americanization 

inspires   the    book  and    is    well    worked    out    by    the 
author. 


196 


THE  CHICAGO  PUBLIC  LIBRARY 


Moore,  Clement  C.  A  visit  from  St.  Nicho- 
las. 1921. 

The  text  of  the  poem  is  that  of  the  original  edi- 
tion (1837).  There  are  three  woodcuts  by  Florence 
Wyman  Ivins. 

Orton,  Helen  Fuller.  Bobby  of  Cloverfield 
Farm.  1922. 

Bobby's  adventures  on  the  farm,  and  how  Rover 
rescued  Bobby  from  drowning.  Useful  for  supple- 
mentary reading  for  primary  grades. 

Potter,  Beatrix.     Histoire  de  Pierre-Lapin. 
1921. 
The   Tale  of  Peter   Rabbit,   told  in   French. 

Pyle,  Katharine.  Fairy  tales  from  far  and 
near.  1922. 

A  collection  of  fascinating  fairy  tales  from  many 
lands  with  illustrations  by  the  author. 

Sanford,  Chester  M.,  and  Owen,  Grace  A. 
Modern  Europeans;  a  biographical  school 
reader  for  the  upper  grades.  1919. 

Contents:  King  Albert  of  Belgium. — Lord  Kitch- 
ener.— Lloyd  George. — Lord  Northcliffe. — Marshal 
Haig.  —  Admiral  Beatty.  —  Winston  Churchill.  — 
George  V. — Edith  Cavell. — Cardinal  Mercier. — 
Rudyard  Kipling. — Marshall  Joffre. — Raymond 
Poincare. — Sarah  Bernhardt. — Georges  Guynemer. — 
Clemenceau. — Marshal  _  Foch. — d'Annunzio. — Victor 
Emanuel  III. — Marconi. — General  Diaz. — Paderew- 
ski. — Madame  Curie. — Venizelos. 

St.  Nicholas.  Outdoor  stories,  retold  from 
St.  Nicholas.  1922. 

Contents:  The  silver  cup,  by  G.  M.  Johnson. — 
How  Queela  hunted  hawk's-bill,  by  _  P.  Lupia. — The 
peacemaker,  by  F.  L.  Pollock. — Adrift  on  the  Ama- 
zon, by  D.  A.  Cobb. — The  fish  that  took  the  medal, 
by  W.  H.  Woods. — Peggy's  'possum  hunt,  by  Mrs. 
B.  Harrison. — Along  the  trout-stream,  by  R.  S.  Lem- 
mon. — The  black  hero  of  the  ranges,  by  E.  J.  Mills. 
— Rob  Dunstan's  first  blackfish  drive,  by  E.  Mor- 
gan.— Racing  waters,  by  L.  de  St.  H.  Guyol. — A 
young  hunter  of  today,  _  by  R.  F.  _Gilder. — The 
moose  that  was  not  afraid,  by  F.  Stick. — A  mes- 
sage by  fire,  by  C.  B.  Snell. — Hunter's  luck,  by 
F.  Stick. 

Schultz,  James  Willard.  The  trail  of  the 
Spanish  horse.  1922. 

A  thrilling  tale  of  life  and  adventure  among  the 
Indians,  in  which  the  Spanish  horse,  the  fleetest 
buffalo-runner  of  the  plains,  is  stolen,  and  many 
things  happen  before  he  is  recovered  by  Otahtoyi, 
and  his  almost  brother,  Pitamakan. 

Tolman,  Albert  W.  Jim  Spurling,  mill- 
man.  1921. 

A  sequel  to  Jim  Spurling,  fisherman  (H  6790) 
telling  the  story  of  four  college  boys  who  try 
running  a  saw  mill  in  the  Maine  woods  to  earn 
money  during  their  summer  vacation.  A  scheming 
promoter  creates  much  trouble  and  excitement. 

Tomlinson,  Everett  T.  Scouting  with  Mad 
Anthony;  a  story  of  the  Indian  wars. 
1922. 

(American  scouting  series.) 

The  story  of  "Mad  Anthony"  Wayne  and  his 
hardy  soldiers  in  trying  times  of  Indian  insurrec- 
tions. There  is  a  basis  of  historic  fact  for  every 
incident  in  the  story. 

With,  K.  H.  The  mouse  story,  told  by  an 
old  schoolmaster.  1922. 

The  translation  of  a  story  of  a  mouse  family 
which  has  delighted  many  generations  of  Danish 
children.  The  incidents  are  so  dramatic  and  the 
story  _  is  told  in  such  a  humorous,  original  manner 
that  it  will  be  enjoyed  almost  equally  by  old  and 
young.  It  deserves  to  be  called  a  children's  classic. 


Circulating  copies  may  be  issued  on  the  regular 
library  card  in  the  Civics  and  Documents  Depart- 
ment, fourth  floor,  Randolph  Street  entrance. 

Education  and  Child  Welfare 
Butterworth,     Julian     E.       Improving     the 
school-building  facilities  of  one-  and  two- 
teacher     districts     through     measurement. 
1922.      • 

(New  York  state.  College  of  Agriculture  Ex- 
tension bulletin  52.) 

Presents  an  analysis  showing  the  more  important 
features  of  a  good  rural  school  plant,  with  a  set 
of  standards  as  basis. 

Egbert,  James  C.  University  summer 
schools.  1922.  116.3  1922-31 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Education.  Bulletin  1922  no. 
31.) 

A  brief  report  giving  comparative  statistics  of 
enrollment  for  the  years  1916,  1917,  1921. 

Gebhart,  John  C.  Malnutrition  and  school 
feeding.  1922.  116.3 1921-37 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Education.  Bulletin  1921  no. 
37.) 

A  review  of  malnutrition  conditions  in  the  vari- 
ous cities  and  the  remedial  work  through  school 
lunches.  Gives  some  of  the  menus  with  caloric 
values  and  costs.  Also  covers  work  of  Great  Britain 
and  France. 

Great  Britain.  Navy.  Recreational  training. 
1922. 

(Handbook  of  physical  and  recreational  training 
for  the  use  of  the  royal  navy,  1920.  v.2.) 

Describes  recreational  games,  giving  list,  boxing, 
fencing,  field  games  and  sports,  aquatic  sports,  and 
gymnastics.  Illustrated.  Valuable  for  playgrounds. 


Jeter,     Helen     Rankin. 
Court.     1922. 


Chicago     Juvenile 
L5.20  104 


(U.  S.  Children's  Bureau.  Bureau  publication 
104.) 

A  full  history  and  description  of  the  work  of 
the  court. 

Muerman,  J.  C.  The  district  owned  or  con- 
trolled teacher's  home.  1922. 

116.3  1922/16 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Education.  Bulletin  1922,  no. 
16.) 

A  novel  method  of  housing  the  teacher  which 
solves  a  serious  community  problem  in  the  country 
schools,  with  plans  of  dwellings. 

Reaney,  Bernice  C.  Milk  and  our  school 
children.  1922.  116.2911 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Education.  Health  education 
11.) 

Estimates  percentage  of  school  children  who  drink 
milk,  gives  its  nutritive  value,  and  contains  lessons 
on  food  values.  A  very  interesting  and  instructive 
pamphlet. 

Zook,   George   F.     National   Conference   of 
junior   colleges,    1920.      1922.    116.3  1922-19 
(U.   S.   Bureau  of  Education.     Bulletin   1922,  no. 
19.) 

Proceedings  of  the  conference  held  in  St.  Louis, 
June  1920,  and  extracts  from  proceedings  of  the 
first  annual  meeting  of  the  American  Association  of 
Junior  Colleges,  Chicago,  1921. 

Residence  of  students  in  universities  and 

colleges.     1922.  116.3  1922-18 

(U.  S.  Bureau  of  Education.  Bulletin  1922,  no. 
18.) 

An  interesting  survey  of  the  Attendance  of  stu- 
dents at  their  own  state  institutions  with  compara- 
tive statistics  of  the  survey  made  in  1896-97.

Day,  Independence  Day,  Labor  Day,  Thanksgiving  Day,  Christmas  Day. 
All  departments   are  closed  en  Independence  Day  and  Christmas  Day.

Independent  collections  of  3,000  to  20,000  volumes  in  quarters  owned  or  rented  by  the 
Library,  or  occupied  by  arrangement  with  the  Park  Boards,  and  administered  by  a  librarian 
and  staff. 

All  the  following  Branches,  except  one,  are  open  from  9  A.  M.  to  9  p.  M.  on  week  days, 
and  closed  on  Sundays.

Small    collections    placed    in    outlying    and  remote   districts,    administered    by    an  attend- 
ant  not   regularly   on   the   Library   Staff   but  paid  according  to  volume  of  circulation.
